summary: An oasis city watered by the Barada river and among the oldest continuously inhabited places on earth — ruled in turn by Aramaeans, Assyrians, Persians, Greeks, and Romans, made the capital of the Umayyad Caliphate that ruled from Spain to India, and today the contested capital of Syria.

title: Dante Alighieri
summary: The Florentine poet (1265–1321) who, banished from his city for life under threat of being burned alive if he returned, wrote one of the greatest poems of the European Middle Ages in a highly crafted Tuscan vernacular rather than the Latin of the learned. In choosing the vernacular for the highest subject matter, and in making that poem the cornerstone of a literary tradition, he did more than any other early writer to make Tuscan the model for literary Italian, and he is held by tradition to be the father of the Italian language and one of the supreme poets of the Western canon.

title: Daoism
summary: China's great philosophy of letting go — the tradition of the Dao, the Way, which set the spontaneity of nature against the rules of society, taught that the wisest action is often no action, and grew from a handful of cryptic texts into an organized religion of gods, alchemists, and seekers of immortality.

title: Darius the Great
summary: The Achaemenid king who seized the Persian throne in 522 BCE amid a violent succession crisis and then reorganized the largest empire the world had yet seen -- dividing it into provinces with fixed tribute, standardizing coinage, building the Royal Road and the ceremonial capital at Persepolis -- while leaving a cliff-carved inscription that is both his official self-defense and a key that later unlocked cuneiform.
aliases: Darius as the Despot Defeated at Marathon
Darius as Restorer of Order under Ahura Mazda
Darius as Possible Usurper behind the Official Story

title: David Hume
summary: The Scottish philosopher who pushed Enlightenment empiricism to its radical conclusions — showing that reason alone cannot prove causation, establish the self, or justify belief in miracles — and who produced the most systematic challenge to religious belief and rationalist philosophy that the Western tradition has faced.

title: Debt
summary: The social relationship in which one party owes something to another — money, labor, goods, or obligation — and the institution through which that relationship has organized power, enforced hierarchy, funded states, and structured the experience of most people who have ever lived.
indexTerms: Reinhart, Carmen M.; Rogoff, Kenneth S.

title: Decolonization
summary: The process by which European colonial empires dissolved between 1945 and 1975 — as Asian and African peoples won independence through armed struggle, negotiation, and political pressure — transforming the map of the world, creating dozens of new states, and generating the political and economic structures of the contemporary Global South.
indexTerms: Acemoglu, Daron; Johnson, Simon; Robinson, James A.
Jan C. Jansen and Jurgen Osterhammel

title: Democracy
summary: The concept that political authority derives from the governed — and the site of the most active contemporary political contestation

title: Deng Xiaoping
summary: The pragmatic survivor who became China's paramount leader after Mao Zedong's death — twice purged and twice restored — and who, without serving as party chairman, general secretary, or state president, reversed much of Maoist economic policy, launched the 'reform and opening' that built a market economy under one-party rule, lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty in a single generation, and, as paramount leader, backed the decision to send the army against the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests, a crackdown that remains heavily censored inside China.
aliases: The Man Who Sent the Tanks
indexTerms: Nathan, Andrew J., and Perry Link (eds.)

title: Desmond Tutu
summary: The South African Anglican archbishop who became the moral voice of the struggle against apartheid and then the conscience of the democracy that followed — a Nobel Peace laureate who named apartheid a sin and a heresy, gave the world the image of the 'Rainbow Nation,' chaired the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's contested attempt to trade truth for amnesty, and never stopped speaking uncomfortable truths to power, Black and white alike, to the end of his life.
associatedWorks: God Has a Dream: A Vision of Hope for Our Time

title: Diaspora
summary: The dispersion of a people from their original homeland, maintaining collective identity across generations in a foreign land — a concept originating with the Jewish exile in Babylon, extended to the African diaspora produced by the slave trade, and now applied to dozens of dispersed communities worldwide, raising fundamental questions about the relationship between identity, territory, and belonging.
indexTerms: Mintz, Sidney W., and Richard Price

title: Diego Rivera and Mexican Muralism
summary: The movement, with Diego Rivera as its most internationally famous figure, in which the Mexican state turned the walls of its public buildings over to painters who covered them with a vast, fierce epic of Mexican history -- from the Aztec past through the Spanish conquest to the promise of revolution -- making art a weapon of the people and giving a wounded nation a new image of itself.
aliases: Art as a Weapon of the People
indexTerms: Alejandro Anreus, Leonard Folgarait, and Robin Adele Greeley (eds.)

title: Djenné
summary: A trading city on the inland delta of the Niger River in Mali, twin to nearby Djenné-Djenno, one of the oldest known cities in sub-Saharan Africa. Djenné grew rich linking the goldfields of the south to the salt and desert routes of the north, became a respected center of Islamic learning, and is best known today for its Great Mosque, the largest mud-brick building in the world, replastered each year by the whole community.
aliases: The Southern Anchor of the Desert Trade
A City That Grew on Its Own
The Building That Must Be Remade

title: Dmitri Mendeleev
summary: The Russian chemist who, in 1869, arranged the sixty-odd known elements by atomic weight into a single table whose recurring pattern he called the periodic law — and who set himself apart from others circling the same idea by leaving gaps for undiscovered elements and predicting their properties in detail. When gallium, scandium, and germanium were found within fifteen years, matching his forecasts with startling precision, the periodic table became the organizing map of chemistry. He worked without knowing why the pattern held; the deeper basis, atomic number, came only later through Moseley. Element 101, mendelevium, bears his name, though he never won the Nobel Prize.
aliases: The Author of the Periodic Law
associatedWorks: On the Relationship of the Properties of the Elements to their Atomic Weights
The Natural System of the Elements

title: DNA
summary: The molecule that carries genetic information in living organisms — deoxyribonucleic acid, whose double-helix structure was determined in 1953 by Watson, Crick, Franklin, and Wilkins — and the conceptual foundation that unified biology, explained heredity, enabled genetic medicine, and transformed humanity's relationship to its own biology.
indexTerms: Doudna, Jennifer A. and Sternberg, Samuel H.
Supreme Court of the United States

title: Don Quixote
summary: Miguel de Cervantes’s two-part novel (1605, 1615) about a Spanish gentleman who reads so many chivalric romances that he loses his mind and sets out to become a knight-errant — widely regarded as the first modern novel, a founding text of European literature, and an inexhaustible meditation on the relationship between imagination and reality.
aliases: The Ingenious Gentleman Don Quixote of La Mancha
El ingenioso hidalgo don Quijote de la Mancha
The Foundation of the Spanish Genius

title: Dream of the Red Chamber
summary: The mid-18th-century Chinese novel by Cao Xueqin, also called The Story of the Stone, widely regarded as the greatest work of Chinese fiction and one of the Four Great Classical Novels of China. It follows the slow ruin of the aristocratic Jia family through the doomed love of the sensitive boy Jia Baoyu and his fragile cousin Lin Daiyu. Cao left it unfinished at his death around 1763; the common 120-chapter version, completed in the Cheng-Gao printed editions associated with Cheng Weiyuan and Gao E, was printed in 1791–92, and both that ending and its authorship are debated.
aliases: The Summit of the Vernacular Novel
The Anatomy of a Dying Aristocracy
indexTerms: Cao Xueqin and Gao E (trans. David Hawkes and John Minford)
Cao Xueqin (trans. Yang Hsien-yi and Gladys Yang)

title: Drought
summary: The prolonged absence of sufficient water — through deficient rainfall, depleted groundwater, or both — that has collapsed civilizations, driven mass migration, and shaped the political geography of every inhabited continent, and whose intensification under climate change is making it one of the defining crises of the twenty-first century.
indexTerms: Weiss, Harvey and Bradley, Raymond S.

title: Drug Policy
summary: How society should respond to drug use and addiction — through prohibition and criminal enforcement, public health treatment, harm reduction, or legalization — with genuine evidence about what works and genuine value disagreements about liberty, order, and the nature of addiction
indexTerms: Office of National Drug Control Policy
MacCoun, Robert J. and Reuter, Peter

title: Du Fu
summary: Du Fu (712–770) is a poet of the Chinese Tang dynasty, revered for centuries as the shi sheng, the 'poet-sage': the moral realist whose verse turned the ruin of the An Lushan Rebellion into poetic witness, detail that complements but does not replace the historical record of a society coming apart, and who, respected in limited circles but not yet accorded his later canonical stature, was in time crowned perhaps the greatest poet in the Chinese tradition.
associatedWorks: The Ballad of the Army Carts
My Thatched Cottage Unroofed by the Autumn Wind

title: Due Process
summary: The principle that the state may not take a person's life, liberty, or property without fair procedure — notice of the charge, a hearing before a neutral judge, the chance to answer, and punishment only by settled law rather than by decree. It is the quiet machinery that stands between every individual and the raw power of the state.

title: Dyes
summary: For most of human history, color was a luxury — Tyrian purple reserved by law for emperors, cochineal red from insects that made Spain rich, indigo blue grown on brutal plantations. The quest to synthesize natural dyes in the laboratory accidentally produced the first synthetic dye in 1856 and launched the modern chemical industry, turning what had once been a royal prerogative into something anyone could buy for pennies.

title: Earthquakes
summary: Earthquakes don't kill people — buildings do. The same magnitude quake that a wealthy, well-engineered city shrugs off can take tens of thousands of lives in a poor one of unreinforced masonry. The shaking is released when strained rock slips along a fault; the death toll is set by construction, wealth, and governance. Haiti in 2010 was not hit harder than Japan or Chile have survived; what failed was the built environment. We can map dangerous faults; we still cannot predict when one will break. The 1755 destruction of Lisbon helped birth seismology, while that fundamental limit remains.
aliases: Killed by the building, not the quake
The disaster that was designed away
indexTerms: Oliver-Smith, Anthony and Hoffman, Susanna M. (eds.)
Anthony Oliver-Smith and Susanna M. Hoffman (eds.)

title: El Niño
summary: A recurring Pacific climate cycle — the warm phase of El Niño–Southern Oscillation — in which warm surface water shifts east every few years, suppressing the cold upwelling off Peru and reshaping weather worldwide: drought in Australia and South Asia, floods in South America, weakened monsoons and famine in Africa and India. Spanish sailors named it for the Christ Child. Mike Davis’s Late Victorian Holocausts argues that the great colonial-era famines came from these cycles colliding with imperial economies that left subject populations no buffer.
aliases: The warm current that took the fish
When the empire closed the granaries
The forecast that arrived in time
indexTerms: Cane, Mark A. and Zebiak, Stephen E.

title: Electricity
summary: The harnessing of electromagnetic energy that reorganized how civilization produces and distributes power, enabling industrial production, communication, computing, and modern urban life.

title: Ellis Island and the Great Wave
summary: Ellis Island and the Great Wave is the name given to the era of mass immigration to the United States, running roughly from the 1880s to 1924, when more than twenty million people arrived. Ellis Island, the federal inspection station that opened in New York Harbor in 1892, processed some twelve million of them before it closed in 1954. Most came from southern and eastern Europe and remade the country's cities, religions, and self-image, until national-origins quotas passed in 1924 shut the door.
aliases: The era as a hard-won passage to a new life
The era from the gate built to keep people out
The era as a crisis of national character to be managed

title: Emergency Powers
summary: The authority a government claims to suspend the ordinary rules in a crisis — to rule by decree, detain without normal trial, and override the limits that bind it in calm times. It is indispensable when the normal pace of law is too slow to save the state, and it is the oldest road by which free states have walked into tyranny.
aliases: The Power a Free State Cannot Do Without
indexTerms: Oren Gross and Fionnuala Ní Aoláin

title: Emiliano Zapata
summary: The horseman from the sugar country of Morelos who led a peasant army in the Mexican Revolution for one stubborn core demand — the return of village lands swallowed by the great estates — and a wider agrarian program built around it — issued the Plan of Ayala when its leaders moved too slowly, refused every compromise the revolution offered him, was lured to his death by a fake defector at Chinameca in 1919, and became in death a far larger symbol than his agrarian aims, the incorruptible conscience of the landless whose name and cry were revived by indigenous rebels in 1994.
aliases: The Incorruptible Conscience of the Landless
The Local Man Behind the Myth

title: Emily Dickinson
summary: One of the founders of modern American poetry, who wrote almost entirely in private. Dickinson spent her adult life in one Amherst house, withdrew from society in her thirties, and left nearly eighteen hundred poems kept in a drawer and shown to almost no one. They use the plain meter of Protestant hymns, then fracture it with dashes, irregular capitals, and slant rhyme, compressing death, the mind, and doubt into a few charged lines. Her first editors smoothed this out; Thomas Johnson’s 1955 edition restored it, revealing how strange the work was. She is ranked with Walt Whitman as a founder of American poetry.
aliases: The poet of the few perfect lines
associatedWorks: Because I could not stop for Death
I heard a Fly buzz — when I died
Hope is the thing with feathers
Tell all the truth but tell it slant
My Life had stood — a Loaded Gun
There's a certain Slant of light
The Soul selects her own Society
After great pain, a formal feeling comes
I felt a Funeral, in my Brain

title: Empire
summary: The political form in which one state rules over multiple peoples who did not choose that rule — the dominant political structure of most of recorded history, the framework through which most of the world's wealth, knowledge, and people were connected, and the system whose legacies continue to shape international politics, economic inequality, and cultural identity
aliases: The Empire That Built Its Own Mythology
indexTerms: Daron Acemoglu, Simon Johnson, and James A. Robinson

title: Empress Dowager Cixi
summary: A low-ranking concubine of the Xianfeng Emperor who, after his death in 1861, seized the regency and became the dominant force in Qing politics for nearly half a century, ruling China from behind a silk screen as regent for two child emperors. She crushed the 1898 Hundred Days' Reform, gambled on the Boxer Rebellion and lost catastrophically, then sponsored sweeping late-Qing reforms before her death in 1908 — and is remembered, depending on the teller, as the reactionary who doomed China or as a pragmatist who held a collapsing empire together.
aliases: The Pragmatist Dealt an Impossible Hand
A Ruler Inside an Impossible System

title: Enclosure
summary: The long process by which England's open fields and common lands were fenced off into private, exclusive property — extinguishing the use-rights that village communities had held for centuries. It unfolded in two great waves: a Tudor-era surge driven by sheep and wool, and the Parliamentary enclosure of the 18th and 19th centuries, carried out through more than five thousand private Acts. Critics call it the original theft that created a landless wage-labor force; defenders call it the foundation of modern, productive agriculture. Either way, enclosure is how shared land became private capital.

title: Epidemiology
summary: The science of how disease is distributed across populations and what causes that distribution — who gets sick, where, when, and why. Working from patterns rather than from individual patients, epidemiology has found the causes of disease again and again before the underlying biology was understood, from cholera in a contaminated water pump to lung cancer in cigarette smoke. Its power and its peril rest on the same fact: it reads cause from correlation, and correlation is not always cause.
aliases: Epidemiology as the Discipline of Confounding
Epidemiology and the Causes Its Methods Hide
indexTerms: Rothman, Kenneth J.; Greenland, Sander; Lash, Timothy L.
Hernán, Miguel A.; Robins, James M.
Kenneth J. Rothman, Sander Greenland, and Timothy L. Lash

title: Equality
summary: The principle that people should, in some respect, be treated or regarded as equals — a single word that conceals several rival ideas (equal worth, equality before the law, equality of opportunity, equality of outcome) that pull against one another, so that most political conflict is really a fight over which equality counts.

title: Erlitou Culture
summary: The Erlitou culture is a Bronze Age archaeological culture of the middle Yellow River valley in Henan, China, dated to roughly 1900–1500 BCE and centered on a large planned city near modern Luoyang. It was one of the earliest state-level societies in the Central Plains, though earlier candidates such as Liangzhu and Taosi complicate any claim that it was the first. Its monumental palace foundations, enclosed bronze workshops, and early Chinese ritual bronze vessels have been interpreted as evidence of centralized coordination. Whether it corresponds to the Xia, the semi-legendary first dynasty of Chinese tradition, is a genuine and unresolved scholarly question.

title: Ethiopian Empire
summary: The Ethiopian Empire was the Christian highland state that endured in the Horn of Africa from the Solomonic restoration of 1270 until the fall of Haile Selassie in 1974 — one of the world's oldest continuous Christian polities, legitimized by a tradition that its kings descended from Solomon and the Queen of Sheba, and the one African kingdom that defeated a European colonial invader at the Battle of Adwa in 1896 and kept its sovereignty through the Scramble for Africa, despite a later Italian occupation from 1936 to 1941.
aliases: The empire as a sacred and unbroken nation
The empire as a beacon of Black freedom
The empire as a conquering power

title: Ethiopian Orthodox Christianity
summary: Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Christianity is one of the world's oldest continuous Christian traditions, established when the Aksumite king Ezana adopted the faith in the 4th century CE. It developed in relative isolation into a distinctive church with its own Ge'ez liturgy, an unusually broad biblical canon, strongly Old Testament–flavored practices, and an identity so fused with Ethiopian nationhood that it survived for centuries as a Christian stronghold amid the spread of Islam.

title: Euclid
summary: The Greek mathematician who, working in Alexandria around 300 BCE, compiled and organized the geometry of his predecessors into the 'Elements' — thirteen books that derived hundreds of theorems from a handful of definitions and axioms, founding the model of deductive proof and serving as the most successful textbook in history for roughly two thousand years, even though almost nothing reliable is known about the man himself.
aliases: A Text to Extend, Not Just Preserve
indexTerms: W. S. Anglin and J. Lambek
Euclid (tr. Thomas L. Heath, ed. Dana Densmore)

title: Euclid's Elements
summary: The geometry textbook written in Alexandria around 300 BCE that gave the world its most influential ancient model of the axiomatic method — truth built up by proof from a handful of stated assumptions — and remained the standard model of rigorous reasoning, and of geometry teaching, for more than two thousand years.
indexTerms: Euclid, trans. & ed. Thomas L. Heath

title: Eva Perón
summary: The illegitimate girl from the Argentine pampas who became a radio and film actress, married Colonel Juan Perón, and as first lady made herself the passionate voice of the country's poor — championing the descamisados, helping win women the vote in 1947, and running the vast Eva Perón Foundation — adored by millions as a living saint even as critics saw the glamorous mask of an authoritarian regime, dead of cancer at thirty-three and remade ever since into a myth Argentina has never stopped fighting over.
aliases: The Myth That Outlived the Woman
associatedWorks: La razón de mi vida (My Mission in Life)

title: Evolution
summary: The process by which populations of organisms change over successive generations through the differential survival and reproduction of heritable traits — the foundational explanatory framework of modern biology, and one of the most contested scientific ideas in the history of human culture.
indexTerms: Nowak, Martin A.; Tarnita, Corina E.; Wilson, Edward O.
Gould, Stephen Jay; Lewontin, Richard C.
United States District Court, M.D. Pennsylvania (Judge John E. Jones III)

title: Existentialism
summary: Existentialism is a twentieth-century philosophy holding that human beings have no fixed essence or built-in purpose. A person exists first and must then define who they are through free choice, bearing full responsibility for those choices in a universe that offers no external guarantee of meaning. It is most closely tied to Jean-Paul Sartre and the postwar French thinkers around him, though its roots reach back to Kierkegaard and Nietzsche.

title: Famine
summary: A widespread scarcity of food severe enough to cause mass death — though, in the modern age, rarely a simple absence of food. The worst famines have struck where food existed or was even being exported, because what collapses is people’s access to it: their wages, rights, and purchasing power. Drought and blight cause shortages; wealth, rights, and politics decide whether a shortage becomes a famine.
aliases: Famine as a failure of access, not supply

title: Fascism
summary: A political ideology that emerged in Europe after World War I, characterized by ultranationalism, authoritarian rule, the cult of the leader, rejection of liberal democracy and Marxism, and the glorification of violence and national rebirth — and by the regimes, primarily in Italy and Germany, that governed under its name.

title: Faust
summary: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's two-part dramatic poem (Part One 1808, Part Two 1832), worked on across some sixty years — often called the supreme work of German literature, in which a restless scholar who has exhausted human knowledge makes a pact with the devil Mephistopheles, wagering his soul for boundless experience. Goethe reworked an older legend in which Faust is damned; his Faust, controversially, is saved. The poem became the central modern myth of striving and its price, and gave the language the phrase 'Faustian bargain.'
aliases: The Cornerstone of the National Genius
The Bargain That Should Have Ended in Hell
Faust as the Engineer of the Modern World
indexTerms: Goethe, Johann Wolfgang von (trans. Stuart Atkins)
Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, trans. David Luke
Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, trans. Stuart Atkins

title: Federalism
summary: Federalism is the art of being one country and many at once. It splits a nation's sovereignty between a central government and its regions, each holding constitutionally protected authority of its own, some powers exclusive and many shared, so that a large and varied people can share a single state without dissolving into one ruler's grip or a quarrelling heap of statelets.
aliases: Federalism as the obstacle and the weapon
indexTerms: Hamilton, Alexander, James Madison, and John Jay
Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay

title: Fela Kuti
summary: The Nigerian musician who was Afrobeat's principal architect and turned it into a weapon — fusing Yoruba rhythm, highlife, jazz, and funk into long, hypnotic, brass-driven songs in pidgin English, aimed squarely at the corruption and brutality of Nigeria's military rulers. Beaten, jailed, and burned out of his own commune, he became one of the most important African popular musicians of the twentieth century and a Pan-African icon, while embodying in his own life the contradictions of liberation.

title: Feminism
summary: The political and intellectual movement demanding equal rights, opportunities, and social power for women — organized in three historical 'waves' from the suffrage campaigns of the 19th century through second-wave liberal and radical feminism to contemporary intersectional feminism — and a philosophical tradition that has transformed every discipline it has touched by asking whose experience was left out of the dominant account.
indexTerms: Federici, Silvia and Mariarosa Dalla Costa
Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, 597 U.S. 215
Simone de Beauvoir (trans. Constance Borde and Sheila Malovany-Chevallier)

title: Fertilizer
summary: The substances added to soil to increase its productivity — from the manure and composted matter of ancient agriculture through the guano trade of the 19th century to the synthetic nitrogen fertilizers of the 20th century whose invention is estimated to have made possible the lives of roughly half the people alive today — one of the most consequential technologies in human history and one whose environmental costs are now among the most serious challenges facing planetary agriculture.

title: Feudalism
summary: The name long given to the political and social order of medieval Europe — a world without a strong central state, held together by personal bonds of land and loyalty, with lords granting fiefs to vassals in return for service and peasants tied to the manor below. It is also a contested category: the tidy pyramid was assembled by later centuries, and many historians now argue the 'feudal system' obscures the messy, local reality more than it reveals it.
aliases: Feudalism as a real social order
Feudalism as a mode of production
indexTerms: Brown, Peter, and others (Lester K. Little and Barbara H. Rosenwein, eds.)
Lester K. Little and Barbara H. Rosenwein (eds.)

title: Fidel Castro
summary: The Cuban lawyer turned guerrilla who seized power in 1959 with a few hundred fighters, built the Americas' first communist state ninety miles from Florida, faced down ten US presidents across half a century, and ruled actively until illness forced him to hand power to his brother Raul in 2006, then formally left the presidency in 2008 — remembered by admirers as the liberator who gave his people literacy, doctors, and a fierce national dignity, and by critics as the dictator who gave them prison, exile, and silence.
aliases: The Dictator Who Built a Prison

title: Fiji
summary: Fiji is an archipelago nation of more than three hundred islands in the southwestern Pacific, of which about a third are inhabited. Settled by ocean voyagers some three thousand years ago and ceded to Britain in 1874, it became a sugar colony worked by indentured laborers carried from India, whose descendants, the Indo-Fijians, came to live alongside the indigenous iTaukei. Independent since 1970 and shaken by four coups since 1987, Fiji is today the hub of the independent Pacific, a country still defined by the effort of two peoples to share one land.
aliases: The Colony That Made the Conflict

title: Fire
summary: The first technology — controlled combustion that cooked food and expanded the human brain, enabled migration into cold climates, powered metallurgy, drove industrial civilization, and now reshapes the atmosphere through the carbon it releases

title: Floods
summary: Water overflowing normally dry land is among the deadliest natural hazards on record and, paradoxically, the force that enabled the first civilizations. The Nile's annual flood, and the wilder floods of the Tigris-Euphrates and Yellow River, deposited the fertile silt that fed the first states. The toll falls hardest on the poorest, who occupy the cheapest floodplains, and deforestation and paved cities turn ordinary rain into catastrophe. Levees and dams offer control but backfire when they fail, and climate change is making extreme floods more frequent.
aliases: The silt that fed the harvest
indexTerms: Milly, P. C. D. et al.

title: Florence
summary: The Tuscan banking town on the Arno that, in two extraordinary centuries, gave the world Dante's language, Renaissance art and architecture, the banking and credit practices behind modern finance, and Machiavelli's politics — a city of perhaps fifty thousand people that is remembered, above all, as the cradle of the Renaissance.

title: Florence Nightingale
summary: The English reformer remembered as the gentle 'Lady with the Lamp' of the Crimean War, who was in fact a ferocious administrator and pioneering statistician — using mortality data and vivid charts to prove that most soldiers died of preventable disease, founding modern professional nursing, and helping to establish statistics as a tool of public-health reform.
aliases: The Deadly Hospital and the Engineers
The Right Practice from the Wrong Theory
associatedWorks: Notes on Nursing: What It Is, and What It Is Not
Notes on Matters Affecting the Health, Efficiency, and Hospital Administration of the British Army
indexTerms: Gill, Christopher J. and Gill, Gillian C.

title: Francisco Goya
summary: The Spanish painter (1746–1828) who rose from provincial Aragon to become first painter to the crown, then, after a severe illness left him deaf around 1792, turned his genius on war, superstition, and madness. His unflinching royal portraits, the satirical print series Los Caprichos, the war prints of The Disasters of War, the execution scene The Third of May 1808, and the nightmarish Black Paintings of his final years make him a hinge of art history: widely called both the last of the old masters and the first of the moderns.
aliases: Francisco José de Goya y Lucientes
The Witness of a Failed Spain
associatedWorks: Charles IV of Spain and His Family

title: Frankenstein
summary: Mary Shelley's 1818 novel, begun when she was eighteen on a ghost-story dare at Lake Geneva, in which a young scientist named Victor Frankenstein assembles a living being from dead flesh and then, horrified, flees from it. Often called the first modern science-fiction novel, it is also the modern world's deepest parable about the things we create and then refuse to take responsibility for. The creature it made famous is the maker's victim, not a brute, and the name 'Frankenstein' belongs to the scientist, not the monster he abandons.
aliases: The First Machine of the Genre
The Birth Denied to the Body
The Monster Who Took the Maker's Name
indexTerms: Gilbert, Sandra M., and Susan Gubar

title: Frantz Fanon
summary: The Martinican psychiatrist and political philosopher whose Black Skin, White Masks analyzed the psychological damage of colonialism on the colonized subject, and whose The Wretched of the Earth provided the theoretical framework for anti-colonial revolution — arguing that decolonization was necessarily violent, that national consciousness was insufficient without social transformation, and that the postcolonial state risked reproducing colonial structures under new management.

title: Franz Kafka
summary: A German-speaking Jewish insurance clerk in Prague who wrote at night and published almost nothing, Kafka turned his private dread into stories where ordinary men are arrested without charge, transformed into insects, and ground down by courts and offices that never explain themselves. He died of tuberculosis in 1924, unknown outside a small circle, after asking his friend Max Brod to burn every unfinished manuscript. Brod refused, and the world received The Trial and The Castle. His name became an adjective for the nightmare of faceless, incomprehensible authority.

title: Frederick Douglass
summary: Escaped enslaved person, abolitionist, orator, journalist, and statesman who became the most prominent Black American public figure of the nineteenth century — and whose life and writings remain foundational documents of American democracy's promise and betrayal.
associatedWorks: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
Life and Times of Frederick Douglass
What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?

title: Free Speech
summary: The principle that people may express ideas, opinions, and information without prior restraint or punishment by the state — a freedom prized across the political spectrum yet absolute nowhere, since every society forbids some speech, so the enduring argument is never whether speech is free but where the line is drawn and who is allowed to draw it.

title: Free Will
summary: The concept that humans are the authors of their own choices in a sense that makes them genuinely responsible for what they do — a concept whose acceptance or rejection determines how societies treat sin, punishment, merit, and the justice of holding people accountable for their actions.
indexTerms: John Martin Fischer, Robert Kane, Derk Pereboom, and Manuel Vargas
Soon, Chun Siong; Brass, Marcel; Heinze, Hans-Jochen; Haynes, John-Dylan

title: Frida Kahlo
summary: The Mexican painter who, after a bus accident at eighteen shattered her spine and left her in lifelong pain, turned a broken body and a turbulent life into around 143 paintings, about 55 of them self-portraits — fusing her suffering with Mexican folk art, votive imagery, and indigenous symbols, weaving in the communist politics that shaped much of her adult life — and who, largely overshadowed by her husband Diego Rivera in her lifetime, was rediscovered from the 1970s to become one of the most recognized and beloved artists in the world, and one of the most relentlessly merchandised.
aliases: Magdalena Carmen Frida Kahlo y Calderón
The Painter of Her Own Wounds
associatedWorks: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird
Self-Portrait on the Border Line Between Mexico and the United States
indexTerms: Lozano, Luis-Martín; Rivera, Juan Coronel; Zavala, Andrea Kettenmann
Luis-Martín Lozano, Juan Coronel Rivera, and Andrea Kettenmann

title: Friedrich Nietzsche
summary: The German philosopher whose radical critique of morality, religion, and truth — developed in works including Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Beyond Good and Evil, and On the Genealogy of Morality — declared the death of God, introduced the concepts of the will to power, eternal recurrence, and the Übermensch, and became one of the most influential and most misappropriated thinkers in modern history.

title: Funan
summary: One of the earliest major states of mainland Southeast Asia, and the first important Hinduized kingdom known from the region: a wealthy trading state on the Mekong delta that, around two thousand years ago, became a gateway through which Indian culture entered the region. It grew rich as a way-station on the sea route between India and China, and then faded so completely that we know it largely through Chinese reports and a scattered archaeological record, above all the port of Oc Eo. Even its name comes from Chinese records — we do not know for certain what its own people called it.
aliases: The Kingdom in the Tributary Registry
The Port Read from the Ground

title: Fyodor Dostoevsky
summary: One of the deepest psychological novelists in any language — a Russian writer who, after a staged mock execution in 1849 and four years in a Siberian prison camp, came back a believing Orthodox Christian. Crime and Punishment, Demons, and The Brothers Karamazov press a single terrifying question: if God is dead, is anything forbidden? He gave the case against God its fullest force, then answered it not with argument but with the image of freely chosen love. The same man who wrote with such compassion also voiced ugly antisemitism in his journalism — a contradiction his legacy has never resolved.
aliases: The anatomist of the divided self
The author of the Writer's Diary

title: Gabriel García Márquez
summary: The Colombian novelist who took the tall tales of his grandmother's kitchen and the violent, dreamlike history of Latin America and spun them into one of the most beloved novels of his age. With One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) he crowned the Latin American Boom and carried magical realism to the world, won the Nobel Prize in 1982, and became, by wide reckoning, one of the most widely read Latin American writers in history — a figure of near-saintly standing in the region whose lifelong friendship with Fidel Castro drew lasting criticism he never disowned.
aliases: The Boom as a global event
associatedWorks: Love in the Time of Cholera
No One Writes to the Colonel
The Story of a Shipwrecked Sailor
indexTerms: Apuleyo Mendoza, Plinio; García Márquez, Gabriel

title: Galileo Galilei
summary: The Italian mathematician, astronomer, and physicist whose telescopic observations, defense of heliocentrism, and development of experimental method made him a founding figure of the Scientific Revolution — and whose conflict with the Roman Inquisition became the paradigm case for the relationship between science and religious authority.
aliases: Scripture, Authority, and the Limits of Science
The Making of the Scientific Method
associatedWorks: Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems
Discourses and Mathematical Demonstrations Relating to Two New Sciences
Letter to the Grand Duchess Christina

title: Gamal Abdel Nasser
summary: Egypt's president from 1956 until his death in 1970, Nasser made himself the voice of the Arab world by fusing secular nationalism, anti-imperialism, and non-alignment into a force that stunned the Western powers when he nationalized the Suez Canal and turned military defeat into political triumph. At home he pursued land reform and industrialization under a one-party state with a powerful secret police. The catastrophic defeat of the 1967 Six-Day War shattered his prestige but not his hold over his people, who flooded the streets to refuse his resignation and then filled Cairo for his funeral.
associatedWorks: Egypt's Liberation: The Philosophy of the Revolution

title: Gandhara
summary: The ancient crossroads region around the Peshawar valley and Swat, in today's northwestern Pakistan and eastern Afghanistan, where Greek and Indian worlds fused under Kushan rule to produce some of the first human images of the Buddha — an art that traveled the Silk Road and shaped Buddhist sculpture across half of Asia.
aliases: The Eastern Reach of Greek Sculpture

title: Gao
summary: The city on the eastern bend of the Niger River, in what is now Mali, that grew rich on the trans-Saharan trade and became the capital of the Songhai Empire — the largest state in the history of West Africa — ruled from here by the askias at their height around 1500, and remembered above all by the great earthen pyramid-tomb of King Askia Muhammad.
aliases: The River Port at the Bend
The Tomb That Remembers an Empire
indexTerms: Nehemia Levtzion and J. F. P. Hopkins (eds.)

title: GDP
summary: The total market value of all goods and services a country produces in a year, and the single most powerful number in modern governance. GDP made the economy legible as one steerable figure — an indispensable tool that, precisely because it became the goal, quietly defined what counts as progress and what does not.
aliases: The Growth Imperative and Its Costs
Counting the Cleanup, Missing the Damage
indexTerms: Stiglitz, Joseph E.; Sen, Amartya; Fitoussi, Jean-Paul
Joseph E. Stiglitz, Amartya Sen, and Jean-Paul Fitoussi

title: Gender
summary: The concept distinguishing the social and cultural dimensions of human identity — roles, behaviors, expressions, and identities associated with being a man, woman, or other category — from biological sex, and the site of sustained political and scholarly debate about what that distinction means and requires.

title: Genghis Khan
summary: The Mongol leader who unified the nomadic tribes of the steppe and conquered the largest contiguous land empire in history — bringing catastrophic destruction to settled civilizations while creating the Pax Mongolica that opened Eurasia to trade, ideas, and plague on an unprecedented scale.
indexTerms: Zerjal, Tatiana; Xue, Yali; Bertorelle, Giorgio; et al.

title: Genghis Khan's Successors
summary: The era after Genghis Khan's death in 1227, when the largest contiguous land empire in history passed to his sons and grandsons and split into four vast khanates — the Yuan in China, the Ilkhanate in Persia, the Golden Horde on the Russian steppe, and the Chagatai realm in Central Asia. For roughly a century these states ruled or dominated lands from Korea and China to Persia, Central Asia, and the Russian steppe, knit Eurasia into one connected world under the Pax Mongolica, then dissolved into the nations that still remember them with both dread and pride.

title: Genocide
summary: The crime so total that it had no legal name until 1944, when the lawyer Raphael Lemkin coined the word for the deliberate attempt to destroy a whole people — a national, ethnic, racial, or religious group — and the 1948 UN Genocide Convention made it a crime under international law, defining it by specific acts done with intent to destroy a group, a definition argued over ever since: what counts, who decides, and why the promise to prevent it keeps failing.
aliases: Genocide as a Failure to Act
indexTerms: Bloxham, Donald and A. Dirk Moses (eds.)

title: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel
summary: The German philosopher whose dialectical idealism — the idea that history unfolds through the conflict and resolution of opposing forces, driven by the development of Spirit toward freedom — shaped Marx, Nietzsche, existentialism, and 20th-century political thought more profoundly than almost any other thinker.
aliases: The Idealist Standing on His Head

title: George Orwell
summary: The English writer whose name became an adjective for the manipulations of power he spent his life exposing. Shot through the throat fighting in the Spanish Civil War and then hunted by Soviet-backed Communists on his own side, Eric Blair — who wrote as George Orwell — turned that betrayal into a lifelong war on political lying. A democratic socialist to the end, he wrote Animal Farm and Nineteen Eighty-Four, giving the language Big Brother, Newspeak, and doublethink. His essay on political language argued that vague prose serves tyranny. He died in 1950, and the adjective Orwellian outlived him.
aliases: The plain style and its certainties
associatedWorks: Down and Out in Paris and London

title: George Washington
summary: The Virginia planter and Continental Army commander who won American independence, presided over the Constitutional Convention, and served as the first President of the United States — and whose most consequential act was relinquishing power — resigning his commission in 1783 and declining to seek a third term before stepping down in 1797 — and thereby establishing that the republic's military commander and its chief executive would answer to law rather than to personal ambition.

title: Germ Theory
summary: The understanding that specific infectious diseases are caused by specific microorganisms — bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites — that invade the body from outside. Germ theory replaced miasma theory, the ancient and medieval belief that disease arose from bad air and environmental corruption, and in doing so transformed medicine from a largely ineffective art into an increasingly precise science.

title: Getúlio Vargas
summary: The gaucho lawyer and politician who seized Brazil in the Revolution of 1930 and dominated it for a quarter-century, ruling as both an iron-fisted authoritarian who shut the congress, censored the press, and jailed his opponents, and as the great architect of the modern Brazilian state, who gave the country labor laws, industry, and a new sense of itself — beloved by workers as the Father of the Poor, condemned by critics as a dictator who admired aspects of European fascism, and who, cornered at last in 1954, shot himself in the heart and left a letter that turned his death into a final political weapon.
aliases: The Strongman and the Closed Congress

title: Ghana Empire
summary: The Ghana Empire, known to its own people as Wagadou, was the first of the great Sahelian trading states of West Africa — a Soninke-ruled power that grew rich not by mining gold but by taxing the trans-Saharan exchange of West African gold for Saharan salt, making 'Ghana' a byword for gold in the medieval Mediterranean before it gave way to its successor, Mali.
aliases: The empire described from a thousand miles away

title: Glass
summary: The manufactured material — made from silica heated to transparency — that gave human civilization windows, lenses, mirrors, and laboratory vessels: reorganizing architecture by separating inside from outside without blocking light, enabling the Scientific Revolution by making the telescope and microscope possible, and now forming the fiber-optic backbone of global communications.

title: Globalization
summary: The accelerating integration of economies, societies, cultures, and political systems across national boundaries — and the analytical framework for understanding the processes that drive that integration, the inequalities it produces, and the political reactions against it.
indexTerms: Autor, David, Dorn, David, and Hanson, Gordon
Hall, Peter A., and Soskice, David (eds.)
Reinhart, Carmen M., and Rogoff, Kenneth S.

title: Goguryeo
summary: Goguryeo (traditionally 37 BCE–668 CE) was the northernmost and most militarized of the Three Kingdoms of early Korea, a fortress state that ruled much of the northern Korean peninsula and southern Manchuria. It is remembered for repelling enormous Chinese invasions before falling to a Silla–Tang alliance, and its heritage is now contested between Korea and China, which advance rival historical claims to a kingdom that predated both modern nations.
aliases: Goguryeo as the strong northern ancestor
Goguryeo as a frontier regime of the northeast
A frontier state that fits no modern map

title: GPS
summary: A constellation of satellites carrying atomic clocks that lets any receiver on Earth calculate its precise location and the exact time, for free, at any moment. Built as a US military navigation system, GPS was opened to civilian use after a 1983 airline disaster, its full accuracy unlocked in 2000. It dissolved one of the oldest human problems — knowing where you are — into invisible infrastructure, then quietly wove its timing signal into the hidden rhythm of financial networks, power grids, and precision weapons, making modern civilization dependent on a faint pulse from orbit.
aliases: The System That Cannot Be Given Away
Infrastructure You Did Not Build and Cannot Control
A Single Point of Failure Disguised as a Utility
indexTerms: Parkinson, Bradford W., and James J. Spilker Jr.
Lombardi, Michael A., Thomas P. Heavner, and Steven R. Jefferts
Bradford W. Parkinson and James J. Spilker Jr.

title: Great Zimbabwe
summary: The massive stone city in southern Africa built by the ancestral Shona between roughly the 11th and 15th centuries — capital of a wealthy gold-trading state, the largest pre-modern stone structure in sub-Saharan Africa, and the target of a long colonial campaign to deny that Africans had built it at all.
aliases: A State Read From Stone and Soil
A Past Too Inconvenient to Allow

title: Greek Tragedy
summary: The dramatic form that emerged in archaic Athens and flourished in democratic Athens in the 5th century BC, performed at the festivals of the god Dionysus — staged in public competition before thousands of citizens, acted by masked men and a singing chorus, in which great figures are destroyed by fate, the gods, and their own flaws. It helped create the Western theatrical tradition, gave us the idea of tragedy itself, and through Aristotle's later theory of catharsis shaped two thousand years of thinking about why we watch suffering.
aliases: Tragedy as the Rulebook of Drama
Tragedy as a Confrontation with Suffering
indexTerms: Csapo, Eric and Slater, William J.

title: Gregor Mendel
summary: The Augustinian friar in Brno who found the arithmetic of heredity by growing and counting roughly 28,000 pea plants between 1856 and 1863. Mendel showed that traits pass not by blending but as discrete factors — one drawn from each parent — with dominant forms masking recessives in a steady 3-to-1 ratio. His 1866 paper was ignored for thirty-four years. Around 1900 three botanists independently rediscovered it and founded Mendelism. In 1936 R. A. Fisher noted that Mendel’s ratios fit theory suspiciously well; the debate over his data remains unresolved.
aliases: The Numbers That Fit Too Well
associatedWorks: On Hieracium-Hybrids Obtained by Artificial Fertilisation

title: Guernica
summary: Pablo Picasso's large-scale oil painting depicting the bombing of the Basque town of Guernica by Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy at the request of Franco's Nationalist forces on April 26, 1937. Guernica became the canonical image of civilian suffering under aerial bombardment — reproduced on protest banners, invoked in Security Council debates, and used by movements across the political spectrum as a symbol of the costs of war. Its political life has operated independently of Picasso's biography for decades.
aliases: The Universal Image of Civilian Suffering
Modernism's Test Case for Political Art

title: Gunpowder
summary: The mixture of charcoal, sulfur, and potassium nitrate discovered in Tang dynasty China that became the first chemical explosive in human history — transforming warfare, undermining the military advantage of fortifications and armored cavalry, enabling European colonial expansion, and restructuring political power by making large standing armies affordable and decisive in ways that had not previously been possible.

title: Haile Selassie
summary: The last emperor of Ethiopia, crowned in 1930 as Haile Selassie I, who modernized an ancient sacred monarchy while keeping near-absolute power, became a global symbol of African independence through his 1936 appeal to the League of Nations and his hosting of the Organization of African Unity in 1963, was revered half a world away as a living god by the Rastafari movement though he remained a devout Orthodox Christian, and was finally deposed in the 1974 revolution after a concealed famine, dying in detention in 1975.
aliases: The dignified father of African unity
The living God and returned messiah
The remote autocrat over a starving land

title: Hamlet
summary: Shakespeare's tragedy of a grieving prince (written around 1600), commanded by his father's ghost to avenge a murder, who instead questions, doubts, and delays — the longest and most famous of his plays, and the work most often credited with putting a fully modern inner life on the stage. In his soliloquies, above all 'To be, or not to be,' a character first seems to think aloud and to possess a hidden self. Why Hamlet delays has never been settled, and the play survives in several differing early texts, with no single original.
aliases: The Tragedy of Hamlet, Prince of Denmark
The Ghost and the Abolished Afterlife
indexTerms: Shakespeare, William (eds. Ann Thompson and Neil Taylor)
William Shakespeare, eds. Ann Thompson and Neil Taylor

title: Hampi
summary: The ruined capital of the Vijayanagara Empire, scattered across a vast field of granite boulders on the Tungabhadra river in Karnataka, southern India. For two centuries one of the richest cities on earth, it was sacked over months after the Battle of Talikota in 1565 and never rebuilt as a capital. Its temples, palaces, and waterworks survive as one of the great ruin fields of the world, with the Virupaksha temple still in worship and the site protected by UNESCO.
aliases: A Temple That Was Never Lost
One Capital in a Shared World
indexTerms: John M. Fritz and George Michell
George Michell and Phillip B. Wagoner

title: Hangul
summary: The native alphabet of Korea, created at the court of King Sejong and promulgated in 1443–46 — one of the few writing systems ever deliberately designed rather than inherited, and built so its letters diagram the sounds they make. Dismissed for centuries by a Confucian elite who kept Chinese for serious writing, it was revived by nationalism and became the script of both Koreas after 1945; linguists rank it among the most systematically designed alphabets ever made.
aliases: An alphabet designed from the mouth
The vulgar script that threatened the learned
The national treasure and democratic gift
indexTerms: Ki-Moon Lee and S. Robert Ramsey

title: Hannah Arendt
summary: German-Jewish political philosopher whose experience of totalitarianism, statelessness, and exile shaped a body of work analyzing the nature of political action, the origins of modern tyranny, the 'banality of evil,' and the conditions under which human freedom and plurality can be sustained.
aliases: Jewish Community and Holocaust Scholarship Perspective
Democratic Theory and Civic Republican Perspective

title: Harriet Tubman
summary: The Maryland-born enslaved woman who escaped slavery in 1849 and then returned south approximately thirteen times over the following decade to guide roughly seventy enslaved people to freedom via the Underground Railroad — becoming the most celebrated conductor in that network's history — before serving as a Union spy, scout, and nurse during the Civil War, and subsequently spending decades advocating for Black civil rights and women's suffrage.
indexTerms: Federal Writers' Project, Works Progress Administration

title: Haudenosaunee Confederacy (Iroquois)
summary: The Great Law of Peace, carried for centuries in memory and wampum rather than ink, bound five warring nations of the northeastern woodlands into one confederacy governed by a council of fifty chiefs who decide by consensus. The Peacemaker and Hiawatha founded it — the date is debated. Clan mothers choose the chiefs and can remove them. The Tuscarora joined in 1722, making Six Nations. The Haudenosaunee became a major military and diplomatic force before the American Revolution split it. Congress formally acknowledged the Confederacy's influence on American ideas of liberty in 1988. It endures today as a living government.
aliases: The Confederacy from inside the longhouse
The Confederacy as a power among empires
indexTerms: Grinde, Donald A., and Bruce E. Johansen
Donald A. Grinde and Bruce E. Johansen

title: Healthcare Policy
summary: How a society ensures people can access medical care, and who pays — the most complex domestic policy domain in the United States, touching financing, delivery, insurance, labor, pharmaceutical economics, and federalism simultaneously
indexTerms: Papanicolas, Irene; Woskie, Liana R.; Jha, Ashish K.
Pollin, Robert; Heintz, James; Arno, Peter; Wicks-Lim, Jeannette; Ash, Michael

title: Hernán Cortés
summary: The Spanish conquistador who destroyed the Aztec Empire in two years, though with an army mostly not his own. Landing in Mexico in 1519 against his governor’s orders, with a few hundred soldiers and scuttled ships, he won by three things Spanish arms alone could not: the armies of the Tlaxcalans and other peoples who hated Aztec rule, the indispensable work of La Malinche as interpreter and guide, and a smallpox epidemic that killed more than any sword. He built Mexico City on the ruins of Tenochtitlan. In Spain he became a hero; in Mexico he has almost no monuments.

title: Hinduism
summary: Not a single religion with a founder, a creed, or a church, but a vast family of related Indian traditions that share a body of sacred texts and core ideas — duty, action and its fruits, rebirth, and release — while differing enormously in deity, practice, and philosophy. The very word is largely a modern, colonial-era umbrella; many practitioners call it Sanatana Dharma, the eternal way. It is now the world's third-largest religious tradition.
aliases: The Eternal Way Seen from Within
The Religion Seen from the Bottom
The Religion Seen as a Modern Object

title: Hip-Hop
summary: The culture that poor Black and Latino youth built in the South Bronx in the early 1970s out of almost nothing: two turntables, a microphone, a spray can, and their own bodies, worked into four linked art forms — DJing, rapping, b-boying, and graffiti. What began in the rubble of an abandoned city became, within thirty years, commercially dominant across the United States and globally influential, carrying long arguments about authenticity, violence, and the corporate industry it grew into.

title: Hippocrates
summary: The Greek physician of Kos, remembered as the father of medicine, under whose name the roughly sixty texts of the Hippocratic Corpus gathered one of the earliest sustained insistences that disease has natural rather than divine causes — though almost nothing about the man can be securely known, the corpus was written by many hands over generations, and the famous Oath bearing his name cannot confidently be traced to him.
aliases: The Father Built by His Heirs
The Oath as Symbol and as Evidence
indexTerms: Hippocrates (tr. G. E. R. Lloyd, ed.)
Hippocrates (ed. G. E. R. Lloyd, tr. Chadwick and Mann)

title: Hiroshige
summary: The Japanese woodblock artist Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858), often called the last great master of the color print called ukiyo-e, who made weather and travel his subject and taught his viewers to feel rain, snow, mist, and distance. His Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido captured the road between Edo and Kyoto in driving rain and muffled snow, and his late One Hundred Famous Views of Edo cropped the city with startling boldness. When his prints and Hokusai's reached Europe after Japan reopened, they helped ignite Japonisme and remade Western painting; Vincent van Gogh made oil copies of two of Hiroshige’s designs.
associatedWorks: The Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido
One Hundred Famous Views of Edo
Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Atake
Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces
indexTerms: Smith, Henry D., II and Poster, Amy G.
Henry D. Smith II and Amy G. Poster

title: HIV/AIDS
summary: The pandemic caused by the human immunodeficiency virus, which crossed from primates to humans in central Africa around the early twentieth century and, after recognition in 1981, killed more than forty million people worldwide. HIV destroys the immune system and, untreated, leads to AIDS — once almost always fatal, now manageable with antiretroviral therapy. The disease's path was shaped less by biology alone than by stigma, poverty, political neglect, and the deeply unequal question of who could afford the drugs that saved lives.
aliases: The Burden the World Watched Slowly
When a Government Rejected the Science

title: Ho Chi Minh
summary: The revolutionary who spent thirty years in exile organizing for Vietnamese independence, then led the movement that defeated French colonial rule and outlasted the United States — founding the Communist state of North Vietnam and becoming the revered 'Uncle Ho' of national legend, while his movement also built a one-party regime whose land-reform purges and suppression of rivals remain a contested part of his record. He died in 1969, six years before the 1975 reunification his cause achieved.
aliases: The Architect of a Lost Country
The Communist in the Nationalist's Clothing
associatedWorks: Declaration of Independence of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam (1945)

title: Hokusai
summary: The Japanese woodblock artist who made what may be the most recognizable image in the history of Japanese art, the towering clawed breaker known as The Great Wave off Kanagawa, part of his series Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji. Astonishingly prolific across a long life, restless enough to change his name dozens of times, he worked in ukiyo-e, the popular print art of Tokugawa Japan, and insisted into his late eighties that he was only beginning to learn. When his prints reached Europe after Japan reopened, they helped ignite Japonisme and reshaped Western painting.
aliases: The Old Man Mad About Drawing
associatedWorks: One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
A Tour of the Waterfalls of the Provinces

title: Hollywood
summary: The American film industry and the Los Angeles district that gave it a name — where, from the 1910s, vertically integrated studios (many built by Jewish immigrant entrepreneurs) turned storytelling into an assembly line, controlling production, distribution, and theaters. Hollywood became the dominant force in world cinema, selling a glossy image of America to the planet. Its record is shadowed: the Production Code censored content for decades, the Red Scare blacklist destroyed careers, and the industry has a long history of racial exclusion, gender discrimination, and off-screen abuse.
aliases: The Dream That Shut the Door

title: Hong Kong
summary: The barren island ceded to Britain in the wreckage of the First Opium War that became one of Asia’s richest cities, long known for open markets, rule of law, and unusually broad civil liberties — a meeting point of China and the West — then returned to China in 1997 under a promise of fifty years of autonomy, and after 2019 the front line of a contested struggle over what that promise meant.
indexTerms: Government of the United Kingdom and Government of the People's Republic of China

title: Horses
summary: The domestication of the horse around 3500 BCE on the Eurasian steppe was among the most consequential events in human history -- transforming warfare, trade, agriculture, and political organization across Eurasia, enabling the steppe empires that repeatedly reshaped the ancient and medieval world, and reverberating through the Columbian Exchange to transform the Americas.
indexTerms: Pablo Librado, Ludovic Orlando et al.

title: Human Rights
summary: The doctrine that all human beings possess inherent rights by virtue of their humanity — rights that governments cannot legitimately violate and that the international community has a responsibility to protect — institutionalized after World War II in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and subsequent treaties, and contested ever since on questions of universality, enforceability, cultural specificity, and political instrumentalization.

title: Humanitarian Intervention
summary: The question of whether states or international bodies are morally and legally permitted — or obligated — to use military force against a sovereign state to protect that state's population from mass atrocities, genocide, or crimes against humanity — a question that pits two foundational principles of international order against each other: the sovereignty of states and the protection of human beings from their own governments.

title: Hurricanes
summary: A vast rotating storm that draws energy from warm tropical water and converts it into wind, rain, and the deadliest hazard of all, storm surge — called a hurricane in the Atlantic, a typhoon in the western Pacific, a cyclone in the Indian Ocean. The word traces through Spanish from a Caribbean Indigenous name, linked to the Maya storm god Huracán. The Caribbean and Gulf remain the most hurricane-prone seas on earth; these storms repeatedly reshaped colonial economies, naval history, and migration patterns, and a warming ocean is expected to intensify the strongest of them.
aliases: The storm that took the provision ground
The storm as ruin and claim
The storm that can be seen coming

title: Hydropower
summary: The generation of electricity from falling or flowing water — the world's oldest and still its largest source of renewable electricity, and the clearest case of clean power whose cleanliness conceals large human and ecological costs.

title: Ibn al-Haytham / Alhazen
summary: The mathematician and astronomer of Basra and Cairo, Latinized as Alhazen, who helped found the medieval science of mathematical and experimental optics. His Book of Optics (Kitab al-Manazir), written under the Fatimid caliphate around the 1010s, argued that vision works by light entering the eye from objects rather than by rays the eye sends out, and built that argument on systematic experiment and geometry. Translated into Latin as Perspectiva, the work shaped Roger Bacon, Witelo, and eventually Kepler, and his insistence on testing claims against controlled observation has led many modern writers to call him an early pioneer of the experimental method.
associatedWorks: On the Configuration of the World (Fi Hay'at al-Alam)

title: Ibn Battuta
summary: The Moroccan scholar and traveler who, between 1325 and 1354, covered approximately 75,000 miles across the Islamic world, Africa, Central Asia, India, Southeast Asia, and China — producing the Rihla, the most extensive account of the medieval world written by a single observer. Ibn Battuta's journeys documented the geographic reach of Islamic civilization at its maximum extent and remain the primary eyewitness source for dozens of medieval societies.
aliases: The Rihla as Genre and as Document
The Most Important and Most Contested Source
Ibn Battuta and the Decolonization of Exploration

title: Ibn Khaldun
summary: The 14th-century Tunisian polymath — historian, sociologist, economist, and political theorist — whose Muqaddimah (Introduction to History) articulated the first systematic theory of how and why civilizations rise and fall, introducing concepts of group solidarity (asabiyyah), economic analysis, and the sociology of knowledge that anticipate by four centuries ideas usually credited to the European Enlightenment and social science traditions.
aliases: The Crown of Islamic Historical Scholarship

title: Ibn Sina
summary: The Persian polymath whose Canon of Medicine became the foundational medical textbook of both the Islamic world and medieval Europe for five centuries, and whose synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Islamic theology in works like the Book of Healing shaped the development of scholastic philosophy — establishing the framework within which Aquinas, Albertus Magnus, and the European universities would engage Greek thought.

title: Ida B. Wells
summary: The journalist born into slavery who became the nation's fiercest investigator of lynching — showing with facts and figures that the public torture-killings of Black Americans were not punishment for crime but a tool of racial and economic control, demolishing the claim that they answered the assault of white women, surviving the destruction of her newspaper and threats on her life to carry the campaign across the Atlantic, helping found the NAACP, and confronting the racism she met even inside the white-led women's suffrage movement.
aliases: The Investigator Who Named the Terror
The Ally They Asked to Stand Behind
The Figure the Country Chose to Forget
associatedWorks: Southern Horrors: Lynch Law in All Its Phases
Crusade for Justice: The Autobiography of Ida B. Wells
indexTerms: Wells, Ida B. (ed. Alfreda M. Duster)
Ida B. Wells (ed. Jacqueline Jones Royster)
Ida B. Wells (ed. Alfreda M. Duster)

title: Ideology
summary: The concept describing the systems of ideas, beliefs, and values through which people understand social and political reality — and the analytical framework for explaining why those systems tend to serve the interests of particular groups while appearing to be universal or natural.

title: Ife
summary: The sacred Yoruba city in southwestern Nigeria, held in Yoruba religion to be the place where the world and humankind began, the spiritual home whose ruler is the Ooni, and the source of an extraordinary tradition of lifelike copper-alloy and terracotta heads made between roughly the twelfth and fifteenth centuries — works so refined that a European explorer in 1910 refused to believe Africans had made them.
aliases: The Place Where the World Began
A Masterwork on the World Stage
indexTerms: Henry John Drewal and Enid Schildkrout

title: Immanuel Kant
summary: The Prussian philosopher who attempted to reconcile rationalism and empiricism, established the Categorical Imperative as the foundation of moral philosophy, argued for the conditions of possible experience in the Critique of Pure Reason, and shaped every major philosophical tradition that followed — from German idealism to analytic philosophy to contemporary ethics.
associatedWorks: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals

title: Immigration Policy
summary: How a state decides who may enter, live, work, and become a citizen, and how it enforces those limits. Treated here as a policy question rather than a settled story, immigration policy sets a nation's right to define itself against the ancient human drive to move toward a better life, and it divides modern democracies like almost nothing else. The economics is genuinely mixed, depending on the immigrant and the measure; the questions of culture, refugees, sovereignty, and the undocumented resist clean answers. This entry maps the contested choice fairly, steelmanning both the case for more open immigration and the case for tighter restriction, without siding with either.
aliases: The Freedom to Seek a Better Life
indexTerms: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine

title: Imperialism
summary: The policy, practice, or advocacy of extending a state's power and influence over other territories — through military conquest, economic domination, or political control — and the theoretical tradition that analyzes and contests the dynamics, justifications, and legacies of imperial expansion.
indexTerms: Acemoglu, Daron, Johnson, Simon, and Robinson, James A.

title: Impressionism
summary: The nineteenth-century French painting movement — Monet, Renoir, Degas, Pissarro, Morisot, and others — that broke with the official Paris Salon to paint modern life outdoors in quick, visible strokes of bright, unmixed color. Chasing changing light rather than polished historical subjects, and aided by the new portable paint tube, it opened the threshold of modern art. Its name was borrowed from a hostile review of Monet’s Impression, Sunrise (1874).
aliases: The Modern Life Women Could Not Enter

title: Indian Ocean Trade System
summary: The maritime trading network that connected East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and China for over three thousand years — the world's most durable commercial system and the mechanism through which Buddhism, Islam, crops, and technologies spread across half the globe.
aliases: The Discovery of the Sea Route
indexTerms: João de Barros, Fernão Lopes de Castanheda, Gaspar Correia

title: Indian Removal
summary: United States policy crystallized in the Indian Removal Act of 1830, compelling Indigenous nations east of the Mississippi to cede homelands and relocate west. It was contested at the time, narrowly enacted, and then carried out in defiance of the Supreme Court — which poses harder questions than the cruelty of the marches alone: about federal power, treaty obligation, and whether a constitutional republic can lawfully dispossess sovereign nations within its borders. The Cherokee, Muscogee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Seminole, and others endure today as self-governing peoples, and the treaty questions removal raised remain active law.
aliases: A Republic Overriding Its Own Law
indexTerms: Theda Perdue and Michael D. Green

title: Indigo
summary: Indigo is the deep blue dye that organized plantation systems across three continents, sparked one of the earliest mass resistance movements in colonial South Asia, and then vanished from the fields within a decade when synthetic chemistry made the natural plant obsolete — only to reappear as the color of blue jeans, the most democratic garment in the world.

title: Indira Gandhi
summary: India's only female prime minister and its dominant political figure for nearly two decades, who championed the poor with her 'Garibi Hatao' (abolish poverty) politics, nationalized banks, and led India to a decisive victory in the 1971 war that helped create Bangladesh, then in 1975 declared a state of Emergency that suspended civil liberties, jailed the opposition, and censored the press — the gravest assault on Indian democracy by an elected leader — before her 1984 assassination by two of her Sikh bodyguards, months after she ordered the army into the Golden Temple, set off anti-Sikh massacres that killed thousands.
aliases: The strong mother of the nation
The leader who broke the republic

title: Inequality
summary: The unequal distribution of income, wealth, opportunity, and status among individuals and groups — a feature of every known society, whose causes, consequences, and moral significance are among the most contested questions in politics and economics.

title: Inflation
summary: A sustained rise in the general level of prices — equivalently, a fall in the purchasing power of money — and one of the most politically charged forces in economic life. Inflation silently transfers wealth between creditors and debtors, erodes savings, and has toppled governments; controlling it became the defining mission of modern central banking.
indexTerms: Reinhart, Carmen M.; Rogoff, Kenneth S.

title: Insurance
summary: The institution through which individuals transfer the financial consequences of catastrophic risk to a collective — turning an unbearable individual loss into a calculable, shared cost — and the mechanism that made probability theory into a commercial technology, turned the future into a product, and raised persistent questions about who is deemed insurable and on what terms.

title: Interest
summary: The price charged for the use of borrowed money over time — the mechanism through which lenders are compensated for waiting, through which small debts can compound into large ones, and through which the economy's master price is set, steering investment and saving across the entire financial system.
aliases: Interest as the Price of Capital
indexTerms: Lukasz Rachel and Thomas D. Smith

title: International Law
summary: The body of rules, treaties, customs, and principles that govern relations between states and, increasingly, between states and individuals — including the laws of war, human rights obligations, trade regimes, and environmental agreements — enforced not by a world government but through reciprocity, reputation, domestic courts, and international tribunals whose authority states themselves must accept.
aliases: Law as the Servant of Power
Law as the Infrastructure of Cooperation
Law as the Continuation of Colonialism
indexTerms: United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change
Goldsmith, Jack L. and Posner, Eric A.

title: Iron
summary: The metal that broke the Bronze Age monopoly on power — by widening access to effective tools and weapons beyond the tin-dependent elite of the Bronze Age, transforming agriculture, and enabling the scale of construction and warfare that defined every subsequent civilization

title: Irrigation
summary: The technology of redirecting water to agricultural land, practiced for eight thousand years across every major civilization. Irrigation made agriculture possible in arid regions, enabled the population densities that produced cities and states, and created the hydraulic infrastructure that became the material foundation for some of the world's most durable empires — while also generating the political conflicts, ecological strains, and infrastructure dependencies that shaped civilizational trajectories from Mesopotamia to the American West.

title: Isaac Newton
summary: The English mathematician and natural philosopher whose Principia Mathematica (1687) unified terrestrial and celestial mechanics under universal gravitation, whose development of calculus provided the mathematical language of modern science, and whose optical discoveries established the nature of light — making him the dominant figure of the Scientific Revolution and the foundation of classical physics.

title: Islam
summary: A monotheistic religious tradition originating in 7th-century Arabia, now comprising approximately 1.8 billion adherents worldwide — encompassing a legal and ethical system derived from the Quran and the Hadith, a civilization that produced major advances in philosophy, mathematics, medicine, and the arts, and a diversity of practice and interpretation across fourteen centuries and dozens of distinct cultural contexts.
aliases: The Living Sunnah and Its Interpretation

title: Islamic Philosophy
summary: Islamic philosophy, known in Arabic as falsafa, is the tradition of rational inquiry that took up ancient Greek philosophy, above all Aristotle and Neoplatonism, and developed it in Arabic across the medieval Islamic world. Pursued by Muslim, Christian, and Jewish thinkers from roughly the ninth century onward, it produced original systems of metaphysics and logic and, through Latin translations, carried much of Greek thought back into Europe.
indexTerms: Adamson, Peter, and Taylor, Richard C. (eds.)
Jon McGinnis and David C. Reisman

title: J. Robert Oppenheimer
summary: The American theoretical physicist who led the scientific work of the Manhattan Project at Los Alamos, building the first nuclear weapons — used weeks later on Hiroshima and Nagasaki — and who spent much of the rest of his life warning about what he had helped make: opposing a crash program for the hydrogen bomb, urging international control, and then being stripped of his security clearance in a 1954 hearing widely condemned as a political show trial, a decision the United States government formally vacated in 2022.
aliases: The Martyr of the Atomic Age
The Builder and the Compromised Adviser
indexTerms: Bird, Kai and Sherwin, Martin J.
Kai Bird and Martin J. Sherwin

title: Jainism
summary: One of the world's oldest living religions, born in the same ancient Indian ferment as Buddhism among the non-Vedic renouncers, and built around a single principle carried further than perhaps any other tradition has carried it: ahimsa, non-harm to every living being, down to the smallest.
