Its central teacher is Mahavira, by tradition the last of twenty-four ford-makers, though Jains hold the path itself to be eternal.
aliases: The Eternal Path Seen from Within
The Tradition Tested by Outside Frameworks

title: James Baldwin
summary: The American essayist and novelist who became one of his country’s essential moral witnesses on race. A boy preacher in Harlem before fifteen, Baldwin carried the pulpit’s cadences into prose of extraordinary force. He moved to Paris in 1948; the distance gave him clarity about America. Go Tell It on the Mountain drew on his boyhood; Giovanni’s Room (1956) was a landmark of gay literature written when that endangered careers; The Fire Next Time (1963) told white America that the racial lie was destroying the liar alongside the lied-about. He refused to separate race from sexuality when both were liabilities, and declined every available political comfort.
associatedWorks: Go Tell It on the Mountain
Tell Me How Long the Train's Been Gone

title: James Clerk Maxwell
summary: Scottish physicist (1831–1879) whose equations united electricity, magnetism, and light as a single electromagnetic phenomenon. Maxwell gave Faraday's experimental intuitions rigorous mathematical form, then found that his equations implied waves traveling at the speed of light — from which he concluded that light is an electromagnetic wave. The theory predicted a whole spectrum of invisible radiation, confirmed by Hertz in 1887. He also helped found statistical mechanics and made the first durable color photograph. Ranked alongside Newton and Einstein, he stands as one of the greatest physicists who ever lived.
aliases: The Source of the Wireless World
The Greatest Physicist Few Can Picture
associatedWorks: A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism
A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field
Illustrations of the Dynamical Theory of Gases

title: James Joyce
summary: Joyce left Dublin young and spent his life in self-imposed exile on the Continent — poor, his eyesight failing — yet wrote about almost nothing but the city he had abandoned. Breaking with the Church and Irish nationalism alike, he pushed fiction further inward than anyone before: Dubliners, A Portrait of the Artist, and above all Ulysses, which renders the unfiltered stream of a single mind across one ordinary Dublin day. Banned for obscenity in Britain and America for over a decade, Ulysses redefined what a novel could do. His final book, Finnegans Wake, dissolved language itself into multilingual dream-puns.
associatedWorks: A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man

title: Jane Austen
summary: Six novels about courtship among country families — yet the comedy covers a precise study of money, manners, and the trap that made marriage a woman's only real security. Born in 1775, a clergyman's daughter who published anonymously and never married, Austen perfected free indirect discourse: narrator and character speaking at once, irony and sympathy inseparable. She died at forty-one with her fame still modest. Scholars argue whether she was a quiet radical exposing women's constraints or a conservative whose happy endings restored the gentry order. Said's reading of Mansfield Park added a third dispute: an empire kept just offstage.

title: Java
summary: Java is a narrow volcanic island in Indonesia, about the same size as England, that holds more than 150 million people — by most measures the most crowded large island on earth. A spine of dozens of active and potentially active volcanoes runs its length; their eruptions are deadly, but their ash makes the soil among the most fertile in the world, feeding intensive wet-rice farming that has supported dense populations for over a thousand years. That fertility built a long succession of civilizations and made Java the political, cultural, and economic heart of the Indonesian world, anchored today by the sinking megacity of Jakarta.

title: Jawaharlal Nehru
summary: Independent India's first and longest-serving prime minister, who governed from 1947 to 1964 and, more than any other single figure, stamped the new nation as a secular, democratic, state-planned, and non-aligned republic — entrenching parliamentary rule and free elections, building industry through Five-Year Plans, and leading the bloc of nations that refused to choose sides in the Cold War, while leaving behind the contested record over Kashmir, the 1962 defeat by China, slow growth, and his daughter's later succession that frames India's arguments with itself to this day.

title: Jayavarman VII
summary: Jayavarman VII was a Khmer king (reigned c. 1181 – c. 1218) who ruled the Angkorian empire near its height, drove out a Cham army that had sacked the capital in 1177, and rebuilt the realm as a Buddhist state. He was the most prolific builder in Khmer history, founding the walled city of Angkor Thom with its face-covered Bayon temple, raising Ta Prohm and Preah Khan for his parents, and endowing a network of roads, rest-houses, and hospitals recorded in his inscriptions.
aliases: The Beloved King of a Golden Age

title: Jazz
summary: Around 1900, Black New Orleans fused the blues, ragtime, spirituals, and brass-band tradition into something built on improvisation, swing, and call-and-response. Carried north and east by the Great Migration, jazz became the century's defining American music: the soloist's art of Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington's orchestral ambition, big-band swing, Charlie Parker's bebop, and the modal and free explorations of Miles Davis and John Coltrane. Its Black creators were segregated and exploited by a white-run industry even as their art entered concert halls worldwide. Amiri Baraka and Albert Murray staked out the terms in which it is still debated.
indexTerms: Ward, Geoffrey C., and Ken Burns

title: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
summary: The Geneva-born philosopher whose ideas about natural goodness, the social contract, the general will, and education shaped the French Revolution, Romanticism, modern democracy, and progressive pedagogy — while his life and contradictions made him one of the most contested figures in Western thought.

title: Jerusalem
summary: The city where three world religions converge, four centuries of empire have claimed, and a political conflict remains unresolved — compressed into one square kilometer of contested sacred geography
aliases: The Holy City and Its Replicas
indexTerms: Israel Finkelstein and Neil Asher Silberman
Government of Israel and Palestine Liberation Organization

title: Jesus of Nazareth
summary: Jewish teacher and healer from Roman-ruled Galilee who proclaimed the kingdom of God, taught in parables, gathered followers, and was crucified in Jerusalem under the prefect Pontius Pilate around 30–33 CE. His followers proclaimed that God raised him from the dead, launching the movement that became Christianity — now the world's largest religion, with roughly 2.4 billion adherents who regard him as the incarnate Son of God. In Islam he is Isa, a revered prophet and Messiah. The historical man and the Christ of faith run through the same texts and must be carefully held apart.

title: Jim Crow
summary: The system of legal racial segregation and disenfranchisement that ruled the American South for nearly a century after Reconstruction, stamping 'white' and 'colored' onto schools, railcars, and drinking fountains, stripping the vote from millions of Black citizens through poll taxes and literacy tests, and enforcing itself through lynching, convict leasing, and the daily threat of violence — until the civil rights movement broke it with the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
aliases: The Defense That Was a Disguise

title: Joan of Arc
summary: The teenage peasant who, in 1429, convinced the heir to the French throne that God had sent her to save France — and briefly did. She helped lift the English siege of Orléans, stood beside Charles VII at his coronation, was captured, tried for heresy by a pro-English church court, and burned at Rouen at roughly nineteen. A posthumous retrial annulled the verdict in 1456; she was canonized in 1920. The trial transcripts survive unusually complete, yet the distance between the historical record and her mythic afterlife — martyr, nationalist emblem, feminist icon, far-right mascot — has never closed.
aliases: A Symbol Claimed Across the Political Spectrum
indexTerms: Charles VI of France and Henry V of England (signatories)

title: Johann Sebastian Bach
summary: The German composer who, working most of his life as a church and court musician far from any limelight, brought the art of weaving independent melodic lines into a single structure to one of its highest and most influential summits — and wrote music of such order and depth that it became a foundation of Western art-music training and contrapuntal thought since.
associatedWorks: Toccata and Fugue in D minor, BWV 565
Cantata 'Wachet auf, ruft uns die Stimme', BWV 140
Cantata 'Jesu, der du meine Seele', BWV 78
Chaconne from Partita No. 2 in D minor, BWV 1004
Passacaglia and Fugue in C minor, BWV 582
indexTerms: David, Hans T., and Mendel, Arthur (eds.), rev. Wolff, Christoph

title: Johannes Kepler
summary: The German astronomer and mathematician who, trusting precise measurement over two thousand years of belief in perfect circles, discovered that the planets move in ellipses and wrote the three mathematical laws of planetary motion — turning astronomy into an exact science and laying the foundation Newton would later explain with gravity.

title: John Locke
summary: The English philosopher whose Two Treatises of Government argued that legitimate political authority derives from consent of the governed, that natural rights to life, liberty, and property are inalienable, and that revolution is justified when governments violate those rights — providing the philosophical foundation of liberal democracy and the American Revolution.

title: John Maynard Keynes
summary: The British economist whose General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money (1936) — written in response to the Great Depression — argued that market economies are not self-correcting and that governments must use fiscal policy to stabilize aggregate demand, thereby founding modern macroeconomics, justifying the postwar welfare state, and providing the intellectual framework for economic management in every advanced democracy.
associatedWorks: The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money
The Economic Consequences of the Peace
The Economic Consequences of Mr. Churchill

title: John Rawls
summary: The American philosopher whose A Theory of Justice (1971) revived political philosophy as a serious academic discipline, argued that justice requires principles chosen behind a 'veil of ignorance' that generates equal basic liberties and the difference principle — permitting inequality only when it benefits the least advantaged — and whose framework has been the central reference point for liberal political philosophy for fifty years.

title: John von Neumann
summary: The Hungarian-American polymath who helped invent both the modern computer and the logic of nuclear war — giving quantum mechanics its mathematical foundation, co-founding game theory, contributing the implosion mathematics behind the plutonium bomb, and writing the stored-program design that became the blueprint for nearly every computer since.
associatedWorks: Theory of Games and Economic Behavior
First Draft of a Report on the EDVAC
indexTerms: John von Neumann and Oskar Morgenstern

title: Jomo Kenyatta
summary: Kenya's founding leader, who moved across a long life from anti-colonial intellectual and author of 'Facing Mount Kenya' to a prisoner the British convicted of leading the Mau Mau rebellion in a trial now widely seen as rigged, and finally to the conservative first prime minister and president of an independent Kenya after 1963, preaching 'Harambee' while choosing a pro-Western, capitalist path that kept the country stable but entrenched a largely Kikuyu propertied elite and slid toward one-party dominance.

title: Jōmon Japan
summary: The Jōmon period is the long prehistoric era of the Japanese archipelago, conventionally dated from roughly 14,000 to 300 BCE, though the boundaries vary by region and convention: the northern UNESCO sites are dated to about 13,000–400 BCE, and rice farming reached the southwest well before the north, overlapping with early Yayoi. 'Japan' here is geographic shorthand for many scattered island communities, not a single people. Across those millennia they built settled villages, made some of the world's oldest pottery, and sustained complex societies mainly by hunting, fishing, and gathering, while some also tended groves and lightly cultivated plants.

title: Jorge Luis Borges
summary: Borges directed Argentina’s National Library as blindness overtook him — what he called God’s magnificent irony, eight hundred thousand books and darkness at once. He never wrote a novel, only short fictions of dazzling compression: infinite libraries, forking labyrinths, a cellar sphere where every point in the universe is visible simultaneously. Ficciones (1944) and The Aleph (1949) reshaped what prose fiction could do and made him a key forefather of both magical realism and postmodern metafiction. His acceptance of honors from the 1976 junta is still debated, and many believe it cost him the Nobel Prize he never received.
aliases: The canonization of a difficult writer
associatedWorks: Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote

title: José de San Martín
summary: The professional soldier, trained for two decades in the Spanish army, who returned to South America in 1812 to fight for its independence, led an army across high Andean passes to surprise the Spanish, helped secure the River Plate revolution and liberated Chile with Bernardo O’Higgins, took Lima by sea and declared Peruvian independence as its Protector — and then, after a private 1822 meeting at Guayaquil with his rival Simón Bolívar, resigned his command, withdrew from war and politics entirely, and sailed into a quiet European exile, dying in modest exile in France in 1850, asking nothing for himself.
aliases: The Selfless Father of the Country
A Liberator Within the Old Order

title: Joseon Korea
summary: Joseon Korea (1392–1910) was Korea's longest-lived dynasty and, by many accounts, one of the most thoroughly Neo-Confucian states ever built — a kingdom that engineered its whole society around Neo-Confucian order, gave Korea its own alphabet under King Sejong, survived a catastrophic Japanese invasion, sealed itself off as the 'Hermit Kingdom,' and was finally annexed by imperial Japan.
aliases: Korea as road and then prize

title: Journey to the West
summary: One of the Four Great Classical Novels of China, rooted in the real seventh-century pilgrimage of the monk Xuanzang but transformed over centuries into something else entirely. Its hero is Sun Wukong, the Monkey King — stone-born, master of seventy-two transformations, briefly at war with Heaven itself — tamed and set to guard a timid Buddhist monk on the road west to India. Riotous and funny on the surface, the novel is also a Buddhist allegory of enlightenment. Traditionally attributed to Wu Cheng'en in the sixteenth century, it remains among the most beloved stories across East and Southeast Asia.

title: Julius Caesar
summary: The Roman general and politician who conquered Gaul, crossed the Rubicon to trigger civil war, became dictator of Rome, and was assassinated on the Ides of March — ending the Roman Republic while claiming to restore it, and becoming one of the most potent symbols in Western political history.

title: Julius Nyerere
summary: The founding leader of Tanzania and the most influential theorist-practitioner of African socialism, whose doctrine of ujamaa — 'familyhood' — held that socialism grew from traditional African communal life rather than imported class struggle. He built one of postcolonial Africa's most unified and peaceful nations, promoting Swahili and mass literacy, while presiding over a villagization program and a self-reliance economy that largely failed and left the country dependent on aid. Rare among his peers, he gave up power voluntarily in 1985, and is remembered for personal integrity even as his signature policy is judged a costly mistake.

title: Just War Theory
summary: The tradition in moral and political philosophy that attempts to specify when the resort to war is morally permissible (jus ad bellum) and how war, once begun, must be conducted (jus in bello) — developed through Augustine, Aquinas, Grotius, and Vitoria, and now institutionalized in international humanitarian law, while remaining contested by both pacifists who reject its permissive conclusions and realists who reject its moral constraints.
aliases: Morality Has No Place in War

title: Just-in-Time Manufacturing
summary: Just-in-time manufacturing is a production method that makes and moves goods only as they are needed, holding inventory to a minimum so that parts arrive at each step of assembly just before they are used. Kiichiro Toyoda framed the idea at Toyota in the late 1930s, but the working system was built after the war by Taiichi Ohno and spread across the company's plants by the early 1960s. It reorganized modern industry around the elimination of waste — and, in doing so, made global supply chains both leaner and more exposed to shock.
indexTerms: Womack, James P.; Jones, Daniel T.; Roos, Daniel
Womack, James P.; Jones, Daniel T.

title: Justice
summary: The organizing concept of political and moral philosophy — the framework through which civilizations have argued about what people are owed, what institutions must do, and what wrongs require remedy.
aliases: Justice as the First Virtue of Institutions

title: Kabir
summary: The weaver-poet traditionally remembered as a low-status julaha of Benares, dated to the 15th century, who became the most piercing voice of North India's sant tradition of devotion to a God without form. By tradition raised in a Muslim weaver family in a holy Hindu city, he mocked the externals of both religions in short, blunt, vernacular verses that have been claimed since by Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs alike. He wrote nothing down; his sayings survive in variant, contested streams, including the Sikh scripture, which makes the recovery of the historical Kabir genuinely uncertain.
associatedWorks: Verses in the Guru Granth Sahib

title: Kalidasa
summary: The supreme poet and dramatist of classical Sanskrit, conventionally placed in the Gupta golden age of the fourth and fifth centuries CE, about whose life almost nothing certain is known — yet whose grace of language, beauty of simile, and fusion of human longing with the natural world set the standard for Indian literature ever after, and whose play The Recognition of Shakuntala, in its first European translation, astonished Goethe and the readers of the late eighteenth century.
aliases: The Discovery of an Eastern Classic

title: Kamehameha I
summary: Kamehameha I (c. 1758–1819), known as Kamehameha the Great, was the Hawaiian chief who conquered and unified the islands into a single realm, founding the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi and the dynasty that ruled it for most of the nineteenth century. He is remembered both as a warrior who mastered newly arrived European arms and as a lawgiver whose kingdom outlived him.
associatedWorks: Kānāwai Māmalahoe (Law of the Splintered Paddle)

title: Kanem-Bornu
summary: Kanem-Bornu was the great central-Sudanic empire of the Lake Chad basin — ruled by the Sayfawa dynasty from the medieval period into the nineteenth century, among the longest continuous ruling lines known. It controlled the eastern trans-Saharan routes to Fezzan, Tripoli, and the Nile, adopted Islam early, and shifted its center from Kanem to Bornu under pressure. After the Sayfawa gave way to the al-Kanemi line in the nineteenth century, the Bornu state they left behind persisted into the colonial era.
aliases: The empire as an unbroken royal line
The empire seen across the desert
The empire judged against a new standard
indexTerms: Levtzion, Nehemia and Pouwels, Randall L. (eds.)
Erik Gilbert and Jonathan T. Reynolds
Nehemia Levtzion and Randall L. Pouwels (eds.)

title: Karl Marx
summary: The German philosopher, economist, and revolutionary whose analysis of capitalism as a system of class exploitation — developed in Capital, the Communist Manifesto, and decades of political journalism — became the theoretical foundation of socialist and communist movements worldwide, inspired revolutions that shaped the 20th century, and remains the most systematic critique of capitalism ever produced.

title: Kashmir
summary: A Himalayan valley long called paradise on earth — celebrated for its lakes, gardens, shawls, and a syncretic culture of Hindu, Buddhist, and Sufi learning — that became the unfinished business of the 1947 Partition and one of the most militarized and bitterly disputed places on the planet, claimed in full by both India and Pakistan and partly held by China.
aliases: A Legal Accession, an Integral Part
Claimed by Capitals, Authors of Nothing

title: Kilwa
summary: The island city-state off the coast of present-day Tanzania that became the richest and most powerful of the medieval Swahili coast cities — a Muslim merchant town of coral-stone palaces and mosques that grew wealthy by controlling the export of African gold into the Indian Ocean world, until Portuguese sea power sacked it around 1505 and broke its hold on the trade.
indexTerms: G. S. P. Freeman-Grenville (ed. and trans.)

title: Konark and the Temple Architecture of India
summary: The Hindu temple is built less for congregation than as a built cosmos: a house for a god and a model of the universe carved in stone. Raised by the thousand across India in a shared grammar that split into magnificent regional styles, the greatest of them served as bank, school, landowner, and stage for art and dance as much as a place of worship. Konark, the thirteenth-century Sun Temple in Odisha, conceived as a colossal stone chariot of the sun-god Surya, is the tradition's boldest single statement.

title: Kongo Kingdom
summary: The Kingdom of Kongo was a large, centralized Central African state near the mouth of the Congo River, ruled by the manikongo from the capital Mbanza Kongo. Its kings adopted Christianity of their own will after 1483 and corresponded as equals with Portugal and the Pope, even as the same Atlantic link drew the kingdom into a slave trade that hollowed it out over the following centuries.
aliases: The kingdom as a Christian sovereign power
The kingdom through European Catholic eyes
The kingdom carried across the ocean
indexTerms: Heywood, Linda M. and Thornton, John K.
Linda M. Heywood and John K. Thornton

title: Krakatoa
summary: The 1883 eruption of Krakatoa, a volcanic island in the Sunda Strait between Java and Sumatra, was one of the most violent volcanic events of the modern era. Its climax on 27 August produced what is often called the loudest sound in recorded history and drove tsunamis that drowned tens of thousands of people on the surrounding coasts. Carried within hours by the new global telegraph network, the disaster became arguably the first natural catastrophe the whole world followed in near-real time, and its high-altitude veil of ash and gas cooled the planet slightly and painted vivid red skies around the globe for months.
indexTerms: Simkin, Tom and Fiske, Richard S.
Symons, G. J. (ed.), Royal Society Krakatoa Committee
Self, Stephen and Rampino, Michael R.
Tom Simkin and Richard S. Fiske
G. J. Symons (ed.), Royal Society Krakatoa Committee
John P. Lockwood and Richard W. Hazlett

title: Kublai Khan
summary: Genghis Khan's grandson, who completed the Mongol conquest of China, founded the Yuan dynasty in 1271, and made himself emperor of one of the richest realms on earth -- claiming the title of Great Khan while ruling China as Son of Heaven, a nomad warlord's heir who put on Chinese robes, built a capital where Beijing now stands, opened Eurasia to the trade and travelers of the Mongol peace, and then spent heavily on failed overseas and southern campaigns, especially the Japan invasions of 1274 and 1281, where storms helped wreck the fleets.
aliases: The Defeated Invader and the Divine Wind

title: Kush
summary: The Kingdom of Kush was the great Nubian power of the middle Nile, centered first at Napata and later at Meroë. It twice built an empire: its kings conquered and ruled Egypt as the 25th Dynasty around 744–656 BCE, and after losing Egypt it flourished for centuries as an African state of iron, pyramids, ruling queens, and its own Meroitic script — a writing system that can be sounded out but whose language is still poorly understood — before declining around the fourth century CE.
aliases: Kush as seen from the north
Kush as a wonder on the edge of the world
Kush in its own unreadable words
indexTerms: Welsby, Derek A. and Anderson, Julie R. (eds.)

title: Kwame Nkrumah
summary: The founding leader of Ghana and the foremost champion of Pan-Africanism, who led the first Black African colony south of the Sahara to independence in 1957 and tried to convert that breakthrough into a continent-wide project of African unity. A principal founder of the Organization of African Unity and the theorist who named neo-colonialism, he built a one-party state under his own personality cult, was overthrown by the army and police in 1966, and died in exile in 1972, leaving a divided legacy as both patron saint of African liberation and cautionary tale of concentrated power.
associatedWorks: Neo-Colonialism: The Last Stage of Imperialism
Ghana: The Autobiography of Kwame Nkrumah

title: Kyoto
summary: The thousand-year imperial capital of Japan, laid out on a Chinese grid in 794 as Heian-kyo to seat the emperor's court, which became the heart of Japanese art, religion, and refinement — and which was spared large-scale firebombing and the atomic bomb during the Second World War, surviving as the living museum of classical Japan.
aliases: The Keeper of the Old Country
indexTerms: Nicolas Fiévé and Paul Waley, eds.

title: Labor Unions
summary: Organizations through which workers combine to bargain collectively with employers, converting the powerlessness of the individual worker into shared strength. From early friendly societies and craft guilds through the industrial unions of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries to a contested present, unions have used collective bargaining, the strike threat, and political action to shape wages and conditions — while their economic and social effects remain genuinely debated.
indexTerms: Freeman, Richard B.; Medoff, James L.
Richard B. Freeman and James L. Medoff

title: Lake Victoria
summary: Africa's largest lake and the world's largest tropical lake, shared by Uganda, Kenya, and Tanzania — the long-sought source of the White Nile, a prize in the Victorian scramble for Africa, the cradle of one of evolution's most extraordinary bursts of speciation, and the scene of one of the largest human-caused extinctions of recent times.

title: Lalibela
summary: Eleven medieval churches in the Ethiopian highlands, carved downward into solid volcanic rock — some as free-standing monoliths, others still attached to the cliff — so the finished churches sit at or below ground level, linked by trenches and tunnels. Traditionally credited to King Lalibela of the Zagwe dynasty around 1200, though the complex likely grew in phases, they were conceived as a 'New Jerusalem' after the holy city fell to Saladin. They remain a living center of Ethiopian Orthodox pilgrimage rather than a ruin.
aliases: The City the Angels Helped Build
A Monument That Will Not Hold Still

title: Language
summary: The human capacity for symbolic communication — the system of sounds, signs, or gestures through which meaning is created, shared, and transmitted — the foundation on which every other human cultural achievement rests, and one of the most contested subjects in cognitive science, linguistics, anthropology, and philosophy.

title: Laozi
summary: The shadowy 'Old Master' traditionally credited with founding Daoism and writing the Dao De Jing, whose historical existence many scholars now doubt — likely a legendary anchor, a title, or a composite figure retrofitted to a text assembled by many hands, which is strangely fitting for the supposed prophet of the nameless and the unforced.
aliases: The Founder That May Not Be

title: Law
summary: The system of rules that a political community recognizes as binding on its members — the mechanism through which states establish authority, protect rights, resolve disputes, and constitute themselves as political communities; and the contested terrain on which every fundamental question about justice, power, and human freedom is ultimately fought out
aliases: Common Law and the Gradual Development of Rights
Legal Transplantation and the Disruption of Customary Law
indexTerms: Rafael La Porta, Florencio Lopez-de-Silanes, Andrei Shleifer, and Robert Vishny
Barons of England and King John

title: Lead
summary: The soft, heavy metal that humanity wove into daily life across six millennia — in Roman water pipes, pewter, paint, solder, and gasoline — before reckoning with the fact that it is a cumulative brain poison with no safe dose, especially for children, and that the slow acknowledgment of that fact cost generations their cognitive potential.
indexTerms: Naomi Oreskes and Erik M. Conway

title: Legalism
summary: The cold, hard-headed Chinese theory of statecraft -- the school of "methods and law" (Fa-jia) that held people are selfish, that a ruler's virtue cannot govern them, and that order comes only from clear law, ruthless reward and punishment, and an all-powerful state. It built the first unified Chinese empire under the Qin, was disgraced when that empire collapsed, and then survived as an administrative skeleton many later "Confucian" dynasties continued to use in altered form.

title: Legitimacy
summary: Legitimacy is the quality that makes political authority feel entitled to obedience rather than merely capable of demanding it. Most people, most of the time, comply with authority without being directly coerced. Understanding why — and what withdraws that compliance — is the central problem of political order.
aliases: Legitimacy as habit and social norm
The legitimacy deficit of inherited institutions

title: Leo Tolstoy
summary: Count, soldier, and supreme realist novelist, Leo Tolstoy (1828–1910) produced War and Peace and Anna Karenina — two novels of epic scale and piercing psychological depth that many regard as the greatest of the nineteenth century. In midlife he underwent a spiritual crisis and emerged a radical Christian moralist who preached nonviolence, rejected the Orthodox Church, and came to view his own fiction with suspicion. The Church excommunicated him in 1901. His doctrine of nonviolent resistance directly shaped Mohandas Gandhi. He died in 1910 at a remote railway station, a fugitive from his own home.
aliases: The count who could not renounce
associatedWorks: The Kingdom of God Is Within You

title: Leon Trotsky
summary: The Russian revolutionary, theorist of permanent revolution, organizer of the Red Army, and founder of the Fourth International — expelled by Stalin, exiled across three continents, and assassinated in Mexico in 1940 — and the most potent symbol of the Stalinist betrayal of the Russian Revolution.
associatedWorks: The History of the Russian Revolution

title: Leonardo da Vinci
summary: The Renaissance painter, anatomist, and engineer who refused to separate art from science

title: Leonhard Euler
summary: The most prolific mathematician who ever lived — a Swiss master who worked at the academies of St Petersburg and Berlin and produced so much that roughly half of it came after he went nearly blind, dictated from memory to his sons while he held elaborate calculations in his head. He standardized much of the notation students still use: f(x), e, i, the summation sign, and the modern symbol for pi. He solved the seven bridges of Königsberg and opened graph theory, ranged across analysis, number theory, mechanics, and astronomy, and remained so productive that publication of his collected works continued for decades after his death in 1783.
aliases: The Scholar of the Royal Courts
indexTerms: Boyer, Carl B. (rev. Uta C. Merzbach)
Bradley, Robert E., and C. Edward Sandifer (eds.)
Robert E. Bradley and C. Edward Sandifer (eds.)

title: Léopold Sédar Senghor
summary: The Senegalese poet, philosopher, and statesman who co-founded the Négritude movement in 1930s Paris and then became the first president of independent Senegal, governing from 1960 to 1980 before voluntarily stepping down — a rare act among the leaders of his generation. He fused African cultural pride with French humanism, built a stable but France-aligned and largely one-party-dominant state, and in 1983 became the first African elected to the Académie française, even as later thinkers attacked his celebration of an African 'emotion' against Greek 'reason' as essentialism and his politics as too tied to the former colonizer.
aliases: The Poet-President Who Reconciled Two Worlds
The Bridge Built from the Colonizer's Bank
associatedWorks: Anthologie de la nouvelle poésie nègre et malgache

title: Li Bai
summary: Li Bai (701–762), also written Li Po, is a poet of the Chinese Tang dynasty, honored since his own century as the shixian, the Immortal of Poetry. Roughly a thousand poems survive under his name, built from wine, moonlight, mountain travel, parting friends, and a Daoist appetite for freedom. He is conventionally paired with his friend Du Fu as one of the two greatest poets in the Chinese tradition.
associatedWorks: The Road to Shu Is Hard

title: Liangzhu Culture
summary: The Liangzhu culture is a Late Neolithic society of the lower Yangtze River in eastern China, flourishing from about 3300 to 2300 BCE around a large walled city near modern Hangzhou. It is best known for its finely carved ritual jades and as a highly stratified, urbanized polity often described as one of the earliest states in East Asia. Its inscription as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2019 was widely presented as proof that Chinese civilization is five thousand years old.
aliases: The proof of five thousand years
Read the mud and the caves

title: Liberalism
summary: The political philosophy that places individual liberty, limited government, the rule of law, and equal rights at the center of political life — the foundational ideology of Western constitutional democracy, the intellectual tradition that produced both the American and French revolutions, and the framework within which the most consequential political arguments of the modern era have been conducted, even by those who reject it.

title: Liberation Theology
summary: The Latin American movement, born among Catholic theologians, clergy, and poor Christian communities, that read the Gospel as a call to liberate the oppressed from unjust structures, drew on Marxist social analysis, produced martyrs under military regimes, and was disciplined by the Vatican before its core idea entered mainstream Church teaching.
indexTerms: Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith (Joseph Ratzinger)

title: Liberty
summary: Liberty, or freedom, is the most stirring word in politics and the most slippery — a single word that every side claims and no two sides define alike, splitting into rival ideals (freedom from interference, freedom to govern oneself, freedom from domination) that often pull against one another, so that the same word can arm the rebel and the slaveholder, the tax-cutter and the revolutionary.
aliases: Liberty as the Power to Act

title: Logistics
summary: The discipline of moving and storing things to the right place at the right time — the management of flow that has decided campaigns, built empires, and made the modern economy work. Logistics is infrastructure's invisible twin: while engineers build the roads and ports, logisticians work out what moves through them and when. Mastering it has determined the outcome of wars and the fortunes of companies; failing at it has brought armies and supply chains alike to ruin.
indexTerms: Womack, James P., Jones, Daniel T., and Roos, Daniel
Coyle, John J., Langley, C. John, Novack, Robert A., and Gibson, Brian J.

title: London
summary: The tidal-river port the Romans founded as Londinium around 47 CE, which grew into the capital of the largest empire in history and one of the great engines of modern finance, law, the English language, and literature — a metropolis whose splendor was built on industry and on the profits of empire and the Atlantic slave trade, and which survived the loss of both to remake itself as a global financial center and one of the world's most diverse cities.
aliases: The Capital That Ran the World
indexTerms: Hall, Catherine; Draper, Nicholas; McClelland, Keith; Donington, Katie; Lang, Rachel

title: Louis Pasteur
summary: French chemist turned microbiologist, Pasteur showed that fermentation, spoilage, and disease are driven by living microorganisms. His swan-neck-flask experiments helped disprove spontaneous generation, and he established pasteurization — gentle heat that kills spoilage microbes — still applied to milk worldwide. Working alongside Robert Koch and often in rivalry with him, he helped cement the germ theory of disease, then developed vaccines for chicken cholera, anthrax, and rabies, the last tested publicly when nine-year-old Joseph Meister survived a bite in 1885. Gerald Geison's 1995 study of his laboratory notebooks revealed gaps between the public and private Pasteur.
aliases: The Founder of a New Science
associatedWorks: Mémoire sur la fermentation appelée lactique

title: Ludwig van Beethoven
summary: The German composer who bridged the Classical and Romantic eras, producing nine symphonies, sixteen string quartets, thirty-two piano sonatas, and a body of work that reshaped what music was thought capable of expressing — and who, in going deaf at the height of his powers, became the defining emblem of the artist-genius creating in defiance of circumstance.
associatedWorks: Symphony No. 3 in E-flat major, Op. 55 (Eroica)
Symphony No. 5 in C minor, Op. 67
Symphony No. 6 in F major, Op. 68 (Pastoral)
Symphony No. 9 in D minor, Op. 125 (Choral)
Piano Sonata No. 14 in C-sharp minor, Op. 27, No. 2 (Moonlight)
Piano Sonata No. 23 in F minor, Op. 57 (Appassionata)
String Quartet No. 14 in C-sharp minor, Op. 131
String Quartet No. 15 in A minor, Op. 132
String Quartet No. 16 in F major, Op. 135
Piano Concerto No. 5 in E-flat major, Op. 73 (Emperor)
Violin Concerto in D major, Op. 61
Missa Solemnis in D major, Op. 123
Piano Sonata No. 29 in B-flat major, Op. 106 (Hammerklavier)

title: Machu Picchu
summary: The fifteenth-century Inca royal estate perched on a ridge above the Urubamba River in Peru — almost certainly built for the emperor Pachacuti, abandoned near the time of the Spanish conquest, and never truly lost, since local Andean farmers always knew it was there. It became the world's most famous image of the Inca only after an American explorer arrived in 1911, guided by local people, and mistook it for a vanished city, launching a legend that mass tourism and popular memory turned into the universal symbol of a civilization.
aliases: A Place That Was Never Lost
A Discovery That Reshaped the Story
A National Symbol and Its Tensions
A Recognizable Site, Not a Mystery
indexTerms: Burger, Richard L. and Lucy C. Salazar (eds.)
Richard L. Burger and Lucy C. Salazar (eds.)

title: Madagascar
summary: The fourth largest island on Earth, separated from other landmasses for tens of millions of years and settled by humans only about 1,300–1,500 years ago — by Austronesian seafarers from Southeast Asia and Bantu-speaking peoples from East Africa — making it both an extraordinary laboratory of isolated evolution and the destination of one of history's most astonishing long-distance migrations, now facing severe deforestation and extinction.
indexTerms: Steven M. Goodman and Jonathan P. Benstead (eds.)
David Burney and Timothy F. Flannery

title: Magical Realism
summary: The literary mode -- above all of 20th-century Latin America -- in which ghosts, miracles, and impossible wonders are narrated in the same calm, matter-of-fact voice as the price of bread, so that the marvelous and the everyday become one fabric. It let a continent tell its own outsized, violent, dreamlike history in a register plain realism could not hold, crowned by Gabriel Garcia Marquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967), and it became one of the most imitated and most contested modes in modern fiction.
indexTerms: Lois Parkinson Zamora and Wendy B. Faris (eds.)

title: Magna Carta
summary: The 1215 charter wrested from King John by English barons at Runnymede — annulled within weeks but reissued repeatedly, and eventually read as the founding text of the principle that the ruler is bound by law. Magna Carta's actual clauses mostly addressed narrow feudal grievances; its civilizational significance lies in the later centuries that reimagined it as the foundation of due process, constitutional government, and the rule of law.
aliases: A Feudal Bargain, Not a Constitutional Moment
Ancient Liberty and the Common Law

title: Mahatma Gandhi
summary: The Indian lawyer and independence leader who developed satyagraha — nonviolent resistance — into the most influential political method of the 20th century, led India's independence movement against British rule, and shaped every subsequent movement for civil rights and liberation from King to Mandela.

title: Maimonides
summary: The preeminent medieval Jewish philosopher, legal scholar, and physician, who twice reorganized his tradition from inside the Islamic world: once by gathering the whole of Jewish law into a single clear code, the Mishneh Torah, and once by trying to square Aristotle's philosophy with the Hebrew Bible in The Guide for the Perplexed, a project so bold it provoked book-burnings among his own people even as it made him, in the tradition's own phrase, the one of whom 'from Moses to Moses there was none like Moses.'
associatedWorks: Commentary on the Mishnah (including the Thirteen Principles of Faith)

title: Maize
summary: The giant grass domesticated from the wild plant teosinte by Indigenous Mesoamerican farmers thousands of years ago — and transformed so completely it cannot reproduce without human help — maize became the sacred staple of Maya and Aztec civilization, spread after 1492 to fuel population booms across Africa, China, and Europe, and now underpins the industrial food system as the world's most-produced grain by volume.
aliases: The People Who Made the Plant

title: Majapahit
summary: Majapahit (c. 1293–1527) was the last major Hindu-Buddhist empire of the Indonesian archipelago, and one of its most powerful — a Javanese sea-power that, at its fourteenth-century height under King Hayam Wuruk and his minister Gajah Mada, claimed influence over ports and islands across maritime Southeast Asia. The entry's purpose is to explain what kind of empire a Javanese thalassocracy was, why its reported extent must be read with care, and how a vanished Hindu kingdom became modern Indonesia's imagined ancestor.
aliases: The hegemon seen from the harbor
indexTerms: Miksic, John N., and Geok Yian Goh
John N. Miksic and Geok Yian Goh

title: Manifest Destiny
summary: The nineteenth-century American belief that the United States was divinely ordained and naturally destined to expand across the North American continent. A blend of religious providentialism, racial hierarchy, and republican mission, it supplied the moral cover for the conquest of Indigenous and Mexican land, recasting the seizure of a continent as the inevitable and benevolent spread of liberty and civilization rather than as a series of deliberate and violent political choices.

title: Mansa Musa
summary: Ruler of the Mali Empire whose 1324 pilgrimage to Mecca, lavish distribution of gold in Cairo, and patronage of mosques and scholars at Timbuktu made him one of the medieval Islamic world's most famous African sovereigns — and, through his portrait on the 1375 Catalan Atlas, the figure who fixed an image of West African wealth in the European imagination for centuries.
aliases: The visitor who broke the gold market
The king who became the face of Africa
The ruler the griots barely sing

title: Mao Zedong
summary: The founder of the People's Republic of China, theorist of peasant revolution, and leader of the Chinese Communist Party — whose decades of rule produced land reform, industrialization, and national unification alongside the Great Leap Forward and the Cultural Revolution, two catastrophes that killed tens of millions.
indexTerms: Ashton, Basil; Hill, Kenneth; Piazza, Alan; Zeitz, Robin

title: Maps
summary: The technology of representing spatial relationships on a surface — one of the oldest cognitive and political tools in human history, a technology that does not merely describe territory but creates it: setting borders, asserting sovereignty, enabling navigation, organizing colonial space, and shaping how people understand their place in the world.

title: Marcus Garvey
summary: A Jamaican printer with a gift for oratory, Marcus Garvey built in the 1910s and 1920s what is widely regarded as the largest organized Black mass movement in history — chapters from Harlem to Lagos, a newspaper crossing oceans. His message: Africa's scattered people were one, with a proud past and a future to seize. He founded the Black Star Line to link the diaspora. The ships were bought badly, the money ran out, and a contested 1923 mail fraud conviction led to deportation. He died in London in 1940; his pride ran into African independence and the Rastafari faith.
aliases: The Man Who Gave Us Pride
associatedWorks: Philosophy and Opinions of Marcus Garvey

title: Marie Curie
summary: The Polish-born physicist and chemist who, with Pierre Curie, isolated polonium and radium, named the phenomenon of radioactivity, and demonstrated that it was an atomic property rather than a chemical reaction — twice awarded the Nobel Prize (Physics 1903, Chemistry 1911), the only person to win in two different sciences, while facing systematic exclusion from French scientific institutions and the gendered mythology that has obscured the rigor and ambition of her work.
aliases: The Exile Who Did Not Forget
The Chemist Who Started a Revolution

title: Mark Twain
summary: Born Samuel Clemens in 1835 — printer, river pilot, miner — Mark Twain made the plain, regional speech of ordinary Americans into literature. His masterpiece, Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885), follows a poor white boy and Jim, an escaped enslaved man, down the Mississippi; widely read as a profound anti-slavery satire, it is also among the most challenged books in American schools. Often called the father of American literature, Twain's humor carried a blade. He turned bitterly anti-imperialist in his later years and died in 1910 under Halley's Comet.
aliases: The book that wounds as it teaches
associatedWorks: A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court
The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County

title: Maroons
summary: The people across the Americas who escaped slavery — and the descendants born free alongside them — who built free, self-governing communities in forests, mountains, and swamps, from Brazil to Jamaica to Suriname to the United States. Across more than three centuries, marronage was the longest-running campaign of resistance to slavery in the hemisphere: freedom not hoped for but seized, defended by force, and sometimes wrested into formal treaties, a few of which bound the maroons to return future runaways.
indexTerms: Reis, João José; Gomes, Flávio dos Santos (eds.)
João José Reis and Flávio dos Santos Gomes (editors)

title: Martin Luther
summary: German friar and theology professor whose 1517 protest against the sale of indulgences sparked the Protestant Reformation — built on three ideas: that salvation comes through faith alone, that scripture alone holds authority, and that every believer stands directly before God — and whose legacy includes both a revolution in religious liberty and a record of virulent antisemitism that the Nazis later cited approvingly.
associatedWorks: Address to the Christian Nobility of the German Nation
On the Babylonian Captivity of the Church
On the Freedom of a Christian
On the Bondage of the Will
On the Jews and Their Lies

title: Martin Luther King Jr.
summary: The Baptist minister and civil rights leader who applied Gandhian nonviolent resistance to American racial segregation, led the Montgomery Bus Boycott and the March on Washington, and was assassinated in 1968 — leaving behind the most important speech in American political oratory and an unfinished revolution whose legacy is still contested.
associatedWorks: Where Do We Go from Here: Chaos or Community?
indexTerms: Martin Luther King Jr. (ed. Cornel West)

title: Mary Wollstonecraft
summary: English writer and philosopher who made the foundational argument for women's rational equality in A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792), challenging the Enlightenment's failure to extend its own principles to half the human race — and whose unconventional life became as contested as her ideas.
associatedWorks: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
A Vindication of the Rights of Men
Letters Written During a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark

title: Mass Incarceration
summary: From the early 1970s, the United States prison and jail population rose roughly fivefold — one of the highest incarceration rates on earth, with stark racial disparities. The scale is undisputed. What serious scholars argue over is causation: how much the rise reflects a genuine surge in crime, how much it reflects deliberate policy choices such as the War on Drugs and mandatory minimums, and whether racial gaps stem from enforcement patterns or other factors. Both causes and remedies remain fiercely contested.
aliases: The Protection the State Long Denied
indexTerms: National Research Council (Jeremy Travis, Bruce Western, and Steve Redburn, eds.)

title: Mathematical Notation
summary: The system of symbols — numerals, operators, variables, and logical signs — through which mathematics is written, communicated, and extended: not mathematics itself, but the technology that made mathematics scalable, shareable, and capable of reasoning beyond what any individual mind could hold.

title: Matsuo Bashō
summary: Matsuo Bashō (1644–1694) was the Japanese poet who transformed and deepened an already sophisticated haikai tradition, lifting the hokku — the seventeen-sound-unit opening verse later called haiku — into a serious art of nature, season, and quiet attention. Working in the long peace of early Edo Japan, he gave the tiny form real depth, trained an influential school of followers in his shōfū style, and left travel diaries, above all Oku no Hosomichi, that braided prose and verse into a new kind of book.
associatedWorks: Oku no Hosomichi (The Narrow Road to the Deep North)
Nozarashi Kikō (Journal of a Weather-Beaten Skeleton)

title: Mauritius
summary: A small volcanic island in the western Indian Ocean with no native human population until the seventeenth century — where the Dutch, French, and British successively built a sugar-plantation colony worked by enslaved Africans and then by indentured laborers from India, producing in a few generations an entirely new multi-ethnic society from scratch. Mauritius was also, through the fate of the dodo, one of the first famous, well-documented lessons that human arrival could drive a species to extinction.

title: Max Weber
summary: The German sociologist who invented the tools — ideal types, verstehen, the disenchantment of the world, the Protestant ethic — through which the modern social sciences understand rationalization, bureaucracy, and the relationship between ideas and economic life.
associatedWorks: The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism
The Methodology of the Social Sciences
indexTerms: Becker, Sascha O., and Ludger Woessmann

title: Maya Civilization
summary: The civilization that flourished across southern Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, and El Salvador for over three millennia — producing the Americas' only fully developed writing system, advanced mathematics including independent invention of zero, a sophisticated astronomical calendar, and monumental architecture. The Maya were never a single empire but a network of competing city-states whose Classic period peaked between 250 and 900 CE before a still-debated collapse that remains one of archaeology's central problems.
aliases: The Maya as a Case Study in Decentralized Complexity
Reading Hieroglyphs: What It Revealed and What It Left Out

title: Maya Script
summary: The most fully developed writing system of the pre-Columbian Americas, and the only one known to record connected speech in full. Maya script mixes whole-word signs with phonetic syllable signs, recording the dynastic history, ritual, astronomy, and calendar of the Maya — a body of knowledge nearly erased by Spanish book-burning, then painstakingly read again in the twentieth century.
aliases: The Friar Who Burned and Recorded
indexTerms: Michael D. Coe and Mark Van Stone
Stephen Houston, Oswaldo Chinchilla Mazariegos and David Stuart

title: McCarthyism and the Red Scare
summary: McCarthyism, also called the Second Red Scare, was the wave of anticommunist fear, investigation, and persecution that gripped the United States from roughly 1947 to 1957. Named for Senator Joseph McCarthy of Wisconsin, it reached far beyond him: loyalty boards, congressional committees, and the FBI hunted suspected Communists in government, Hollywood, schools, and unions, costing thousands their jobs and reputations while exposing almost no actual spies.
indexTerms: John Earl Haynes and Harvey Klehr

title: Measurement
summary: The technology of assigning numbers to properties of the world — one of the oldest and most consequential cognitive tools in human history, the foundation of science, commerce, and administration, and a technology whose apparent neutrality conceals the power embedded in who measures, what gets measured, and how the results are used.
indexTerms: Stiglitz, Joseph; Sen, Amartya; Fitoussi, Jean-Paul
Angwin, Julia; Larson, Jeff; Mattu, Surya; Kirchner, Lauren

title: Mecca
summary: The holiest city in Islam, birthplace of the Prophet Muhammad, and site of the Kaaba — the point toward which 1.8 billion Muslims orient their daily prayers and to which they aspire to make the Hajj pilgrimage at least once in their lives.

title: Medieval European Society
summary: The social, economic, and cultural order that developed in Europe after the fall of the Western Roman Empire — organized around feudalism, the Catholic Church, and the manorial economy — before being transformed by the Black Death, the Renaissance, the printing press, and the Reformation into the early modern world.

title: Mehmed II and the Fall of Constantinople
summary: The Ottoman capture of Constantinople on May 29, 1453, by Sultan Mehmed II — the conquest that ended the Eastern Roman, or Byzantine, Empire after more than a thousand years centered on Constantinople, made the city the seat of a new Islamic empire, and was read across Christian Europe as the close of one age and the start of another.
indexTerms: Nicolle, David; Haldon, John; Turnbull, Stephen

title: Melanesia
summary: Melanesia is the name for the arc of Pacific islands running from New Guinea through the Bismarck Archipelago, the Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, and New Caledonia to Fiji — a region of extraordinary linguistic diversity settled by humans for some fifty thousand years. It is also a nineteenth-century European racial category, coined for the dark skin of the islanders it grouped together, that many modern Melanesians have both criticized and reclaimed.
aliases: A Name Turned Into a Tool
A Natural Record, Not a Racial One
indexTerms: John Edward Terrell, Terry L. Hunt, and Chris Gosden
Jack Golson, Tim Denham, Philip Hughes, Pamela Swadling, and John Muke, eds.

title: Memory and Commemoration
summary: The field of inquiry examining how societies selectively remember and represent their pasts — through monuments, anniversaries, museums, and rituals — and the political, psychological, and cultural processes that determine what is preserved, what is forgotten, and whose experiences are rendered visible or invisible in public memory.

title: Mencius
summary: The Confucian thinker who argued that human nature is good — the great second teacher of the tradition, who built Confucius's ethics into a bold philosophy of innate goodness and drew from it a doctrine that a ruler who turns tyrant forfeits the right to rule and may be removed.

title: Menelik II
summary: Emperor of Ethiopia who did what no other African ruler of his age managed, crushing an invading Italian army at Adwa in 1896 and keeping Ethiopia free — with Liberia, one of only two African states to escape the European Scramble, and the only one to do so by defeating a European invasion outright, while at the same time he roughly doubled his empire through southern and western conquests that brought modernization to the center and dispossession, killing, enslavement, and famine to the Oromo, Sidama, and other peoples he absorbed, making him at once the great African anti-colonial victor and a conquering emperor in his own right.
aliases: The unifier who saved the nation
The conqueror who took the south
The proof that empire could be beaten

title: Meritocracy
summary: The ideal that positions and rewards should go to the most talented and hard-working, regardless of birth. The word was coined in its modern political sense in 1958 by the British sociologist Michael Young as a warning — a satire of a society where a new elite, selected by exam and credential, had come to believe it deserved its advantages. That origin is now largely forgotten. Critics argue that meritocracy does not replace hierarchy; it makes the winners feel they earned it and the losers feel they deserve their fate.

title: Mesa Verde
summary: The most famous cliff dwellings in North America: stone villages built into the canyon alcoves of a high forested mesa in southwestern Colorado, raised by Ancestral Puebloan builders in the 1200s and lived in for only a few generations before the whole region emptied. Found and stripped of its contents in the 1880s, then made the first U.S. national park created to protect human-built heritage in 1906, Mesa Verde remains an ancestral place for the living Pueblo nations who descend from its builders.
aliases: An Ancestral Place, Not a Lost World
A Site Read From Stone and Tree Rings
indexTerms: Linda S. Cordell and Maxine E. McBrinn

title: Mesopotamia
summary: The river valley system between the Tigris and Euphrates where urban civilization, writing, law, and empire were invented — and the 'cradle of civilization' framing that tells us as much about the civilizations that use it as about Mesopotamia itself

title: Michael Faraday
summary: The largely self-taught English experimentalist, born poor and apprenticed as a bookbinder, who discovered electromagnetic induction — the principle behind every electric generator and transformer — built the first primitive electric motor, found the laws of electrolysis linking electricity and chemistry, and gave physics the revolutionary idea of the field. Lacking advanced mathematics, Faraday pictured electric and magnetic forces as invisible lines of force filling space; James Clerk Maxwell later turned that picture into rigorous mathematics. His experiments laid the foundation of the electric age, and the field concept he introduced ran through Maxwell to Einstein, who reportedly kept a portrait of Faraday on his wall.
aliases: The Engine of the Electric Age
The Bookbinder Who Had No Mathematics
associatedWorks: The Chemical History of a Candle
Experimental Researches in Chemistry and Physics

title: Michel Foucault
summary: Michel Foucault (1926–1984) was a French philosopher and historian of ideas. He wrote pathbreaking histories of madness, medicine, punishment, and sexuality, arguing that what a society treats as knowledge is bound up with how it exercises power. His books, among them The Order of Things, Discipline and Punish, and The History of Sexuality, made him one of the most influential and most contested thinkers of the late twentieth century.
indexTerms: Dreyfus, Hubert L. and Rabinow, Paul

title: Michelangelo
summary: The Florentine sculptor, painter, and architect whose David, Pietà, and Sistine Chapel ceiling set a standard of human grandeur the West has measured itself against ever since — one of the supreme artists of the Italian Renaissance, who thought of himself first as a man who freed living figures from stone.

title: Micronesia
summary: Micronesia is a region of thousands of small islands scattered across the western Pacific north of the equator — the Mariana, Caroline, and Marshall archipelagoes, along with Palau, Nauru, and the Gilbert Islands of Kiribati. It is also a nineteenth-century European label, meaning 'small islands,' that gathered hundreds of distinct communities under one name coined from outside. The region holds both a deep human past and a colonial classification that its own people still argue over.
aliases: The Ocean as Home, Not Emptiness

title: Migration
summary: The movement of people across space in search of better conditions — the most ancient and most consequential human behavior after reproduction itself, the process through which Homo sapiens populated the entire planet, built every civilization, and continues to shape the demographics and politics of every country on earth — and the subject of the most consequential political conflicts of the contemporary world.
indexTerms: Clement, Viviane; Rigaud, Kanta Kumari; et al. (World Bank)

title: Miguel de Cervantes
summary: Soldier, slave, debtor, tax collector — Cervantes (1547–1616) lived the biography before writing the book. The wound at Lepanto crippled his left hand for life; five years enslaved in Algiers ended only when his family raised the ransom. Late and poor, he wrote Don Quixote (1605, 1615) — the story of an aging gentleman undone by chivalric romances, very widely called the first modern novel for its sustained irony, unreliable narrator, psychological depth, and self-conscious fictionality. He died in 1616, the same year as Shakespeare. Spanish is still called the language of Cervantes.
associatedWorks: Los trabajos de Persiles y Sigismunda

title: Miriam Makeba
summary: The South African singer known as Mama Africa, who carried Xhosa and South African song to the world and became the most famous voice of the anti-apartheid struggle in exile. Hits like Pata Pata and the Click Song made South African sound global, and her testimony against apartheid cost her thirty years of banishment from her own country. A Grammy-winning artist whose warmth on stage never softened her insistence on speaking the truth, she traced the whole arc of the struggle, from exile in 1960 to her return after Mandela's release.

title: Moctezuma II
summary: The ninth Aztec emperor, who ruled at the empire’s height and was overwhelmed by the Spanish arrival. History remembered him as the coward who gave away an empire, but that verdict was largely written by the people who took it. From around 1502 he expanded tribute and deepened the gulf between nobles and commoners, building resentments the Spanish later exploited. When Cortés arrived in 1519, Moctezuma admitted the strangers to Tenochtitlan and was soon held captive, dying in 1520 in disputed circumstances. Scholars now read his response not as fatalism but as a calculated, if failed, attempt to manage a threat no ruler had ever faced.

title: Modern Art
summary: Roughly a century of successive avant-garde movements, from Impressionism in the 1860s to Abstract Expressionism in the 1960s, in which Western artists abandoned realistic representation and reinvented what a picture could be. Partly freed by photography from the task of recording appearances, they turned to color, form, emotion, and idea. The sequence ran through Post-Impressionism, Cubism, Dada, Surrealism, and pure abstraction, provoking a lasting popular backlash — my child could do that — while permanently remaking design, advertising, and the modern eye. The gap between adventurous art and a wary public has never fully closed.
aliases: The Long March to Pure Painting
The Art of a Shaken World

title: Mogadishu
summary: The great port on the Horn of Africa, in present-day Somalia, that was for centuries one of the oldest and richest Muslim ports of the Benadir coast and the wider western Indian Ocean trading world — a cosmopolitan Muslim center of commerce and learning, famous above all for its exported cloth, whose medieval golden age has been all but erased from popular memory by a later history of war.
aliases: The Stone City of the Coast
indexTerms: Ibn Battuta (translated by H. A. R. Gibb)
Erik Gilbert and Jonathan T. Reynolds (Africa in World History)

title: Money
summary: The technology of exchange — from grain tallies in Mesopotamian temples to coined metal in Greek city-states to global digital credit — whose most consequential property is not what it buys but what it does to the relationships, values, and institutions of every civilization that uses it
aliases: The Global Currency Nobody Taught You About
indexTerms: Edward L. Deci and Richard M. Ryan

title: Monotheism
summary: The belief in a single, all-powerful God who created the universe and demands exclusive worship — the founding theological claim of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam that shaped the religious, philosophical, and political history of half the world, and whose logic of exclusive truth generated both civilization and persecution.

title: Moscow
summary: The fortress city on the Moskva river that rose under Mongol overlordship to gather the Russian lands around its Kremlin, claimed for itself the title of a third Rome and last seat of Orthodox Christianity, burned to deny Napoleon his victory, and became the capital of the Soviet Union and the command center of its terror.
aliases: The Unbroken Heart of the Nation

title: Mosquitoes
summary: The insect that has killed more humans than any other animal — and that shaped the geography of empire, the patterns of the Atlantic slave trade, and the limits of colonial expansion through differential immunity to the diseases it carries
aliases: The Differential That Built a Labor System
indexTerms: John Luke Gallup and Jeffrey D. Sachs

title: Mughal Miniature Painting
summary: The court painting of the Mughal Empire: small, intensely detailed images in opaque watercolour and gold on paper, made in the imperial workshop to illustrate histories, epics, and memoirs or to fill albums. Born when the emperor Humayun brought Persian masters to India and Akbar set them to train a large workshop of mostly Indian painters, many of them Hindu, it fused Persian elegance, Indian colour and vigour, and, increasingly, European devices into one of the supreme schools of painting. It flowered under Akbar, refined under Jahangir and Shah Jahan, and dispersed to regional courts as imperial patronage faded.
aliases: The empire as it wished to be seen
The mostly Hindu hands behind the throne
indexTerms: Caron Smith and Sonya Rhie Quintanilla
J. P. Losty and Malini Roy

title: Muhammad
summary: The Prophet of Islam — born in Mecca around 570 CE, a merchant who received revelations he identified as the word of God, who united the Arabian Peninsula under a new monotheism, and whose life, teaching, and example became the foundation of a civilization that at its height stretched from Spain to Central Asia and remains a living force for 1.8 billion people — and a figure whose historical reconstruction is entangled with theological commitment in ways that make him one of the most studied and most contested persons in history.

title: Muhammad Ali Jinnah
summary: The Karachi-born, London-trained barrister who began his career as a champion of Hindu-Muslim unity and ended it as the founder of Pakistan, the first modern state created expressly as a homeland for Muslims — winning it not through mass agitation but through constitutional argument and the two-nation theory, and serving barely a year as its first governor-general before tuberculosis killed him in 1948, leaving the question of whether he wanted a secular state or a religious one to be fought over ever since.
associatedWorks: Presidential Address to the Constituent Assembly of Pakistan (11 August 1947)

title: Murasaki Shikibu
summary: The Japanese court lady, known only by a nickname, who in the early eleventh century wrote The Tale of Genji, a vast prose narrative of love, longing, and impermanence that is often called the world's first novel. A member of the lesser Fujiwara aristocracy, unusually learned in an age that reserved Chinese letters for men, she served the empress Shoshi at the Heian court and left, besides her masterwork, a sharp diary and a body of poetry — yet her real name, her exact dates, and most of her life remain uncertain.

title: Nagarjuna
summary: Nagarjuna was an Indian Buddhist philosopher, active by tradition around the second century CE, who founded the Madhyamaka or 'Middle Way' school and gave systematic argument to the Mahayana idea of emptiness: the claim that nothing possesses an independent, self-standing nature of its own. His Fundamental Verses on the Middle Way became one of the most influential and most argued-over texts in the history of Asian philosophy.
aliases: The View from the Middle Way
associatedWorks: Mulamadhyamakakarika (Fundamental Verses on the Middle Way)

title: Nalanda
summary: The vast Buddhist monastic university — a mahavihara — of eastern India that, for some seven centuries, drew students from across Asia to study philosophy, logic, medicine, and astronomy — endowed by the Gupta and Pala kings, recorded by Chinese pilgrims, and destroyed around 1200 CE in a loss that became a symbol of the end of Buddhist learning in its Indian homeland.
aliases: The Source That Was Carried North
The University Seen by an Outsider

title: Napoleon Bonaparte
summary: The military and political genius who codified the French Revolution and turned its energies into continental empire

title: Nationalism
summary: The idea that the world should be divided into nations with the right to self-determination — and the most productively ambiguous concept in modern political life

title: Natural Law
summary: The tradition in moral and political philosophy holding that there exists a moral order discoverable through reason, independent of human legislation, that grounds universal rights and obligations — developed from Stoic and Roman sources through Aquinas's theological synthesis, adapted by Grotius and Locke into the foundations of international law and constitutional government, and contested by legal positivism, which holds that law is whatever human institutions make it.
aliases: The Moral Order Written in Creation
Law Is What Institutions Make It

title: Navigation
summary: The technology of controlled movement across open water — from Polynesian star-path wayfinding to Arab dhow routes across the Indian Ocean to European oceanic exploration — that connected distant civilizations, enabled global trade and empire, and made the modern world's geographic integration possible
aliases: The Compass and What Was Not Done With It
The Longitude Prize and the Chronometer

title: Negritude
summary: The literary and cultural movement launched in 1930s Paris by Black writers from France's colonies that turned a word coined as an insult into a banner of pride, affirming a shared Black identity and the value of African heritage against a colonial system that taught the colonized to despise their own culture. Led above all by Aimé Césaire of Martinique, Léopold Sédar Senghor of Senegal, and Léon-Gontran Damas of French Guiana, it was one of the major cultural counterparts to political Pan-Africanism, and remains one of the most influential and most contested ideas in twentieth-century Black thought.

title: Nelson Mandela
summary: The South African anti-apartheid leader who served 27 years in prison, emerged to negotiate the peaceful transition from white-minority rule to democracy, served one term as the country's first democratically elected president, and became the global symbol of reconciliation — a figure whose specific political choices made a specific political outcome possible, and whose symbolic elevation has sometimes obscured the complexity of what he actually did.
aliases: The Freedom Fighter Who Became President
associatedWorks: I Am Prepared to Die (Rivonia Trial speech)

title: Newton's Principia
summary: The 1687 book in which Isaac Newton showed that the same simple mathematical laws govern a falling apple and the orbiting moon — uniting heaven and earth under one system of motion and gravity, and founding the science of physics.
indexTerms: Isaac Newton, trans. I. Bernard Cohen & Anne Whitman

title: Ngugi wa Thiong'o
summary: The Kenyan novelist, playwright, and theorist who became the most forceful advocate for African literature written in African languages — a writer who won acclaim in English, then renounced English as the primary language of his fiction, choosing Gikuyu, arguing in Decolonising the Mind that to write Africa in the colonizer's language was to complete the colonization of the imagination, a stand that cost him prison and exile.
associatedWorks: Ngaahika Ndeenda (I Will Marry When I Want)

title: Niccolò Machiavelli
summary: The Florentine diplomat, historian, and political theorist whose short treatise The Prince (1513) offered a ruthlessly unsentimental account of how political power is actually acquired and maintained — founding modern political science and generating five centuries of controversy about whether he was a realist, a cynic, a republican, or a teacher of evil.

title: Nicolaus Copernicus
summary: The Polish churchman and astronomer who, working quietly for decades, proposed that the Earth and planets orbit a central Sun and that the Earth spins daily on its axis — a sun-centered cosmos that displaced the Earth from its ancient place at the center and opened the revolution in astronomy that Kepler and Galileo would carry forward, even though Copernicus himself kept the perfect circles of the ancients and a model barely simpler than the one he replaced.
aliases: The Founding Premise, Not the Finished System
The End of a Long Tradition
associatedWorks: De Revolutionibus Orbium Coelestium (On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres)
Monetae cudendae ratio (On the Minting of Coin)
indexTerms: N. M. Swerdlow and O. Neugebauer

title: Niels Bohr
summary: The Danish physicist who founded the quantum theory of the atom and, from his Copenhagen institute, presided over the field for a generation. His 1913 model showed why atoms don’t collapse: electrons occupy fixed orbits and jump between them in discrete leaps, each releasing or absorbing a precise wavelength of light, explaining the hydrogen spectrum and winning him the Nobel Prize. He developed complementarity — wave and particle descriptions both necessary yet mutually exclusive — and debated Einstein for decades over whether nature is fundamentally probabilistic. He escaped Nazi-occupied Denmark in 1943, advised the Allied bomb project, and spent his final years arguing, without success, for an open nuclear world.
aliases: The Architect of the Quantum Atom
associatedWorks: On the Constitution of Atoms and Molecules
The Quantum Postulate and the Recent Development of Atomic Theory
Open Letter to the United Nations

title: Nikola Tesla
summary: The Serbian-American inventor whose alternating-current induction motor and polyphase system won the 'War of Currents' and became central to how the world is wired — a real genius of the electrical age whose later wireless dreams went unrealized, who died broke and alone, and who is now half-buried under a modern internet legend.

title: Nitrogen
summary: The element that feeds the world and arms it — abundant in every breath at 78% of the air, a necessary part of every protein and every strand of DNA, yet locked in its common gas form behind a chemical bond so strong that almost nothing alive can break it. The whole drama of nitrogen is how to force it into a usable form, the line between hunger and plenty, and the chemistry that conjured bread from thin air also conjured the explosives of total war.

title: Noh Theater
summary: Noh is a masked, chanted dance-drama of Japan, refined in the fourteenth century and one of the world's oldest continuously performed theatrical traditions. Its principal actor, often masked, moves in slow, stylized steps while a chorus and small ensemble carry the story. Shaped by the actor-playwright Zeami under shogunal patronage, prized for the subtle grace its admirers call yūgen, and long the ceremonial theater of the samurai class, Noh survives today as a recognized heritage of humanity.
aliases: The Flower That Must Be Hidden
indexTerms: Zeami (trans. J. Thomas Rimer and Masakazu Yamazaki)

title: Nok Culture
summary: One of the oldest known artistic traditions of sub-Saharan Africa, named for the Nigerian village of Nok and flourishing across central Nigeria in the first millennium BCE. It produced strikingly precise terracotta sculptures of human figures and ranks among the region’s earliest iron-working cultures. The figures are dazzling; the world that made them is almost entirely lost. No writing survives; language, religion, and social structure are beyond recovery. Late-twentieth-century looting stripped much remaining material of its archaeological context, leaving dates and meaning still debated.
aliases: The Nok as a society, not a style
Overturning the myth of a static past
indexTerms: Thurstan Shaw, Paul Sinclair, Bassey Andah, and Alex Okpoko (eds.)

title: Nomadism
summary: Nomadism is the concept used to name a way of organizing social and economic life around mobility rather than a fixed home. In its main historical form, pastoral nomadism, communities live off herds of domesticated animals and move with the seasons across arid land that cannot support farming. As an analytical category it groups the mobile herding societies of the Eurasian steppe and other dry regions — including the empires that repeatedly reshaped the settled world — and it carries a long argument about whether such mobility was a primitive stage or a specialized adaptation.
aliases: The cycle of desert and city

title: Nonviolence
summary: The principle and practice of refusing to use violence in pursuit of political or social change, grounded in diverse philosophical and religious traditions and developed as a systematic political strategy in the 20th century through the work of Gandhi and King. Nonviolence is both a moral claim — that violence is wrong, or at least counterproductive — and a strategic claim — that it is an effective method of resistance against entrenched power.
aliases: Nonviolence as Complicity with Colonial Power
