Nonviolence as Spiritual Discipline, Not Strategic Choice
Nonviolence as a Problem to Be Managed
indexTerms: Erica Chenoweth and Maria J. Stephan

title: Norse Mythology
summary: Norse mythology is the body of pre-Christian myths and religious beliefs of the Norse-speaking peoples of Scandinavia and Iceland: the stories of gods like Odin, Thor, and Loki, of the world-tree Yggdrasil, and of Ragnarök, the foretold end of the world. Most of what survives was written down by Christian Icelanders two centuries after the North converted, above all in the two works known as the Eddas.

title: Nubia
summary: Nubia — the land of the middle Nile in what is now southern Egypt and northern Sudan — was one of Africa's oldest centers of power, supplying gold, ivory, and soldiers to the wider ancient world. Across three thousand years its cultures ran from the early A-Group through the C-Group to the powerful kingdom of Kerma, trading, warring, and intermarrying with Egypt to the north, before the kingdom of Kush (covered in its own entry) grew to rule Egypt itself.
aliases: Nubia as a power in its own right

title: Nuclear Deterrence
summary: The strategic doctrine that nuclear war can be prevented by ensuring that any nuclear attack would trigger a retaliatory strike capable of inflicting unacceptable damage on the attacker — making the initiation of nuclear war irrational for any state, and thereby achieving peace through the permanent mutual threat of civilizational destruction.
indexTerms: Sagan, Scott D. and Waltz, Kenneth N.

title: Nuclear Power
summary: The generation of electricity from the heat of splitting atoms — the most concentrated energy source humans have harnessed, and the one most entangled with weapons, catastrophe, and dread, now re-examined as a large, near-zero-carbon option in a warming world.
indexTerms: Joshua S. Goldstein and Staffan A. Qvist

title: Oil
summary: The fossil fuel that powers the modern world economy -- extracted commercially since 1859, the foundation of transportation, agriculture, plastics, and warfare for over a century, and the primary driver of anthropogenic climate change.
aliases: Oil as One Stage Among Many

title: Olaudah Equiano
summary: The formerly enslaved African writer, sailor, and abolitionist whose 1789 autobiography, The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano, became the most influential first-person account of the transatlantic slave trade ever written — a bestseller that put a human voice and an 'I' at the centre of the British abolition campaign, even as modern scholarship has raised an unresolved question about whether the African childhood it so movingly describes was his own memory.
associatedWorks: The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano, or Gustavus Vassa, the African

title: Omar Khayyam
summary: The Persian mathematician and astronomer from Nishapur who gave medieval algebra its high point — a systematic classification and geometric solution of cubic equations using intersecting conic sections — and who led the reform of the Persian calendar under the Seljuks, yet who is known in the West chiefly as the poet of the Rubaiyat, a fame that rests on quatrains of uncertain attribution and on Edward FitzGerald's loose 1859 English version.
associatedWorks: Treatise on the Demonstration of Problems of Algebra
Commentary on the Difficulties of Euclid's Postulates
Treatise on the Division of a Quadrant

title: On the Origin of Species
summary: The foundational document of modern biology — and the most culturally contested scientific text in history

title: Opera
summary: Born in Florence around 1600 from scholars trying to revive Greek sung drama, opera is staged drama in which characters sing rather than speak — voice, orchestra, and spectacle fused into one overwhelming experience. Its structure alternates recitative (sung speech that moves the plot) with the aria, the showpiece of pure emotion. It spread through Mozart, Verdi, Wagner, and Puccini into distinct national styles. Its glory carried a cost: the castrati, boys castrated to preserve high voices, were central to baroque opera for two centuries. Expensive and long tied to wealth, it remains a great art and a contested one.
indexTerms: Grout, Donald Jay; Williams, Hermine Weigel
Donald Jay Grout and Hermine Weigel Williams

title: Operation Condor
summary: The secret pact among South America's Southern Cone military dictatorships, formally launched in 1975, to share intelligence and reach across borders to kidnap, torture, and murder the political exiles and dissidents who had fled them. Run in the name of fighting communism and shielded by the Cold War, it joined Chile, Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia, and Brazil in a continent-wide machinery of forced disappearance whose declassified records later helped convict its architects.
aliases: The Hemisphere as a Cold War Front
indexTerms: Comisión Nacional sobre la Desaparición de Personas

title: Orientalism
summary: Two things share the name. The older sense is a Western tradition of depicting the Middle East and Asia — above all a lush 19th-century painting genre (Delacroix, Gérôme) of harems, bazaars, and deserts, vivid and often imaginary. In 1978 Edward Said published a book of the same title recasting the word as critique: a Western discourse of power portraying the East as exotic and irrational to justify imperial domination. Said's book founded postcolonial studies and drew serious counter-criticism from scholars including Robert Irwin and John MacKenzie. The word now means both the art and the charge.

title: Oyo Empire
summary: The Oyo Empire was the Yoruba cavalry state that dominated the savanna of what is now southwestern Nigeria and the Republic of Benin from roughly the sixteenth to the early nineteenth century. It built its power on horses across open grassland, taxed trade toward the Atlantic coast, and bound its sacred king, the alaafin, inside a web of constitutional checks so strong that a council could, by custom, require him to take his own life. Its early-nineteenth-century collapse fed the Yoruba civil wars and a surge of enslaved Yoruba carried to the Americas.
aliases: The empire as a contest over the kingship
The empire from the receiving end of the cavalry
The empire seen through its scattering
indexTerms: Toyin Falola and Matthew M. Heaton

title: Pablo Neruda
summary: The most widely read poet of the Spanish language — a Chilean who wrote aching love poems at nineteen that still sell by the millions, odes to onions, wool socks, and lemons, and an epic reaching to hold an entire continent in verse. He won the Nobel Prize in 1971. He was also a communist who praised Stalin, a diplomat, and a close ally of Salvador Allende, dying twelve days after the 1973 coup that destroyed that government — and his memoir confessed a private cruelty his admirers have never fully reconciled with the luminous work.
aliases: The poet of the people and the body
The compromised man behind the verse
associatedWorks: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair

title: Pablo Picasso
summary: The Spanish painter and sculptor who co-invented Cubism, transformed what Western art could look like, and became the most commercially dominant artist of the twentieth century

title: Palau
summary: Palau is a small archipelago nation of several hundred islands at the western edge of Micronesia, in the western Pacific. Home to a matrilineal island society for at least three thousand years, it was ruled in turn by Spain, Germany, Japan, and the United States before becoming independent in 1994, the last piece of the American-run Pacific trust territory to do so. A country of fewer than twenty thousand people, Palau is known for the Rock Islands and their marine lakes, for the ferocious 1944 Battle of Peleliu, and for using treaties and law to act far larger than its size.
indexTerms: Fitzpatrick, Scott M.; Nelson, Greg C.; Clark, Geoffrey

title: Palmyra
summary: The oasis caravan city in the Syrian desert that grew rich brokering trade between the Roman Mediterranean and Persia, India, and China, briefly broke away under Queen Zenobia, and survived as a magnificent ruin until parts of it were deliberately destroyed in 2015.
aliases: The Eastern Upstart Brought to Heel
A Shared Inheritance and Its Limits

title: Pan-Africanism
summary: The long political and intellectual movement built on the idea that African peoples and the African diaspora share a common history and destiny and should act together — an idea that began among the descendants of the enslaved in the Atlantic world, crossed the ocean to help drive Africa's decolonization, and produced institutions from the Organization of African Unity in 1963 to the African Union, while never achieving the continental political unity its boldest advocates imagined.

title: Paper
summary: The surface on which civilization recorded itself — invented in China around 100 BCE, transmitted westward through the Islamic world, and eventually combined with movable type to produce the information revolution that made the modern world; now being displaced by digital surfaces whose consequences are still unfolding
aliases: Paper and the Islamic Knowledge Tradition
Paper and the Transformation of Legal Authority
indexTerms: Lauren M. Singer and Patricia A. Alexander

title: Paper Money
summary: Paper money is a printed token that stands for value without holding it — a slip whose worth rests on trust and state authority rather than on the metal in a coin. Private merchants in Song-dynasty Sichuan circulated the first such notes, the jiaozi, around the turn of the eleventh century; after several issuers failed, the Song state took over and in 1024 issued the first government-backed paper currency. The instrument detached the money supply from the weight of metal and, after centuries of inflation and abandonment in China, became the model for every banknote and every fiat currency in use today.
aliases: A Practical Fix, Not a Grand Design
Money as an Instrument of Rule

title: Paris
summary: The capital on the Seine that grew from a Roman river town into one of Europe's great centers of learning and Enlightenment argument, a central stage of modern revolution, and a self-appointed capital of art and beauty — a splendor repeatedly written in blood, from the Terror's guillotine to Haussmann's displacements to the Nazi occupation and the 1942 roundup of its Jews.

title: Passports
summary: The small booklet that ties a person to a state and governs their right to cross borders — converting citizenship from a legal status into a portable document, and turning movement across the earth from a natural act into a privilege that states may grant or deny. From ancient letters of safe-conduct to the World War I checkpoint and the biometric chip, the passport is the state's monopoly on authorizing human mobility made physical.

title: Patrice Lumumba
summary: The first prime minister of the independent Congo, a self-taught nationalist who led his country to freedom from Belgium in June 1960, held power for barely ten weeks before independence collapsed into army mutiny and the secession of Katanga, was dismissed in a power struggle and then captured and killed in January 1961 with the documented complicity of his former colonizer and amid Cold War hostility from the United States, and who in death became the defining martyr of African independence — the symbol of how dangerous it was for a newly free African nation to choose a leader the great powers would not accept.
aliases: The Soviet danger in the heart of Africa
The dangerous radical who had to go

title: Peking Opera
summary: Peking opera, known in Chinese as jingju or "capital drama," is China's best-known form of traditional theatre: a stylized blend of song, heightened speech, mime, dance, and acrobatic combat performed on a nearly bare stage. It took shape in Beijing across the 1800s, after touring troupes from Anhui and Hubei merged their tunes with older styles, and it is younger than its grand air suggests. Its performers specialize in fixed role types, and painted-face characters wear bold, color-coded makeup. Once court and popular entertainment, later a national symbol, it was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2010.
aliases: The Art of the Perfected Sign

title: Peronism
summary: The Argentine political movement built around General Juan Perón and his wife Eva, which gave the working poor a voice, a welfare state, and an enduring loyalty, while critics charge it muzzled the press and built a personality cult — and which remains, eighty years on, the most powerful and most divisive force in Argentine politics.
aliases: The Nation Given Back to Its People
The Strongman and the Hollowed Republic
The Revolution That Was Promised and Betrayed

title: Persepolis
summary: The ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Persian Empire in southwestern Iran, built from around 518 BCE and burned by Alexander the Great in 330 BCE. Its carved reliefs of subject peoples bringing gifts to the king turned an empire of two dozen nations into a single sculptural image of order, and its ruins became a charged symbol of the ancient Persian past and modern Iranian identity.
aliases: The Throne of a Deep Past

title: Petra
summary: The rock-cut capital of the Nabataean kingdom in southern Jordan, where desert nomads grew rich controlling the incense caravan trade and engineered a city to thrive in a place with almost no water — abandoned after Rome and a shifting trade map drained it, and famous in the modern West as a city that was "lost" and then "found."

title: Petrarch
summary: The Italian poet and scholar who invented the concept of historical distance — the idea that ancient Rome was a distinct civilization that had ended, not simply a continuous past — and whose recovery of classical texts and sense of historical belatedness made the Renaissance possible as an intellectual project.

title: Photography
summary: The making of lasting images by recording light — a technology with no single inventor: Nicéphore Niépce made the first surviving photograph around 1826–27, Louis Daguerre perfected the daguerreotype announced in 1839, and William Henry Fox Talbot devised the negative-positive process that allowed copies. Photography democratized the portrait, bore witness to war and injustice, and freed painters from the duty of exact likeness — even as regimes airbrushed out the purged and empires catalogued colonized peoples as specimens. Its old claim to never lie was always false, and digital tools and AI have now dissolved whatever evidentiary authority remained.
aliases: The Machine That Saved the World's Face

title: Photosynthesis
summary: Photosynthesis is the quiet chemistry in every green leaf that turns sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into sugar and releases oxygen. It is the engine on which nearly all life runs: almost every food chain begins with an organism doing it, and the breathable air is its by-product. It also caused Earth's first great pollution crisis, the Great Oxidation, when the oxygen early microbes poured out was poisonous to most life of the time. Fossil fuels are ancient stored photosynthesis, so the modern carbon and climate problem is, at its root, downstream of this single reaction.
aliases: The force that remade the air
The buried sunlight that runs the economy
indexTerms: Lyons, Timothy W., Reinhard, Christopher T., and Planavsky, Noah J.
Sven Beer, Mats Bjork, and John Beardall

title: Pigs
summary: Domesticated from wild boar about nine thousand years ago, the pig became one of humanity's most efficient food animals — an omnivore that converts scraps into protein faster than any ruminant, raised almost entirely for meat. That biological utility sits alongside a striking cultural fact: the prohibition on pork in Judaism and Islam touches more than a billion people today, making the pig the center of the world's most analyzed food taboo. No other domestic animal so starkly exposes the distance between what a creature is good for and what it is made to mean.

title: Plastic
summary: Synthetic polymers shaped by heat and pressure — from Bakelite in 1907 to the petrochemical explosion of polyethylene, PVC, and PET — plastic remade daily life by making everything cheaper, lighter, and more durable. It is now an ecological reckoning: a material so permanent that it does not disappear, only fragments, and so pervasive that microplastics are found in human blood, Arctic ice, and the deepest ocean trenches.
aliases: The Material That Served Human Welfare
indexTerms: Geyer, Roland; Jambeck, Jenna R.; Law, Kara Lavender

title: Plate Tectonics
summary: Earth's outer shell is broken into roughly a dozen rigid plates that drift a few centimeters a year over hot flowing rock beneath. In a single stroke the theory explains why mountains rise, where earthquakes strike, where volcanoes burn, and how the continents drifted apart from a single ancient landmass. Alfred Wegener proposed continental drift in 1912 and was ridiculed for decades; only in the 1950s and 1960s did seafloor spreading and magnetic evidence vindicate him, and the full theory was accepted around 1970. Plate boundaries also explain the unequal global geography of natural hazard.
aliases: The theory that unified a science
The idea that came back from the dead
The ground that will not hold still
indexTerms: Vine, Frederick J. and Matthews, Drummond H.
Kearey, Philip; Klepeis, Keith A. and Vine, Frederick J.
Philip Kearey, Keith A. Klepeis and Frederick J. Vine

title: Plato
summary: The Athenian philosopher whose dialogues invented Western philosophy's central questions — the nature of knowledge, justice, beauty, and the good life — and whose theory of Forms, defense of philosopher-kings, and suspicion of democracy have been argued with, built upon, and reacted against for 2,400 years
indexTerms: Plato (trans. G.M.A. Grube, rev. C.D.C. Reeve)

title: Popol Vuh
summary: Sacred narrative of the K'iche' Maya of highland Guatemala, tracing the gods' creation of the world, their failed attempts with mud and wood, and the final fashioning of humans from maize. Its dramatic center is the Hero Twins, Hunahpu and Xbalanque, who descend into the underworld of Xibalba and outwit its lords of death. The original hieroglyphic tradition is lost; the text survived because K'iche' authors wrote it in their language using the Latin alphabet around the 1550s, and a Spanish friar, Francisco Ximénez, copied it around 1701. It remains a living sacred narrative for K'iche' Maya communities.
aliases: Saved by the Conquest That Tried to Erase It
indexTerms: Michael D. Coe and Stephen Houston
Rita M. Palacios and Paul M. Worley

title: Populism
summary: The political style and ideology that pits a virtuous people against a corrupt elite — a form of politics that has appeared across the left-right spectrum, in democratic and authoritarian contexts, and in every region of the world — and one of the most contested concepts in contemporary political science, not least because it is simultaneously a diagnosis of democratic dysfunction and its symptom.
indexTerms: Mudde, Cas and Kaltwasser, Cristóbal Rovira

title: Porcelain
summary: The hard, white, translucent ceramic perfected in China that the rest of the world could not make — porcelain was a luxury so prized it lent its country a permanent name, drove one of history's great industrial-espionage races, and moved millions of objects across oceans before anyone outside China understood what it actually was.
aliases: The Source That Named the Material
The Material That Mapped the Trade

title: Potatoes
summary: The starchy tuber domesticated in the Andes and carried to the Old World after 1492 — the potato's extraordinary caloric yield per acre helped fuel Europe's population explosion and, historians argue, contributed to the labor surpluses that powered industrialization; yet the same crop's monoculture vulnerability turned a fungal blight into the catastrophic Irish Famine of the 1840s, making the potato the clearest single case of how an imported plant can remake a continent's demography and then, through fragility and policy failure, help produce one of the nineteenth century's defining demographic catastrophes.

title: Potosí
summary: The silver mountain in the Bolivian Andes whose ore bankrolled the Spanish empire and helped build the first global economy — and beneath which generations of conscripted Andean and enslaved African workers died in the mines and the mercury refineries. For a time one of the largest cities on Earth, its name became Spanish for a fortune, and Andean memory called its peak the mountain that eats men.
aliases: A Fortune Dug From a Hill
indexTerms: Dennis O. Flynn and Arturo Giráldez

title: Power
summary: The concept that makes the invisible architecture of social life visible — and reveals why authority persists even when it could be challenged

title: Pramoedya Ananta Toer
summary: Pramoedya Ananta Toer (1925–2006) was one of Indonesia's most internationally prominent novelists, best known for the Buru Quartet, a four-novel saga of his nation's awakening under Dutch colonial rule. Held without trial in a penal colony, he first told the story aloud to fellow prisoners, wrote it down once he regained pen and paper, and published it after his release — then lived to see his books banned in his own country for decades.
associatedWorks: It's Not an All Night Fair

title: Privacy
summary: The claim of individuals to a sphere of life shielded from observation, intrusion, and judgment — a right most people notice only once it is gone, easy to feel and famously hard to define, now pressed on every side by states that surveil for safety, companies that profit from data, and a public that wants secrecy for itself and transparency from the powerful.
aliases: the right to be let alone
The Right to Be Let Alone
indexTerms: Samuel D. Warren and Louis D. Brandeis

title: Probability
summary: The mathematics of uncertainty — which transformed how civilization manages risk, enabled the insurance industry, statistical medicine, quantum mechanics, and machine learning, and produced an intellectual foreclosure that made prior frameworks for reasoning about uncertain futures permanently inadequate
indexTerms: Blaise Pascal and Pierre de Fermat

title: Prohibition
summary: Prohibition was the nationwide ban on making, selling, and transporting alcohol in the United States, in force from 1920 to 1933 under the 18th Amendment and the Volstead Act. It was the crowning victory of a century-long movement to rid the country of drink. Widely evaded and linked to a boom in organized crime, it became the only constitutional amendment ever repealed.
aliases: A Rational Answer to a Real Harm
The Futility of Banning a Want

title: Propaganda
summary: The deliberate manipulation of beliefs, emotions, and perceptions through selective presentation of information — a practice as old as organized power and a concept whose modern form was shaped by the First World War, theorized by Freud's nephew Edward Bernays, industrialized by totalitarian states, and then so thoroughly absorbed into advertising, public relations, and electoral politics that its boundaries with legitimate persuasion became genuinely contested.
indexTerms: Bennett, W. Lance, and Robert M. Entman (eds.)
Lazarsfeld, Paul F., Bernard Berelson, and Hazel Gaudet
McCombs, Maxwell E., and Donald L. Shaw

title: Property
summary: Property is not a thing but a relationship — a bundle of enforceable claims against everyone else about who can use, exclude others from, and transfer something. Understanding how a claim becomes ownership, and what kinds of things can be owned, is one of the most consequential questions in political and economic life.
aliases: Property as the structure of exploitation
Property rights as prerequisite for economic development

title: Ptolemy
summary: The Greek-speaking mathematician of Roman Alexandria who, around 150 CE, built the most accurate model of the heavens the ancient world produced — an Earth-centered system of circles upon circles that predicted where the planets would appear and held that authority for roughly fourteen centuries until Copernicus, Kepler, and Galileo overturned it; almost nothing is known of the man, so his career is read entirely through the works that bear his name.
aliases: A System to Repair, Not Just Receive
indexTerms: J. Lennart Berggren and Alexander Jones

title: Public Debt
summary: The money a state borrows against its future taxes — the device that has let governments spend far beyond what they collect, funding wars, infrastructure, and ambitions no single year of revenue could cover. Public debt turns a government's promise to repay into the bedrock of modern finance, which makes the question of whose promises are trusted, and who ultimately pays, one of the central problems of state power.
indexTerms: Reinhart, Carmen M.; Rogoff, Kenneth S.

title: Public Health
summary: The organized effort of societies to protect and improve the health of populations as a whole, rather than treating individuals one at a time. Public health rests on the insight that the conditions in which people live — water, air, housing, food, income — shape health outcomes more powerfully than medicine alone.
aliases: Public Health as a Colonial and Post-Colonial Problem
Public Health, Liberty, and the Limits of State Authority
indexTerms: Bruce G. Link and Jo Phelan
Richard H. Thaler and Cass R. Sunstein
WHO Commission on Social Determinants of Health

title: Qin Shi Huang
summary: The king of the western state of Qin who conquered the other six Warring States, declared himself First Emperor of a unified China in 221 BCE, and in a single brutal decade forced a vast field of rival kingdoms into one standardized, centrally governed empire whose basic shape lasted two thousand years -- built on forced labor, fear, and Legalist rule through law, and remembered ever after as both the maker of China and the archetype of the tyrant.
indexTerms: Yuri Pines, Gideon Shelach, Lothar von Falkenhausen, Robin D.S. Yates (eds.)

title: Quantum Mechanics
summary: The physical theory developed between 1900 and 1930 that describes the behavior of matter and energy at atomic and subatomic scales — replacing classical physics in that domain, resisting intuitive interpretation for a century, and underpinning virtually all modern technology from semiconductors to lasers to medical imaging.
indexTerms: Einstein, Albert; Podolsky, Boris; Rosen, Nathan

title: Quarantine
summary: Whether, and how far, a society may detain or confine healthy people who have been exposed to a contagious disease, against their will, to stop them from spreading it before they fall sick — a contested choice that sets the collective claim to safety from an epidemic against the individual claim to liberty, bodily autonomy, and due process, and that has repeatedly been turned against the poor and feared minorities under a health pretext.

title: Queen Nzinga
summary: Njinga Mbande (c. 1583–1663), ruler of the kingdoms of Ndongo and Matamba in what is now Angola, who fought, allied with, converted to Christianity, and out-maneuvered the Portuguese empire for nearly four decades — using war, diplomacy, and shifting alliances to hold off full Portuguese domination in her lifetime, and becoming an enduring symbol of African and female resistance to European conquest and the Atlantic slave trade.
aliases: The barbarous queen and the lost convert
indexTerms: Heywood, Linda M. and Thornton, John K.
Linda M. Heywood and John K. Thornton

title: Rabbinic Judaism
summary: The form of Judaism that emerged after the Roman destruction of the Second Temple in 70 CE — replacing Temple sacrifice with prayer, priestly authority with rabbinic scholarship, and territorial religion with a portable legal and intellectual system that could survive anywhere on earth.

title: Rabindranath Tagore
summary: The Bengali poet, songwriter, novelist, painter, and educator who in 1913 became the first non-European to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, on the strength of his own spare English recasting of selected Bengali devotional poems — and who then spent the rest of his life resisting every box the world tried to put him in, lionized in the West as a mystic sage of a 'spiritual East' he distrusted, while at home he remade Bengali letters and music and wrote the words and music for the national anthems of two countries.
aliases: The Friend Who Would Not March
associatedWorks: The Home and the World (Ghare Baire)

title: Race
summary: The concept of dividing humanity into distinct biological groups based on physical characteristics — a system of classification whose scientific validity is rejected by contemporary biology but whose social and political consequences have been, and remain, thoroughly real.
indexTerms: Massey, Douglas S.; Denton, Nancy A.
Shapiro, Thomas M.; Oliver, Melvin L.

title: Radio
summary: The technology that sent sound through the air without wires — and by reaching everyone at once, created the first electronic broadcast mass medium and the modern broadcast nation. Radio collapsed the distance between a single voice and millions of listeners, making it possible, for the first time, for one person to speak intimately to a whole country simultaneously. That power built democratic movements and fueled dictatorships alike.
indexTerms: Lazarsfeld, Paul F. and Katz, Elihu

title: Railways
summary: The network of iron and steel tracks that reorganized the movement of goods, people, and armies across land surfaces, collapsing distance and time in ways that restructured economies, empires, and daily life. Railways did not merely accelerate existing patterns of movement — they created new geographies of production, settlement, and power.

title: Ramadan
summary: The ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar, during which observant Muslims fast from dawn to sunset — abstaining from food, drink, and other appetites in daylight as one of the Five Pillars of Islam. Tied by tradition to the first revelation of the Quran, it is a yearly discipline of restraint, charity, and communal worship that moves through the seasons and is now observed across a global Muslim population of roughly two billion people, on every continent.

title: Rammohun Roy
summary: The Bengali scholar, religious reformer, and public intellectual, often called the father of the Indian Renaissance, who fused deep learning in Sanskrit, Persian, Arabic, and English to argue for a rational, monotheistic reform of Hinduism, campaigned against the burning of widows, founded the reformist Brahmo Sabha, and pioneered modern education, a free press, and political petition under a colonial rule he both used and partly endorsed.
aliases: The Reformer Who Leaned on Empire

title: Ranjit Singh
summary: The one-eyed warlord who fused a dozen rival Sikh war-bands into a single kingdom and gave the Sikhs, for the first and only time, an empire of their own. Ranjit Singh (1780–1839), the Lion of Punjab, captured Lahore in 1799 and was proclaimed Maharaja in 1801, built a state across the Punjab, Kashmir, and Peshawar, and is remembered for ruling Sikhs, Hindus, and Muslims with unusual even-handedness. The empire collapsed within a decade of his death and was annexed by the British.
aliases: The Maharaja of a Mixed Land
indexTerms: Singh, Patwant, and Jyoti M. Rai
Patwant Singh and Jyoti M. Rai

title: Rapa Nui (Easter Island)
summary: The famous story — that Rapa Nui's Polynesian settlers mindlessly deforested their island, collapsed their own society, and serve as a parable for human self-destruction — is largely wrong. Rats devoured palm seeds and prevented forest regeneration as much as human axes did; the islanders adapted and endured. The true catastrophe came from outside: Peruvian slave raids in 1862–63 abducted much of the population, including the specialists who could read rongorongo, the island's still-undeciphered script. Epidemics followed, reducing the Rapanui to about 110 people by 1877. The collapse story says more about outsiders' fears than about the people who actually lived there.
aliases: A Society That Adapted, Then Was Attacked
A People Who Survived Being Nearly Erased
indexTerms: Terry L. Hunt and Carl P. Lipo

title: Rastafari
summary: The religious movement born among poor Black Jamaicans around 1930 that proclaimed the new Ethiopian emperor Haile Selassie a living God and Messiah, built a way of life from the Bible, Marcus Garvey's Back-to-Africa prophecy, and the longing for a homeland, and through reggae and Bob Marley carried its message of Black pride and resistance to a watching world.
aliases: A Devout Man, Not a God
A Public Nuisance to Be Suppressed

title: Reconstruction
summary: The dozen years after the American Civil War, 1865 to 1877, when the United States tried for the first time to build a genuine multiracial democracy on the ruins of slavery — writing Black freedom and citizenship into the Constitution, electing Black men to office across the South, and then abandoning the experiment to a campaign of white terror that would take a century to undo.

title: Redlining
summary: The practice, formalized by the federal government in the 1930s, of marking Black and immigrant neighborhoods as too risky to lend in while steering government-backed home loans toward new white suburbs. Treated here as a policy question, it asks how a constitutional democracy built racial inequality into the ordinary machinery of mortgage lending, and why the color drawn on Depression-era maps still shows up, generations later, in the wealth, segregation, and even the health of American cities. Outlawed by the Fair Housing Act of 1968, its consequences remain measurable and its exact mechanism still debated.
indexTerms: Robert K. Nelson, LaDale Winling, et al.
Price V. Fishback, Jonathan Rose, Kenneth Snowden, and Thomas Storrs

title: Refugees
summary: A refugee is the person the state system was not built to accommodate: someone whose own country has become the danger, crossing a border with no automatic right to stay. The 1951 Refugee Convention fixed the definition — well-founded fear of persecution, cannot safely return — and the central rule: do not send anyone back into the danger they fled. The subject pits two real goods against each other: a duty to protect the persecuted, and a nation's right to control its border. How to honor both when millions press the claim is among the sharpest disputes in democratic politics.
indexTerms: Guy S. Goodwin-Gill and Jane McAdam
United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees

title: Rembrandt
summary: A miller's son who became Amsterdam's most sought-after portraitist, Rembrandt van Rijn (1606–1669) painted biblical drama and ordinary faces with equal conviction, lifting figures from deep shadow into warm light until the paint seemed to glow from within. He painted himself across four decades in roughly eighty self-portraits — the most sustained record any artist has left of a single aging face. The Night Watch is his most famous work; his etchings place him among the greatest printmakers in history. Bankruptcy, bereavement, and church censure marked his later years; the work grew only deeper.
associatedWorks: The Anatomy Lesson of Dr Nicolaes Tulp
The Return of the Prodigal Son
indexTerms: Rembrandt Research Project (Bruyn, J., et al.)

title: Representation
summary: The device that lets millions rule without ever gathering: the idea that a few chosen people can stand in for the many, speak and act in their name, and so make self-government possible at the scale of a nation — even as it never quite settles whether the few truly speak for the many or only claim to.
aliases: An Assembly That Looks Like the People

title: Republicanism
summary: Republicanism is the political tradition holding that free self-government exists to prevent any person or group from holding arbitrary power over others, and that liberty depends on dividing power, binding it with law, and cultivating citizens willing to defend the public good. It runs from the Roman Republic through Machiavelli's Florence and the Atlantic revolutions to a philosophical revival that began in the 1990s.

title: Revolution
summary: The analytical concept describing the sudden, fundamental transformation of a political system — typically through popular mobilization, the overthrow of existing authority, and the seizure of state power — and the framework scholars use to explain why revolutions happen, how they develop, and what they produce.

title: Rhetoric
summary: Rhetoric is the systematic art of persuasion — the study and practice of using language to move an audience toward belief or action. From ancient Greece through the nineteenth century it was a central subject of formal education in the West, and its five-part method for composing a speech shaped how people were taught to argue, write, and reason in public.

title: Rice
summary: The crop that fed half of humanity — domesticated in China's Yangtze River valley around 7000 BCE, spread across Asia through millennia of agricultural development, and shaped the labor-intensive paddy civilizations of East and Southeast Asia whose demographic weight, political structures, and cultural patterns remain distinct from the wheat-based civilizations of the West
aliases: Rice and the African Knowledge Transfer

title: Richard Feynman
summary: The American theoretical physicist who shared the 1965 Nobel Prize for quantum electrodynamics, invented the Feynman diagrams that became one of the standard languages of particle physics, worked on the Manhattan Project as a young man, and — through his lectures, his memoirs, and his televised diagnosis of the Challenger disaster — became the twentieth century's most famous example of science as joyous, skeptical play.
associatedWorks: QED: The Strange Theory of Light and Matter
There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom
indexTerms: Feynman, Richard P.; Leighton, Robert B.; Sands, Matthew
Richard P. Feynman, Robert B. Leighton, Matthew Sands

title: Rights
summary: A right is the trump card an individual holds against the world — a justified claim so strong that even the government and the majority must honor it, the idea that a person is owed certain things simply as a person. It is among the most powerful and the most contested moves in all of politics, splitting into deep arguments over where rights come from (nature, law, or social agreement) and which claims rise to that status.
indexTerms: David A. J. Richards (or the relevant author in the VSI series)

title: Roads
summary: The technology of surfaced paths connecting human settlements — one of the oldest and most consequential investments any civilization makes, the infrastructure through which trade, armies, messages, and ideas move, and a technology that has organized the political geography of every complex society from ancient Mesopotamia to the modern highway system.

title: Rock and Roll
summary: Black rhythm and blues crossed segregated radio into the white mainstream in the early 1950s and became rock and roll. Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Fats Domino, and Sister Rosetta Tharpe built the sound from blues and gospel, driven by electric guitar and a hard backbeat. White teenagers embraced it before the law integrated their schools. A segregated industry channeled the greatest fame and profit to white performers — above all Elvis Presley — while the Black creators who invented the sound were underpaid and uncredited. Rock seeded fifty years of popular music; who got paid for it remains unsettled.
aliases: A Sound Made on the Color Line

title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms
summary: Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo yanyi) is a Chinese historical novel, traditionally attributed to Luo Guanzhong in the 14th century, that retells the fall of the Han dynasty and the long war among the states of Wei, Shu Han and Wu. It is counted among the Four Great Classical Novels of Chinese literature. Built from Chen Shou's third-century Records and centuries of oral storytelling and opera, it survives today mainly in a 120-chapter version edited by Mao Lun and his son Mao Zonggang c. 1679.
aliases: A verdict inherited from the scholars

title: Romanticism
summary: The intellectual, artistic, and political movement that arose in Europe in the late 18th century as a reaction against Enlightenment rationalism — emphasizing emotion, imagination, nature, the individual, and the organic — whose influence on art, literature, music, philosophy, and politics has been so pervasive that we still live within it.
aliases: The Recovery of the Whole Person

title: Rome
summary: The city on the Tiber that ruled an empire, became the seat of a universal Church, was sacked and rebuilt multiple times, and remains the most continuously symbolically contested location in the Western world — a place that every subsequent civilization has claimed, imitated, mourned, or defined itself against.

title: Roxelana / Hurrem Sultan
summary: An enslaved woman from Ruthenia who became the freed, legally married wife of Suleiman the Magnificent at the height of the Ottoman Empire — she broke two long-standing customs of the dynasty, helped open the era later called the Sultanate of Women, corresponded with foreign kings, and funded major public works in Istanbul, while a hostile contemporary and Western tradition reduced her to a scheming concubine.
aliases: The Sorceress Who Bewitched a King
The Architect of a New Role

title: Rubber
summary: The elastic, waterproof latex of tropical trees that began as a curiosity and became indispensable once vulcanization made it stable — rubber put the modern machine on wheels and sealed it watertight, and the world's hunger for it drove both an industrial transformation and one of the cruelest episodes of colonial extraction, the wild-rubber atrocities of the Congo and the Amazon.
aliases: The Victims of the Quota System

title: Rumi
summary: The 13th-century Persian Sufi poet, born in the eastern Islamic world and settled in Konya in Anatolia, who began as a respected Muslim scholar and jurist and, after his transforming friendship with the wandering mystic Shams of Tabriz, became the ecstatic poet of divine love whose vast Masnavi and lyric Divan made him one of the most widely read poets in any language — and whose order inspired the whirling sema ceremony of the Mevlevi dervishes.

title: Saladin
summary: The Kurdish-born founder of the Ayyubid dynasty who ended the Shia Fatimid caliphate in Egypt, united Egypt and Syria, crushed the Crusader army at Hattin in 1187, and recaptured Jerusalem for Islam eighty-eight years after the First Crusade had taken it — and whose comparatively restrained conduct toward the conquered turned him, in later Western and Muslim memory alike, into the archetype of the chivalrous, magnanimous foe.
aliases: The just sultan and defender of the faith
The noble Saracen and worthy foe
The symbol of unity and resistance
indexTerms: Lyons, Malcolm Cameron and Jackson, D.E.P.
Baha al-Din ibn Shaddad (trans. D.S. Richards)
Malcolm Cameron Lyons and D.E.P. Jackson

title: Salt
summary: The mineral sodium chloride — essential for human survival, one of the oldest trade commodities in the world, a driver of civilizational development across every inhabited continent, and a substance whose history illuminates how the most basic biological necessities become instruments of commerce, taxation, and political power.

title: Salvador Allende
summary: The Chilean physician and lifelong socialist who, in 1970, became the first openly Marxist president elected in a liberal democracy, promised to build socialism through the ballot and the constitution rather than the gun, nationalized the copper mines and much of industry at the head of his Popular Unity coalition, and died three years later in the burning presidential palace as the military bombed his project into seventeen years of dictatorship — remembered by admirers as a martyr of the democratic left and by critics as a leader whose radical program, alongside US hostility, helped plunge Chile into chaos.
aliases: The Martyr of the Peaceful Road
The Radical Who Wrecked a Republic
A Hemisphere on the Front Line
associatedWorks: First Year speech to the National Congress (1971)
Speech to the United Nations General Assembly (1972)

title: Samarkand
summary: One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities of Central Asia, in present-day Uzbekistan — the ancient Sogdian Maracanda that Alexander the Great took in 329 BCE, a great Silk Road oasis hub devastated by Genghis Khan’s armies in 1220, and above all the imperial capital that Timur (Tamerlane) rebuilt with the plunder and captive artisans of a continent into a vision of turquoise domes and soaring tiled portals, so dazzling its name became Western shorthand for the unreachably exotic East.
indexTerms: Thomas W. Lentz and Glenn D. Lowry
Ruy González de Clavijo (trans. Guy Le Strange)

title: Samba
summary: The Afro-Brazilian music and dance, rooted in the rhythms of enslaved Africans and their descendants, in candomble worship and African circle dances, with deep roots in Bahia and a modern urban form that crystallized in Rio de Janeiro -- once raided by police as the disreputable culture of the favela, then within a generation embraced as the very sound of Brazil, the soul of Carnival, and one of the most joyful and widely loved musical forms on earth, even as the Black Brazilians who created it remained marginalized.
aliases: A Sound to Unite the Nation

title: Samoa
summary: Samoa is an independent Polynesian nation of two main islands and several smaller ones in the central South Pacific, long regarded as a heartland of Polynesian culture. Settled by Lapita voyagers roughly three thousand years ago, it carried the matai chiefly-title system and the customary order known as fa'a Samoa through a colonial era that split the archipelago among Germany, the United States, and New Zealand. After a nonviolent independence movement, it became, on 1 January 1962, the first Pacific island nation to regain its independence in the twentieth century. It is distinct from American Samoa, the United States territory that occupies the eastern islands.

title: Samora Machel
summary: Guerrilla commander, then Mozambique's first president in 1975, Machel tried to build a socialist state from a poor, largely rural colony — nationalizing land and services, opening schools, sheltering liberation fighters from Rhodesia and South Africa. Those neighbors answered by arming RENAMO, an insurgency that wrecked the economy and killed enormous numbers of civilians. In 1986 his presidential aircraft went down near the South African border; the cause was never conclusively settled. His record is divided: founding national hero and architect of policies that hardened a war he could not end.
associatedWorks: Mozambique: Sowing the Seeds of Revolution
Samora Machel: An African Revolutionary (selected speeches and writings)

title: Samori Ture
summary: Rising from trader to ruler across parts of present-day Guinea, Mali, and Côte d'Ivoire, Samori Ture built the Wassoulou Empire and fought the French advance for roughly seventeen years. He purchased modern rifles, trained gunsmiths, and used scorched-earth retreats — stripping entire districts bare and moving his population east — to stay one step ahead of conquest. His armies were also financed by raiding and slave-trading; the lands he crossed paid dearly. Captured in 1898 and exiled to Gabon, he died in 1900. Guinea's first president, Sékou Touré, claimed descent from him and made Samori an icon of anti-colonial resistance.
aliases: The state caught in a trap

title: Scholasticism
summary: The dominant intellectual method of medieval European universities from roughly 1050 to 1350, organized around the systematic reconciliation of Christian theology with ancient philosophy — above all Aristotle — through rigorous logical analysis. Scholasticism produced the first institutionalized tradition of rational inquiry in the Western world and established the intellectual frameworks that both shaped and were disrupted by the Scientific Revolution.
aliases: Scholasticism as Partial Heir and Partial Distortion
Scholasticism as the Problem That Faith Must Overcome
Scholasticism as What Reason Must Escape

title: Secularism
summary: Secularism is not the absence of religion from public life. It is a family of political arrangements for managing disagreement about ultimate truth. Different societies have answered the same question — what do we do when citizens disagree about what God requires? — in structurally different ways. Understanding those differences is more analytically useful than treating secularism as a single thing.

title: Sejong the Great
summary: The fourth king of Korea's Joseon dynasty, who presided over its golden age and gave his people an alphabet. A Confucian sage-king and tireless patron of science, he gathered scholars into a royal research institute and sponsored advances in astronomy, agriculture, and medicine. His greatest legacy was Hangul, a simple writing system promulgated so that ordinary Koreans could read and write their own language, championed against the resistance of his own scholar-elite.
aliases: The script that threatened the learned

title: Separation of Powers
summary: Separation of powers is the machine built to make power fight power. It splits a government against itself, handing law-making, law-enforcing, and law-judging to rival branches so that no single hand can seize them all, on the wager that liberty survives only where power is forced to check power.
aliases: The Case for a Strong Hand
indexTerms: Montesquieu, Charles de Secondat, Baron de
Hamilton, Alexander, Madison, James, and Jay, John
Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, John Jay
Juan J. Linz and Arturo Valenzuela (eds.)

title: Serfdom
summary: Serfdom is a system of unfree labor in which peasants were legally bound to the land they worked and owed compulsory labor and dues to its lord. Unlike a slave, a serf was tied to an estate rather than owned outright as movable property, keeping a family, a customary holding, and a recognized place in law. For much of the European Middle Ages it was the condition of most people, and in Russia it lasted until 1861. As a name for dependent labor that cannot freely leave, the idea has never quite disappeared.
aliases: Why bind a worker at all

title: Sewers
summary: The infrastructure for removing human waste from populated areas — one of the oldest public health technologies, a system whose presence or absence has determined whether cities could grow beyond a few thousand people, and whose massive construction in the 19th century transformed European and American cities from places where epidemics were normal into places where they were exceptional.
indexTerms: Haines, Michael R. and Price V. Fishback

title: Shaka Zulu
summary: The founder-king who forged a small southern African chiefdom into the Zulu Kingdom within roughly a decade, traditionally credited with military and political innovations that made Zulu power possible — and one of the most heavily mythologized figures in African history, whose popular portrait as a genius-tyrant rests largely on a handful of unreliable European accounts written after his death in 1828.

title: Shakespeare
summary: The English playwright and poet who wrote approximately 37 plays and 154 sonnets between roughly 1590 and 1613, working in the commercial theater of Elizabethan and Jacobean London — producing a body of work that has been continuously performed, translated, adapted, and argued over for four centuries, and that now functions as the closest thing the English-speaking world has to a universal literary canon.
indexTerms: Taylor, Gary; Egan, Gabriel; Bourus, Terri; Jowett, John (eds.)

title: Shanghai
summary: The city the world built on China's coast — a marshy county town pried open after the Opium War and carved into foreign-run concessions, which exploded into the glittering, unequal 'Paris of the East,' became the birthplace of Chinese modernity and of the Chinese Communist Party, had its treaty-port order shut down after revolution, and was then unleashed again to become the showcase of China's return to wealth and power.
aliases: The Cradle That Closed the Concessions

title: Shareholder Value
summary: The doctrine that a corporation's single legitimate purpose is to maximize financial returns to its shareholders — an idea forged in the economics departments of the 1970s, institutionalized through stock-based pay and hostile takeovers in the 1980s, and now contested by a stakeholder movement arguing that a firm answerable only to its owners produces inequality, short-termism, and damage it was never designed to account for.
aliases: The Doctrine That Was Never Law
indexTerms: Jensen, Michael C. and Meckling, William H.
Berle, Adolf A. and Means, Gardiner C.
Shleifer, Andrei and Vishny, Robert W.
Adolf A. Berle and Gardiner C. Means

title: Sharia
summary: In Muslim thought, sharia is God's eternal way for human life, known in full only to God; what people call sharia in practice is fiqh, the centuries of fallible human jurisprudence that tries to understand and derive it. Plural, revisable, and overwhelmingly concerned with worship and everyday conduct rather than the harsh punishments of Western caricature, it is drawn from the Quran and the Prophet's example through schools of law that disagree among themselves — and its meaning is fiercely contested in the modern age of nation-states.

title: Sheep
summary: Among the first animals humans domesticated, the sheep gave the world wool — a renewable fiber that clothed temperate civilization and drove economies from ancient Mesopotamia to medieval England. The demand for wool funded kingdoms, built cathedrals, and created the global merino trade. It also remade landscapes: sheep pasture was one of the prime engines of early English enclosure, especially in the Tudor period, when landlords cleared common fields and drove peasants off the land (later enclosure had other agricultural and legal drivers too). The gentlest-looking of domestic animals reorganized who owned the land and who was allowed to live on it.

title: Shehu Usman dan Fodio
summary: Fulani Islamic scholar and reformer whose preaching against the Hausa city-states grew into the jihad of 1804 and produced the Sokoto Caliphate, the largest state in nineteenth-century West Africa. A prolific writer in Arabic and Fulfulde, Dan Fodio argued that the Hausa kings practiced a corrupted Islam and that reform, when blocked, required armed struggle. He handed military authority to his brother and son and left a contested inheritance: a vast Muslim state and a rich written tradition on one side, conquest and enslavement — including of Muslims — on the other.
indexTerms: Toyin Falola and Matthew M. Heaton
Beverly B. Mack and Jean Boyd

title: Shinto
summary: The native religion of Japan — the way of the kami, the countless sacred presences felt in nature, place, and ancestry. It has no founder, no official scripture in the strict sense, and no fixed creed, only shrines, purification, and festival. For over a thousand years it fused with Buddhism, then in the modern era was briefly forged by the state into an instrument of emperor-worship and nationalism, before returning after 1945 to the quiet rhythms of Japanese life.
indexTerms: Kuroda Toshio (trans. James Dobbins and Suzanne Gay)

title: Shipbuilding
summary: The technology and industry of constructing vessels capable of crossing open water — from the dugout canoes of the Neolithic to the ironclad battleships and container carriers of the industrial age — that connected continents, moved goods and armies, transmitted religion and disease, and organized the global economic system that still operates today.
indexTerms: de Vries, Jan and van der Woude, Ad

title: Siddhartha Gautama
summary: The historical founder of Buddhism, whose analysis of suffering and the conditions of human experience produced one of the most enduring and geographically expansive intellectual traditions in human history.

title: Sigmund Freud
summary: The founder of psychoanalysis — the theory that unconscious mental processes shape behavior, that childhood experience forms adult personality, and that repressed desires drive neurotic symptoms — whose ideas transformed how educated people in the 20th century understood themselves, regardless of whether those ideas were scientifically valid.
aliases: The Interpretive Tradition (or "Reading Culture Through Freud")
associatedWorks: Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality

title: Sikhism
summary: The religion founded in the Punjab by Guru Nanak (1469–1539) on the teaching that there is one formless God beyond every human division, and that caste, ritualism, and the Hindu–Muslim divide are beside the point — a truth lived through honest work, sharing with others, and devotion. Carried forward by a line of ten Gurus and then by a living scripture, the Guru Granth Sahib, and the initiated community of the Khalsa, it is today the world's fifth-largest organized religion.
aliases: An Independent Revelation Seen from Within
The Tradition Seen as a Developing History
The Faith Seen from Outside the Punjab
indexTerms: Fenech, Louis E., and W.H. McLeod

title: Silent Spring
summary: Rachel Carson’s 1962 book, which built a meticulous, lyrical case that synthetic pesticides — above all DDT — were moving through the food chain and poisoning far more than their target insects, humans included. Vindicated against a furious industry campaign by a presidential science panel, it is widely credited with launching the modern environmental movement and is tied to the 1972 US ban on DDT — though Carson had sought regulation, not the near-total bans that followed.

title: Silicon Valley
summary: Silicon Valley is a region at the southern end of the San Francisco Bay Area in California, and the name for the technology industry and innovation system centered there. Since the mid-twentieth century it has been the world's leading source of computer chips, and later of personal computers, software, and internet companies. Built around a distinctive mix of university research, venture capital, and constant company founding, it is admired as a model of innovation and criticized for its concentrated wealth and power.

title: Silk
summary: The lustrous textile produced from silkworm cocoons — China's most consequential technological secret, the commodity that named the most important trade route in pre-modern history, and a material whose production, trade, and political symbolism reorganized economies and societies from East Asia to the Mediterranean for three thousand years.
indexTerms: Xinru Liu and Lynda Norene Shaffer

title: Sima Qian
summary: The father of Chinese history — the Han court astrologer who chose the disgrace of castration over death so that he could finish his father's great work, and in the Shiji built the model for how China would write its own past for the next two thousand years.
aliases: The Historian as a Servant Who Judged

title: Simón Bolívar
summary: The Venezuelan-born military commander and political leader who liberated most of Spanish South America between 1813 and 1825, governing as president or dictator across Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia — and who spent his final years watching the republics he had created fragment, federate against him, and slide toward the caudillo instability that would define Latin American politics for generations.

title: Simone de Beauvoir
summary: French existentialist philosopher whose The Second Sex (1949) provided the twentieth century's foundational analysis of women's oppression — arguing that 'woman' is not a biological fact but a social construction imposed by a male-defined world — and whose life as a public intellectual, writer, and activist embodied the freedom she theorized.

title: Simone Weil
summary: French philosopher, labor activist, and religious mystic who refused the distance between thought and suffering — leaving the lecture hall to work in factories, making attention and affliction the center of ethics, and pursuing God to the very edge of the Church without entering it, in a body of work published almost entirely after her early death.
associatedWorks: The Iliad, or the Poem of Force

title: Slavery
summary: The institution of owning human beings as property — one of the most widespread and durable institutions in human history, practiced on every inhabited continent, powering the ancient economies of Greece, Rome, and the Near East, and reaching its most systematic modern form in the Atlantic plantation complex that built the wealth of the early capitalist world — and one of the central subjects of moral and political philosophy, whose abolition required a reconception of what it means to be human.
indexTerms: Fogel, Robert William; Engerman, Stanley L.

title: Smallpox
summary: The disease caused by the Variola virus that killed an estimated 300 million people in the 20th century alone, shaped the outcome of colonial conquest in the Americas and elsewhere through catastrophic differential mortality, and was eradicated by a global vaccination campaign in 1980 — the only human infectious disease to be deliberately eliminated from nature. Smallpox is simultaneously civilization's most lethal recurring catastrophe and its greatest public health victory.
aliases: Disease as the Enabling Condition of Genocide

title: Social Class
summary: Social class is an attempt to explain why inequality persists across generations in societies that claim to offer equal opportunity. Whether it does explain this — and which of several competing frameworks explains it best — is one of the most contested questions in social science.

title: Social Darwinism
summary: The application of evolutionary ideas — competition, survival of the fittest, natural selection — to human societies, used to justify inequality, empire, and eugenics.

title: Socialism
summary: The family of political and economic ideas holding that production, distribution, and the major resources of society should be owned or regulated collectively rather than by private individuals — an idea that has generated revolutionary movements, welfare states, authoritarian regimes, and ongoing debates about the relationship between equality and freedom.

title: Socrates
summary: An Athenian philosopher who wrote nothing but transformed Western philosophy — through the practice of public questioning he called elenchus, his conviction that the unexamined life is not worth living, and his trial and execution for impiety and corrupting the youth of Athens, which became the defining image of philosophy as a dangerous practice.
indexTerms: Thomas C. Brickhouse and Nicholas D. Smith

title: Soil
summary: The thin living skin of the land — a slow-built mixture of weathered rock, water, air, dead matter, and an immense population of microscopic life — on which nearly all food and therefore civilization grows; a substance that forms over centuries yet can be lost in a season, that cradled the first cities and has been worn away beneath societies that depended on it, and that now stores more carbon than the atmosphere and all plants combined, making its loss or repair a major lever on the climate.
indexTerms: Brevik, Eric C. and Hartemink, Alfred E.
David R. Montgomery and Anne Biklé

title: Sojourner Truth
summary: The woman born enslaved in Dutch-speaking New York who freed herself by walking away, sued a white man to recover her stolen son and won, renamed herself Sojourner Truth, and became one of the most powerful spoken voices in America for the freedom of both Black people and women — remembered, partly through a later legend, for a speech that asked the nation whether she was not a woman too.
aliases: The Woman Who Was Her Own Evidence
The Woman the Movement Could Not Contain
The Woman and the Legend, Built Together

title: Solar Power
summary: The conversion of sunlight directly into electricity via the photovoltaic effect — the only major form of large-scale generation that uses no turbine and no moving parts — and the technology whose roughly 99% cost collapse over recent decades has made clean electricity, in many places, the cheapest humanity has ever produced.

title: Sonni Ali
summary: The warrior-king who reigned over Songhai from roughly 1464 to 1492 and forged the largest empire precolonial West Africa would know — seizing Timbuktu in 1468, starving Djenne into surrender, and building a war fleet on the Niger. He is remembered in two irreconcilable ways: as a cruel tyrant in the Arabic chronicles of the Timbuktu scholars he persecuted, and as a fearless, invincible sorcerer-king in Songhai oral tradition. The chronicles written by his enemies remain the main written record of a man whose true character lies largely beyond recovery.
aliases: The third great empire of the gold roads
associatedWorks: The siege and capture of Djenne
The founding of the Songhai imperial state

title: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz
summary: Writing from a convent cell in seventeenth-century New Spain, Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz produced some of the most dazzling lyric, satirical, and philosophical verse in the Spanish Baroque — including the philosophical poem Primero Sueño and her famous Respuesta a Sor Filotea, a reply to a bishop's criticism that argued, with wit and scripture, for women's right to study. Under pressure from the church that remains debated, she surrendered her library and her pen in her last years and died in 1695 nursing epidemic victims. She is now recognized as a founding figure of Mexican and Latin American letters.
aliases: The early defender of women's minds
associatedWorks: Respuesta a Sor Filotea de la Cruz
indexTerms: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz
Arenal, Electa; Powell, Amanda (eds. and trans.)

title: Sovereignty
summary: Sovereignty is the claim to final authority within a territory — the right to make ultimate decisions, answer to no higher power, and exclude others from intervening. It is not a fact but a claim, and the most revealing cases are the ones where two sovereignty claims collide and neither can yield without ceasing to be sovereign.
indexTerms: Biersteker, Thomas J. and Cynthia Weber (eds.)
International Commission on Intervention and State Sovereignty

title: Soviet Central Asia
summary: Soviet Central Asia is the era from the 1917 revolution to the Soviet collapse in 1991, when the Russian-ruled steppe and oases of Turkestan were reorganized, in stages, into five national republics: Uzbek and Turkmen in 1924, Tajik by 1929, Kazakh and Kyrgyz only in 1936. Collectivization fell unevenly, making cotton belts of Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, and parts of Tajikistan while the Kazakh and Kyrgyz lands centered on herding, grain, and mining; on the Kazakh steppe forced sedentarization brought a famine that killed roughly 1.5 million, near a third of the Kazakh people. In 1991 the five emerged as independent states carrying Soviet borders and economies.

title: Sparta
summary: The Greek city-state that subordinated all life to military excellence, produced the most feared army in the ancient world, and invented a social system so extreme — the agoge, the helot system, the suppression of individual life — that it has fascinated and disturbed observers from Plato to the present.

title: Spinoza
summary: The Amsterdam-born philosopher whose Ethics demonstrated, in geometric form, that God and Nature are one substance, that the universe operates by necessary laws admitting no exceptions, that human beings are modes of that single substance rather than its masters, and that freedom consists not in transcending necessity but in understanding it — producing a system that was denounced as atheism in his lifetime and became foundational to the Enlightenment, secular political philosophy, and the philosophy of mind.
associatedWorks: On the Improvement of the Understanding

title: Sri Lanka
summary: The teardrop island off the southern tip of India that became, for more than two thousand years, the great fortress of Theravada Buddhism and a crossroads of the Indian Ocean — home to the ancient hydraulic civilizations of Anuradhapura and Polonnaruwa, the island where the Pali canon was first written down, ruled in turn by Sinhalese kings, south Indian invaders, and European traders, and torn in its modern life by a 26-year civil war between the Sinhalese-majority state and Tamil separatists that ended in 2009 amid mass civilian death.
indexTerms: United Nations Secretary-General's Panel of Experts

title: Srinivasa Ramanujan
summary: The poor, almost entirely self-taught South Indian clerk who, with little formal training, produced mathematics of such strange depth that it still feels less discovered than received — who sent his theorems to Cambridge, worked with G.H. Hardy, was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society, and died at 32, the great modern emblem of raw, untaught mathematical genius.
aliases: The Talent the Empire Almost Missed
The Mathematician Who Credited a Goddess
indexTerms: Andrews, George E.; Berndt, Bruce C.
Ken Ono and Amir D. Aczel
George E. Andrews and Bruce C. Berndt

title: Srivijaya
summary: Srivijaya (c. 7th–13th century) was a maritime empire centered on Sumatra that commanded key chokepoints and grew rich by servicing, taxing, and protecting the trade that passed between China and India. It was also a major center of Mahayana Buddhist learning. The entry's purpose is to explain what kind of power a thalassocracy was — and how a state this important could be largely forgotten and then pieced back together from scattered evidence.
aliases: The empire as a station on the road to India
The hub seen from the periphery

title: St Petersburg
summary: The city Russia willed into being on a Baltic marsh — founded by Peter the Great in 1703 by forced labor, raised as a planned imperial capital of palaces and canals to face Europe, made the cradle of the 1917 revolution, and renamed Leningrad to endure a German siege that starved roughly a million people.

title: Standardized Testing
summary: The practice of measuring people with the same instrument scored the same way, to rank and sort them at scale — and the long argument over what such tests actually measure and whether they open doors for the talented or mainly reproduce the advantages students already bring

title: Statistics
summary: The mathematical discipline for collecting, analyzing, and drawing inferences from data — and the conceptual framework through which modernity learned to reason about populations, uncertainty, and risk. Statistics transformed medicine, social science, government, and science, and its misuse produces some of the most persistent errors in public reasoning.

title: Steel
summary: The cheap, strong alloy of iron and carbon that the Bessemer converter made suddenly abundant in the 1850s — whose mass production built the railways, skyscrapers, warships, and reinforced concrete that shaped the modern world, and whose geography of dominance ran from Britain and America through Japan and South Korea to the overwhelming Chinese supremacy of the 21st century.
indexTerms: Sabel, Charles F. and Zeitlin, Jonathan
Brooks, Stephen G. and Wohlforth, William C.

title: Stephen Hawking
summary: Working almost entirely in his head as motor neurone disease stripped him of movement and then speech, Stephen Hawking showed with Roger Penrose that classical general relativity drives the early universe toward a singularity, and then derived that black holes are not perfectly black but slowly radiate and evaporate. That second result — Hawking radiation — is theoretically powerful but too faint to detect, which is why it never earned him a Nobel Prize. A Brief History of Time made cosmology a global popular passion and his synthesized voice one of the most recognized sounds on earth.
associatedWorks: The Large Scale Structure of Space-Time
indexTerms: Hawking, S. W.; Ellis, G. F. R.

title: Steve Biko
summary: The South African anti-apartheid leader who founded the Black Consciousness Movement, built on the insight that apartheid’s laws were only half the prison: the other half was the belief, beaten into Black South Africans, that white was superior. As a medical student he made psychological liberation the precondition for political freedom. The regime banned him in 1973 and confined him to one district. In September 1977 the police detained him without charge; weeks later he was dead in their custody. The inquest held no one responsible, but the lie collapsed over time, and his death made him a global symbol of the anti-apartheid struggle.
associatedWorks: Black Consciousness (the movement and its founding documents)
Steve Biko: Black Consciousness in South Africa (testimony, ed. Millard Arnold)
indexTerms: Truth and Reconciliation Commission of South Africa

title: Stoicism
summary: The ancient Greek and Roman philosophical school — founded by Zeno of Citium in Athens around 300 BCE — that taught that virtue is the only true good, that external circumstances are 'indifferent,' that we can control our judgments but not outcomes, and that living according to reason and nature is the path to flourishing: a philosophy that shaped Roman imperial culture, influenced early Christianity, and has undergone a remarkable 21st-century revival as a practical philosophy of life.

title: Suburbia and the American Dream
summary: Suburbia and the American Dream refers to the mass movement of Americans into newly built residential suburbs after the Second World War, roughly 1945 to 1970, when federal home loans, cheap cars, and assembly-line construction put a single-family house with a yard within reach of millions of working families. It became the physical form of the American Dream. It was also, by the design of the same government programs, a machine of racial exclusion that shut Black families out of the wealth it created.
aliases: The suburb as a state project

title: Sufism
summary: The mystical and interior current within Islam, devoted to direct experience of God through purification of the self, the remembrance of God, and love. From early ascetics it grew into great orders of master and disciple, produced poets read across the world, carried Islam into Africa and Asia, and remains in long tension with strict scriptural authority.

title: Sugar
summary: Pure crystallized sweetness, a luxury that became a craving and then a daily necessity — sugar's demand built the Atlantic plantation complex, the deadliest of the slave colonies, where millions of enslaved Africans were worked to death to feed Europe's appetite. The same drive carried sugar from elite spice to cheap industrial fuel and, in the modern diet, into a global health reckoning over diabetes and obesity.
aliases: The People the Cane Fields Consumed
The Sweet Tooth and the Sugar Interest

title: Sukarno
summary: Founding president of Indonesia and a spellbinding orator, Sukarno held together an archipelago of thousands of islands and hundreds of peoples through force of voice and a unifying national creed, Pancasila. He proclaimed independence on 17 August 1945, hosted the 1955 Bandung Conference as a global voice for the non-aligned world, then replaced parliamentary democracy with an authoritarian Guided Democracy. His balancing act between army and Communist Party collapsed after a murky 1965 coup attempt; General Suharto seized power, mass killings followed, and Sukarno died under house arrest in 1970.
aliases: The Demagogue Who Built No Walls
associatedWorks: Sukarno: An Autobiography (as told to Cindy Adams)

title: Suleiman the Magnificent
summary: The tenth Ottoman sultan, who reigned from 1520 to 1566, the longest of any Ottoman ruler, and presided over the empire at its height — he took Belgrade, Rhodes, and Hungary, besieged Vienna, dominated the Mediterranean, systematized the secular law that earned him the name 'the Lawgiver,' and patronized a golden age of Ottoman art and architecture, while the same reign was marked by the brutal logic of dynastic succession that led him to order the deaths of two of his own sons and his closest friend.
aliases: The Grand Turk at the Gates
The View from Hungary and Persia

title: Sun Yat-sen
summary: The doctor turned professional revolutionary honoured as the Republic of China’s ‘Father of the Nation’ in Taiwan and commemorated on the mainland as a great revolutionary forerunner — the figurehead of the 1911 revolution that toppled more than two thousand years of imperial rule, the first provisional president of the Republic of China for a matter of weeks in 1912, and the author of the 'Three Principles of the People,' who spent decades in exile plotting failed uprisings against the Qing and died in 1925 before reunification, leaving an unfinished revolution and a record that scholars judge thinner in governing power than in inspiring symbol.
aliases: The Founding Father of the Republic
The Symbol More Than the Statesman
associatedWorks: Three Principles of the People (Sanmin Zhuyi)

title: Sundiata Keita
summary: The founder of the Mali Empire, who united the Mandinka chiefdoms around 1235 and defeated the Sosso king Sumanguru at Kirina, building the West African state that would later astonish the world under his descendant Mansa Musa. By legend a boy who could not walk before he rose to conquer, he survives above all through the Epic of Sundiata, the oral tradition kept alive by griots for eight centuries, which recounts his childhood disability, exile, and return. The broad achievement is historically secure; the vivid detail belongs to legend.
aliases: The figure behind a single sentence
The first mansa of the gold roads
associatedWorks: The founding of the Mali Empire
indexTerms: Levtzion, Nehemia and Hopkins, J. F. P. (eds.)

title: Surgery
summary: The treatment of injury and disease by physically cutting into, removing from, or repairing the living body — a deliberate, controlled wounding meant to heal. Surgery is ancient, found from prehistoric skull-drilling to the detailed operative texts of ancient India, yet for almost all of that history it was capped by three barriers: pain, bleeding, and infection. The conquest of pain by anesthesia in the 1840s, and of infection by antiseptic, germ-based method around 1867, remade it into the central, planned tool of modern medicine, able to open the abdomen, chest, and skull.
aliases: The Craft That Imported Its Own Release
indexTerms: Meara, John G.; et al. (Lancet Commission on Global Surgery)

title: Surrealism
summary: Reason had failed — the trenches proved it. From that conviction, Andre Breton launched Surrealism in Paris in 1924, arguing that truth lay in what reason suppressed: dreams, desire, accident. Drawing on Dada and Freud, writers practiced automatic writing while painters split between Dali and Magritte's hyper-real dream-imagery and the abstract automatism of Miro. Breton ran the movement with authoritarian control, expelling dissenters and treating women artists as muses rather than makers. Its devices — melting clocks, uncanny juxtaposition — now saturate advertising and film: a victory that is also a kind of capture.

title: Surveillance
