summary: The systematic, purposeful watching of people — their movements, communications, transactions, and associations — in order to know, manage, predict, or control them; both a tool of genuine protection and the quietest instrument of power ever built, because the mere knowledge of being watched makes a person govern themselves.

title: Tahiti
summary: Tahiti is the largest island of the Society Islands, in the South Pacific territory that France governs as French Polynesia. A high volcanic island settled by Polynesian voyagers about a thousand years ago, it became, after European ships arrived in the 1760s, the West's defining image of a tropical paradise, and later a French colony at the center of the twentieth-century Pacific. Home to a living Polynesian society, it remains far more complicated than the postcard the world made of it.

title: Tamerlane
summary: Timur (1336-1405), the lame warlord from a minor clan near Samarkand who spent thirty-five years in almost ceaseless war, claiming the mantle of Genghis Khan he could not legally wear. He ruled as emir behind puppet khans, turned terror into policy by piling the heads of cities that resisted into towers, shattered the Golden Horde, sacked Delhi in 1398, captured the Ottoman sultan Bayezid at Ankara in 1402, and died at nearly seventy while marching on China. He was both one of history's most destructive conquerors and a patron of beauty who funneled the plunder of a continent into Samarkand, and his memory remains split between scourge and national hero.
associatedWorks: Tuzuk-i Timuri / Malfuzat-i Timuri — later disputed memoir tradition attributed to Timur
indexTerms: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi (trans. as the Zafarnama)

title: Taxation
summary: The compulsory extraction of resources from individuals and institutions by a political authority — the financial mechanism through which states have funded armies, built infrastructure, sustained bureaucracies, and redistributed wealth for five thousand years. No state has ever operated without some form of taxation; the history of how states have taxed — what they taxed, who they taxed, and how they justified it — is inseparable from the history of political power itself.
indexTerms: Saez, Emmanuel; Slemrod, Joel; Giertz, Seth H.

title: Tea
summary: The cured leaf of Camellia sinensis is almost certainly the world's most consumed drink after water, and around it an empire, two wars, and a revolution turned. Britain's addiction to Chinese tea created a trade deficit paid first in silver, then in opium; the Opium Wars followed. British botanical espionage and new plantations in India and Ceylon — drawing on both stolen Chinese plants and indigenous Assam tea — broke China's monopoly. And in Boston in 1773, a tax on tea helped ignite an American revolution.

title: Television
summary: The technology that added the moving image to broadcasting — and became, for most of the twentieth century, the single screen around which families, nations, and consumer culture organized themselves, before cable and streaming shattered the shared audience into a billion private feeds.
aliases: The View from Outside the Screen

title: Tenochtitlan
summary: The island capital of the Aztec (Mexica) empire, a planned city of perhaps two hundred thousand people that rose from a lake in the Valley of Mexico, sustained by raised garden plots, causeways, and aqueducts. The Spanish who saw it in 1519 were astonished by its scale; a Spanish-led coalition of Indigenous rivals and enemies of Mexica power, especially Tlaxcalans and others, aided by smallpox, besieged and razed it in 1521. Mexico City was built on its ruins, the lake later drained, so the Aztec capital now lies mostly underground.
aliases: The City as Wonder and Justification
The Fall From Inside the City
The Buried City Read From the Ground
The Drowned Capital as National Origin

title: Teotihuacan
summary: One of the largest cities of the ancient world — a meticulously planned metropolis in the highlands of central Mexico, dominated by colossal pyramids and a grand central avenue, home to well over a hundred thousand people at its height. Its monuments survive in extraordinary detail, yet we do not know what its builders called themselves, what language they spoke, or how they governed.
aliases: A City Read From the Ground
Seen in the Mirror of Others

title: The Abbasid Caliphate
summary: The Islamic caliphate that ruled from Baghdad from 750 to 1258 CE — overthrowing the Umayyads through a revolutionary movement, governing the largest empire of its era, presiding over the Islamic Golden Age of science, philosophy, and medicine, and ending with the Mongol sack of Baghdad that destroyed the caliphate's political and cultural center.

title: The Abolition Movement
summary: The organized campaign to end the transatlantic slave trade and slavery itself, spanning roughly a century from the 1780s through Brazilian emancipation in 1888 — a movement that drew on Quaker organizing, Black resistance and testimony, Enlightenment philosophy, and evangelical Christianity to produce what was called at the time the first great international human rights campaign.
indexTerms: Hall, Catherine; Draper, Nicholas; et al.

title: The Aeneid
summary: Virgil's epic poem recounting the journey of Aeneas from the fall of Troy to the founding of the line that would produce Rome — composed between 29 and 19 BCE at the request of Augustus and left unfinished at Virgil's death. The Aeneid performed a specific political function: it gave Rome a mythological origin equal in prestige to the Greek tradition while making Roman imperium seem divinely destined. Its world effect ran through the medieval and Renaissance imagination of empire, Dante's Commedia, and every subsequent literary culture that used it to legitimate political authority.

title: The Agricultural Revolution
summary: The transition from hunter-gatherer foraging to settled farming — occurring independently in at least seven regions across ten thousand years — that produced surplus food, population growth, cities, states, writing, and social hierarchy, and that Jared Diamond called 'the worst mistake in the history of the human race' even as it made civilization possible.

title: The Ainu
summary: The Ainu are the Indigenous people of Hokkaido, Sakhalin, and the Kuril Islands, whose distinct language, animist religion, and way of life set them apart from the ethnic Japanese who colonized their homeland. For much of the modern era the Japanese state tried to assimilate them out of existence; only in 2008 and 2019 did it formally recognize them as an Indigenous people. They are a living people whose culture is now being revived.
aliases: A nation that refused to vanish
indexTerms: William W. Fitzhugh and Chisato O. Dubreuil (eds.)

title: The Aksum Stelae
summary: The giant carved stone obelisks raised over royal tombs at Aksum in northern Ethiopia around the third and fourth centuries CE, cut to look like multi-storey towers with false doors and windows. The tallest ever attempted, weighing over five hundred tons, fell and broke. A twenty-four-metre stele was looted by Fascist Italy in 1937, shipped to Rome, returned to Ethiopia in 2005, and re-erected at Aksum in 2008, making it a landmark case in the modern fight over looted heritage.
aliases: A Test Case for Whose Past It Is

title: The Algerian War
summary: The war (1954–1962) through which Algeria won independence from France — fought over territory France legally treated as part of itself, against a Muslim majority excluded by a European settler minority. It brought down the French Fourth Republic, exposed the systematic use of torture by a democratic state, and became the twentieth century's defining case of decolonization as both catastrophe and reckoning.
aliases: The Lost Homeland and the Abandoned
The War That Could Not Be Named

title: The Algorithm
summary: A finite, exact procedure — a recipe of steps that, followed mechanically, produces a result. Algorithms predate computers by more than a millennium, but the word acquired a second life as the name for the opaque ranking, recommendation, and decision systems that now sort social feeds, set prices, screen job applicants, and allocate risk across modern life.
indexTerms: Kleinberg, Jon, Mullainathan, Sendhil, and Raghavan, Manish

title: The Alhambra
summary: The red-walled palace-fortress on the hill above Granada, built by the Nasrid dynasty in the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries as the last Muslim kingdom in Western Europe contracted toward its final stronghold — the supreme surviving work of late Andalusi art, and the place where al-Andalus ended when Granada surrendered to Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492.

title: The Almohads
summary: The Berber Muslim reform movement and empire that preached absolute divine oneness, ruled the Maghreb and Muslim Spain in the 12th and 13th centuries, persecuted Jews and Christians, yet sheltered the philosophers Ibn Tufayl and Averroes suffering a decisive blow at Las Navas de Tolosa before collapsing in the Maghreb by 1269.

title: The Alphabet
summary: The writing system in which a small set of symbols — typically 20 to 30 — represents the sounds of a language rather than its syllables or words, enabling literacy at a scale and speed that no earlier writing system achieved — and reorganizing what could be thought, recorded, traded, governed, and believed.

title: The Amazon
summary: The Amazon is the planet's largest river system and the vast rainforest it waters — a natural force on a planetary scale that carries roughly a fifth of all the river water reaching the world's oceans, holds the richest concentration of living species on land, makes much of its own rainfall through a cycle of aerial moisture called flying rivers, and now sits close enough to a tipping point that its fate has become a question for the whole climate of the planet.
aliases: The forest as home, managed over millennia
Sovereignty and the double standard of conservation
The Amazon as a node in the planetary climate machine
indexTerms: Nobre, Carlos A. and Lovejoy, Thomas E.
Salati, Eneas and Vose, Peter B.
Lovejoy, Thomas E. and Nobre, Carlos, eds.

title: The American Civil War
summary: The war fought between the United States of America and the Confederate States of America from 1861 to 1865, in which the Union’s victory ended slavery in America and settled the question of whether states could secede — at a cost of approximately 620,000 soldiers’ lives, the deadliest conflict in American history.
aliases: The Cause That Was Not Lost
indexTerms: Confederate States (South Carolina, Mississippi, Georgia, Texas)
Berlin, Ira, et al. (Freedmen and Southern Society Project)
Beard, Charles A.; Beard, Mary R.

title: The American Revolution
summary: The colonial revolt that established the first modern republic, created the template for constitutional self-government, produced founding documents whose contradictions between universal rights and racial slavery structured American politics for a century, and became the reference point for anti-colonial movements across two centuries
indexTerms: Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay

title: The American West
summary: The American West is both a region — the lands beyond the Mississippi River that the United States conquered and settled across the nineteenth century — and a national myth about that region, built around the idea of the frontier as the force that shaped American character. This entry treats the West as an idea: the frontier thesis, the doctrine of manifest destiny, and the later scholarship that revised both.
aliases: The View from the Land Itself

title: The Analects
summary: The collected sayings and conversations of Confucius, compiled by his disciples after his death around 479 BCE. The Analects is the foundational text of Confucianism and the most influential book in East Asian history — not primarily as philosophy but as a technology of governance. For fourteen centuries, the civil service examination system that governed China, Korea, Vietnam, and Japan required mastery of the Analects and the classical commentaries upon it. The text did not merely describe an ideal of learned virtue; it selected the officials who administered the largest bureaucratic states in human history.
indexTerms: Roger Ames and Henry Rosemont Jr.
E. Bruce Brooks and A. Taeko Brooks

title: The Ancestral Puebloans
summary: Stone rooms tucked into sandstone alcoves, great houses at Chaco Canyon, cliff dwellings at Mesa Verde — the Ancestral Puebloans built one of North America's most sophisticated architectural and agricultural traditions across the Four Corners region from the first centuries CE to about 1300. They farmed maize, beans, and squash in an arid landscape and developed from pit-house villages into multi-story masonry pueblos. Long misnamed the "Anasazi," they did not vanish: their descendants are the living Pueblo nations — Hopi, Zuni, Acoma, and the Rio Grande peoples — who continue traditions stretching back a thousand years.
indexTerms: Linda S. Cordell and Maxine E. McBrinn

title: The Andes
summary: The world's longest mountain range, running some 7,000 kilometers down the western edge of South America — a vertical system of stacked environments that forced those who lived there to master altitude, terrace steep slopes, manage cold and thin air, and cultivate entirely different crops at every elevation, giving the world the potato and a lineage of civilizations stretching back four thousand years.
aliases: The Mountains as Home and System
The Mountains That Fed the World
indexTerms: Ruth Shady Solís, Jonathan Haas, and Winifred Creamer
Terence N. D'Altroy and Timothy Earle

title: The Aral Sea Disaster
summary: The Aral Sea disaster is the human-caused collapse of what was once the world's fourth-largest lake, in Central Asia, after the Soviet Union diverted its two feeder rivers to irrigate cotton beginning in the 1960s. Over the following decades the sea lost most of its water, broke apart, and left behind toxic dust, a ruined fishing economy, and elevated rates of illness in the communities nearby, though the dust's exact share of that harm remains hard to measure. It is now the standard example of an environmental catastrophe made by human hands, and it is still unfolding.
indexTerms: Zonn, Igor S.; Glantz, Michael; Kostianoy, Andrey; Kosarev, Aleksey

title: The Archive
summary: The institution through which states, churches, corporations, and other organized powers have accumulated and controlled records — creating the documentary basis for legal authority, historical knowledge, and administrative power, while simultaneously determining what is preserved, who may access it, and what is destroyed.
indexTerms: Schwartz, Joan M. and Cook, Terry (eds.)
Schwartz, Joan M. and Cook, Terry

title: The Armenian Genocide
summary: The systematic deportation and mass killing of the Ottoman Empire's Armenian population by the ruling Committee of Union and Progress during the First World War — beginning in April 1915 and recognized by most historians as the first genocide of the twentieth century, with estimates of the dead ranging from roughly 600,000 to 1.5 million, many clustering near or above one million.
aliases: The Catastrophe and the Demand for Recognition
The Official Denial and Its Stakes
The First Genocide of the Century
indexTerms: Ugur Ümit Üngör and Mehmet Polatel

title: The Army
summary: The organized land force a society raises to wage war — a system defined not by the individual warrior but by discipline, hierarchy, drill, and supply, which turns a crowd of individuals into a single instrument that moves and fights as one. The army is among humanity's oldest large-scale organizations, and it has worked in two directions at once: it made mass killing and conquest possible, and in the same motion it forged states, roads, bureaucracies, and the modern machinery of government.
aliases: The Army as Engine of Suffering
The Army as Maker of States

title: The Art of War
summary: A brief military treatise attributed to the Chinese strategist Sun Tzu — the foundational text of East Asian military thought and the most widely applied strategic framework outside its original military context in world history.

title: The Arthashastra
summary: The great Sanskrit treatise on statecraft and political economy, traditionally attributed to Kautilya, the minister said to have helped found the Maurya Empire — a vast, coldly practical manual on how to run a state, fill its treasury, wage war, and keep a king alive through an apparatus of spies and surveillance, lost for centuries and rediscovered in the early twentieth century.

title: The Asante Empire
summary: The Asante (Ashanti) Empire (c. 1700–1902) was the powerful Akan state of the West African forest, ruled from Kumasi in present-day Ghana. Unified under the sacred Golden Stool and the office of the Asantehene, it grew rich on gold and on supplying captives to the Atlantic slave trade, built a sophisticated bureaucracy and road network, and fought a long series of wars against Britain before its final annexation. The entry holds together Asante's genuine statecraft and wealth, its central role as a slave supplier, and its long, effective resistance to conquest.
aliases: The empire from the wrong end of its wars
Asante as rival, partner, and useful story
The kingdom as a sacred nation
indexTerms: Boahen, A. Adu (with Emmanuel Akyeampong et al.)

title: The Asante Golden Stool
summary: The Sika Dwa Kofi, the Golden Stool of the Asante people of present-day Ghana, is the sacred object that holds the soul of the nation. By Asante tradition the priest Okomfo Anokye called it down from the sky around 1700 to seal the union of the Asante confederacy under the first Asantehene, Osei Tutu. It is never sat upon and never allowed to touch the ground. Its honor triggered the War of the Golden Stool in 1900, after a British governor demanded to sit on it, and it was hidden rather than surrendered. It remains the central emblem of Asante identity today.
aliases: The Living Soul of the Nation
The Stool That Was Never Taken

title: The Assassination of Franz Ferdinand
summary: The shot that activated a system optimized for catastrophe

title: The Assyrian Empire
summary: The Neo-Assyrian Empire of roughly 911 to 609 BCE, the first state to dominate most of the major centers of the ancient Near East — built on a standing professional army, iron weapons, and siege engineering, run through the mass deportation of conquered peoples, and remembered both for its own boasts of terror and for the great library at Nineveh that preserved the Epic of Gilgamesh.
aliases: The King and the God Ashur

title: The Atomic Bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: The American use of atomic bombs against the Japanese cities of Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, and Nagasaki on August 9, 1945 — killing approximately 200,000 people immediately and tens of thousands more from radiation — which ended the Second World War in the Pacific and inaugurated the nuclear age: a permanent condition of human civilization in which the means to destroy it exist and cannot be uninvented.
aliases: The First Act of the Cold War

title: The Avesta
summary: The sacred scripture of Zoroastrianism, written in an otherwise-unrecorded ancient Iranian language called Avestan. Its oldest core, the Gathas, is a set of hymns traditionally attributed to the prophet Zarathustra and among the oldest religious poetry in any Indo-Iranian language. Carried orally by priests for many centuries and committed to writing only late, under the Sasanian Empire, the Avesta survives today as a fraction of a once-vast canon — most of it lost over the long decline of Zoroastrianism after the Arab conquest of Persia, the remainder preserved chiefly through Parsi and Iranian Zoroastrian manuscript lines.
indexTerms: Michael Stausberg and Yuhan Sohrab-Dinshaw Vevaina (eds.)

title: The Axial Age
summary: The period between roughly 800 and 200 BCE when, independently and without evident contact, thinkers in China, India, Iran, and the Greek and Hebrew worlds developed the philosophical, religious, and ethical frameworks that still organize human civilization -- Confucianism, Buddhism, Zoroastrianism, philosophical monotheism, and Greek philosophy all emerging within a few centuries of each other.
aliases: The Axial Age as an Achievement of Liberation
The Hundred Schools as Axial Diversity

title: The Aztec Empire
summary: The Aztec Triple Alliance (c. 1428–1521) built a tributary empire, a sophisticated water-management infrastructure, and one of the largest cities in the world — before the Spanish conquest reduced it to rubble within two years. The entry asks what the Aztec state actually was as a political-economic structure, independent of how it ended.
indexTerms: Matos Moctezuma, Eduardo and Solís Olguín, Felipe
Anonymous Mexica scribes; commissioned under Viceroy Antonio de Mendoza

title: The Balance Sheet
summary: The accounting statement that captures what an entity owns and what it owes at a single moment in time — and, in doing so, became the foundational technology through which capital was measured, compared, and mobilized across the modern world.

title: The Balfour Declaration
summary: The 1917 statement in which the British government pledged support for ‘a national home for the Jewish people’ in Palestine, while promising not to prejudice the rights of the existing non-Jewish communities — then the large Arab majority, named only by what they were not. Just sixty-seven words in a letter from the Foreign Secretary, it never defined ‘national home’ and clashed with other wartime British promises. Folded into the Mandate, it became a founding document for the State of Israel and a founding grievance in the Palestinian national story.
aliases: The Promise of One Who Did Not Own
The Sentence That Could Not Hold

title: The Bangladesh Liberation War
summary: The 1971 war in which East Pakistan broke away from West Pakistan to become independent Bangladesh — a country built on a single religion that tore in two over language and political power. After the Pakistani army's crackdown on 25 March 1971 unleashed mass killing, rape, and the flight of roughly ten million refugees into India, Bengali guerrillas and an Indian military intervention forced the Pakistani surrender on 16 December 1971. The death toll is bitterly contested, ranging from official Bangladeshi figures of three million to independent estimates in the hundreds of thousands.
indexTerms: Sisson, Richard, and Leo E. Rose
Richard Sisson and Leo E. Rose

title: The Banknote
summary: The paper promise-to-pay that became money itself — a printed instrument whose value rests not on the worth of its material but on the trust that it can be redeemed or accepted by others. From the jiaozi of Song-dynasty China to the goldsmith receipts of seventeenth-century London, from notes redeemable for metal to pure fiat currency backed by nothing but the state, the banknote is one of the most successful confidence devices in economic history: a piece of paper that an entire society agrees to treat as wealth.
aliases: Paper as a Tool of the State

title: The Bantu Migrations
summary: The slow spread, over roughly two to three thousand years, of farming Bantu-speaking communities, later often associated with ironworking from a homeland near the modern Nigeria–Cameroon border across most of central, eastern, and southern Africa — a long, many-streamed process of migration, settlement, and intermarriage, driven by farming and later iron rather than conquest, that seeded a continent with one great family of related languages and laid the human groundwork for most of its later kingdoms, cities, and cultures.

title: The Basmachi Revolt
summary: The Basmachi Revolt was a decentralized Muslim insurgency against Soviet rule across Central Asia. Its roots lay in a 1916 revolt against wartime conscription, but the movement usually called Basmachi took shape only after the 1917 revolutions, and the major insurgency ran from about 1918 into the early 1930s. Fought by loosely allied local bands rather than a single army, it was worn down by a mix of Red Army pressure, amnesties and co-optation, intelligence and local alliances, economic and religious concessions, and later renewed coercion, and it was long remembered only through the label its enemies gave it.

title: The Battery
summary: A device that stores energy chemically and releases it as electric current — from Volta's pile of 1800 through lead-acid and lithium-ion cells to grid-scale storage — and now the linchpin of mobile technology and the renewable-energy transition.

title: The Battle of Adwa
summary: The day an African army beat a European empire and kept its country free
aliases: The proof that empire could be beaten

title: The Bazaar
summary: The bazaar — souk, covered market, urban caravanserai node — is not merely a place to buy things but an institution organized around relationships, reputation, and trust rather than fixed prices and anonymous exchange. From Istanbul's Grand Bazaar to Isfahan's Royal Bazaar to Cairo's Khan el-Khalili, these dense commercial spaces ran on guilds, extended credit, face-to-face bargaining, and the social weight of a merchant's name. The anthropologist Clifford Geertz called this the 'bazaar economy' — a distinct model of how trade can be organized when information is scarce and reputation is the currency that matters most.

title: The Beatles
summary: John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, and Ringo Starr — the four Liverpool musicians who, between 1960 and 1970, remade popular music. They honed their craft in Hamburg's clubs, led the 1964 British Invasion of America, and stopped touring in 1966 to work exclusively in the studio. The Lennon-McCartney partnership drove them from three-chord singles to the orchestrated complexity of Revolver and Sgt. Pepper's. Their debt to Black American blues and rock and roll is foundational. The 1970 breakup — and its causes — remains contested; the music is not.
aliases: The Stories We Tell About Them
associatedWorks: Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band

title: The Bengal Famine of 1943
summary: The mass starvation of roughly two to three million people in British-ruled Bengal during 1943 and 1944, in one of the most economically important — yet deeply unequal — provinces of the British Empire, in the middle of the Second World War. No single drought explains it. Its causes were a tangle of wartime disruption, the Japanese conquest of Burma, scorched-earth defense measures, inflation, market collapse, and slow colonial relief — and the question of British and Churchill-era responsibility remains fiercely debated.
aliases: A Failure of the Imperial Center

title: The Berlin Conference
summary: The 1884–85 meeting of fourteen nations — most of them European, along with the Ottoman Empire and the United States — convened by Bismarck, that set the diplomatic ground rules for the European partition of Africa — with no African ruler present — adopting the doctrine of effective occupation, opening the Congo basin to free trade, and granting international standing to King Leopold II's Congo project, soon his private Congo Free State, so that while it did not itself draw most of Africa's borders, it gave the Scramble for Africa its legal cover and accelerating logic.
aliases: The continent that had no seat
The laundering of conquest into law
indexTerms: Förster, Stig, Mommsen, Wolfgang J. and Robinson, Ronald (eds.)
Stig Förster, Wolfgang J. Mommsen and Ronald Robinson (eds.)

title: The Bhagavad Gita
summary: A philosophical dialogue embedded in the Indian epic Mahabharata, in which the god Krishna instructs the warrior Arjuna on duty, action, and the nature of the self — the foundational text of Hindu ethical and spiritual thought and one of the most widely read philosophical works in human history.
aliases: The Gita as Teaching on Non-Dual Knowledge
The Gita as a Defense of the Caste Order
The Gita as Philosophy of Action
indexTerms: Vyasa (attributed); critical edition ed. V. S. Sukthankar et al.

title: The Bhakti Movement
summary: A loose, centuries-long family of devotional movements that reshaped Hinduism through vernacular song. Sung in everyday languages rather than Sanskrit, it placed personal love of God above ritual and priestly mediation, and often above caste, making the divine reachable by the low-caste, the illiterate, and women — even as it was also carried by temple worship and royal patronage. Traced from the Tamil south of roughly the sixth century, it spread across India over the next thousand years and remains the dominant mode of popular Hindu worship.
aliases: Devotion Seen as a Modern Object
indexTerms: John Stratton Hawley and Mark Juergensmeyer

title: The Bible
summary: The compilation of texts — assembled over roughly a thousand years of composition, canonized through centuries of religious politics, translated into every major language, and used to justify slavery, liberation, empire, resistance, persecution, and redemption — that remains the most widely distributed and most contested book in human history

title: The Bill of Rights
summary: A short list of things the government is not allowed to do: silence your speech, dictate your worship, search your home without cause, or jail you without a fair trial. Drafted by Madison and ratified in 1791 as the price of the Constitution's acceptance, the first ten amendments wrote these limits into supreme law. At ratification they did not protect the enslaved, women, or Native peoples, and they bound only the federal government. The Fourteenth Amendment and twentieth-century court rulings slowly extended these guarantees against the states. The promise was written first; making it real took two centuries, and is not finished.
aliases: The United States Bill of Rights
The Promise Turned Into a Shield

title: The Bison
summary: The American bison, often called the buffalo, was the keystone animal of the North American grasslands and the material foundation of Plains Indigenous life. Tens of millions once shaped the prairie itself and sustained whole nations who used nearly every part of the animal. Within roughly two decades in the late nineteenth century, the herds were slaughtered almost to extinction in a collapse that was partly commercial and partly a deliberate weapon against the Plains peoples who depended on them. Saved from extinction by a small conservation effort, the bison survives today mostly in managed herds and stands as a symbol of both loss and restoration.

title: The Black Death
summary: The most lethal pandemic in recorded history — and the catastrophe that revealed what medieval European civilization was built on, and what it wasn't
indexTerms: Douglass C. North and Robert Paul Thomas

title: The Blues
summary: Forged around 1900 in the Mississippi Delta out of the work songs and field hollers that Black people carried out of slavery into the world of sharecropping and Jim Crow, the blues put individual feeling — loss, desire, trouble, endurance — at the center and found beauty in suffering. Its bent blue notes, twelve-bar form, and call-and-response traveled north with the Great Migration from Charley Patton and Robert Johnson to the electrified Chicago blues of Muddy Waters. From there it became the common root of jazz, rock and roll, soul, and much of hip-hop.

title: The Bond Market
summary: The marketplace where governments and corporations borrow by selling tradeable promises to repay with interest, and where the price of that borrowing is continuously set. The bond market turns a loan into a security that can change hands, prices a government's credibility every day, and exercises a discipline over states that the stock market does not — which is why it has often mattered more than the exchange where shares trade.
indexTerms: Reinhart, Carmen M.; Rogoff, Kenneth S.

title: The Book of Documents
summary: The Book of Documents, or Shujing (also called the Shangshu, 尚書), is one of the Five Classics: a collection of speeches, proclamations, and counsels the tradition attributes to sage-rulers and early kings, from the legendary Yao, Shun, and Yu to the founders of the Shang and Zhou. Its chapters span very different strata, from an early Zhou core to later, in part forged material. It is one of the foundational textual sources for the Mandate of Heaven — that rule depends on Heaven's conditional approval — above all in its Zhou proclamations, and it shaped Chinese thinking about legitimate rule and its overthrow for two thousand years.
aliases: The Book as a Charter of Conquest
indexTerms: trans. Martin Palmer, Jay Ramsay and Victoria Finlay

title: The Book of Songs
summary: The Book of Songs (Shijing) is the oldest anthology of Chinese poetry: 305 poems, with six titles preserved without text, composed between the 11th and 7th centuries BCE — from folk love songs to court odes and ritual hymns. One of the Confucian Five Classics, for two millennia it was central to elite education and memorized by generations of students, quoted as a shared moral and political language. Its poems are voiced as farmers, lovers, soldiers, and laborers, though whether they are folk songs, courtly compositions, or adapted performances — and who authored and collected them — remains disputed. It ranks among the East Asian canon's most intensively commented works.
aliases: The Poems as a Moral Mirror
The Airs as the People's Voice

title: The Bounty Mutiny
summary: The Bounty mutiny was the 1789 seizure of a small British naval ship by part of its crew, an act that cast its commander adrift in an open boat and drove the rebels to found a hidden island colony. It grew into one of the most retold sea stories in the English-speaking world, and its descendants still live on Pitcairn Island.
indexTerms: Charles Nordhoff and James Norman Hall

title: The Boxer Rebellion
summary: The anti-foreign uprising in north China (1899–1901) led by a secret society whose members believed that martial-arts ritual and spirit possession made them invulnerable to bullets. Amid drought, famine, and rage at a century of foreign domination, the Boxers killed Chinese Christians and foreign missionaries, besieged the foreign legations in Beijing, and were backed by the Qing court when the Empress Dowager Cixi declared war on the powers. An eight-nation army crushed them, sacked Beijing, and imposed the 1901 Boxer Protocol with its crushing indemnity. The rising is remembered in radically opposed ways, and its death tolls remain contested.
aliases: Resistance to a Century of Humiliation
indexTerms: Bickers, Robert, and R. G. Tiedemann (eds.)
Robert Bickers and R. G. Tiedemann (eds.)

title: The Bridge
summary: A structure that carries a path across an obstacle — and the purest expression of structural engineering: a visible diagram of how forces are tamed. Across history the span a society could build measured the materials and mathematics it commanded, while the bridges it chose to build reshaped trade, cities, and power.
indexTerms: Scanlan, Robert H. and Tomko, John J.

title: The British East India Company
summary: The private joint-stock trading company, chartered in 1600, that ended up conquering and governing the Indian subcontinent. It began as a venture seeking spices and textiles and became a corporate state: ruling some 200 million people, fielding a private army larger than Britain's own, collecting taxes, coining money, making war and treaty, and answering ultimately to its shareholders. It financed its conquests with Indian revenue and fought them with Indian soldiers, plundered Bengal, built the Indian opium system that fed the China trade, and governed India for a century before the British Crown took it over in the wake of the Rebellion of 1857.

title: The British Empire
summary: The largest empire in history by land area, encompassing at its peak in the 1920s approximately 25% of the world’s land surface and governing roughly 458 million people — whose creation, governance, and dissolution shaped the modern world’s economic structures, political institutions, national borders, and racial ideologies.

title: The Bronze Age Collapse
summary: The near-simultaneous destruction of every major civilization in the eastern Mediterranean around 1200 BCE — the Mycenaeans, the Hittites, the Egyptians, the Ugaritic trading city — ending the first great age of international trade and complex palace economies, and remaining one of history's most studied and least explained catastrophes.
indexTerms: Knapp, A. Bernard and Manning, Stuart W.
McAnany, Patricia A. and Yoffee, Norman (eds.)
Various (royal chanceries of Egypt and the Near East)

title: The Buganda Kingdom
summary: Buganda was the powerful centralized kingdom of the Baganda people on the northern shore of Lake Victoria, in present-day Uganda. Ruled by the kabaka through appointed chiefs rather than a purely hereditary aristocracy, it built an unusually flexible administration and a war fleet of canoes before any European arrived, expanded at the expense of its rival Bunyoro, and then negotiated and partly harnessed British colonialism — securing land and privilege through the 1900 Buganda Agreement and becoming the favored core of the Uganda Protectorate, even as it gave its name to the country it would be folded into.
aliases: The kingdom from inside the royal household
The kingdom as a partner to be managed
The kingdom as the neighbor that gained at your expense
indexTerms: Kaggwa, Apolo (trans. Ernest B. Kalibala, ed. May Mandelbaum Edel)

title: The Burmese–Siamese Wars
summary: The Burmese–Siamese Wars were a long series of conflicts between the Siamese kingdom of Ayutthaya and successive Burmese dynasties — the Toungoo, then the Konbaung — fought across the 16th to 18th centuries. They culminated in the Burmese capture and destruction of Ayutthaya in 1767, ending a kingdom more than four centuries old, and were followed by a Siamese recovery that founded Bangkok and the Chakri dynasty that still reigns in Thailand.
indexTerms: Richard D. Cushman (trans.), David K. Wyatt (ed.)

title: The Byzantine Empire
summary: The eastern continuation of the Roman Empire — centered on Constantinople, Christian in religion, Greek in language and culture, Roman in legal tradition and political identity — that survived the fall of the Western Empire by a thousand years, preserved and transmitted classical learning, shaped the civilizations of Eastern Europe and Russia, and fell to the Ottoman Turks in 1453, an event that shook the Christian world and sent Greek scholars westward to fuel the Renaissance.
aliases: A World on Its Own Terms

title: The Canal
summary: The constructed waterway — dug, embanked, and locked — through which civilizations have moved grain, goods, and armies for six thousand years. Canals come in two great families: irrigation channels that carry water to crops, and navigation canals that carry boats across terrain. Both are acts of will against geography, enormously expensive to build and maintain, and transformative where they succeed — rewriting the logic of distance, making agriculture possible in dry lands, and collapsing oceans for the ships that connect the modern world.

title: The Caravanserai
summary: A square of thick walls, a single gate tall enough for a loaded camel, a courtyard, water, and stabling — the caravanserai was the fortified roadside inn that made long-distance overland trade viable across the Islamic world and adjacent Asian corridors. Its power was repetition: way-stations spaced roughly a day's march apart turned a dangerous crossing of empty country into a chain of safe overnight halts. Built by states, pious donors, or rulers chasing trade, caravanserais sustained commerce, pilgrimage, and the movement of news for over a thousand years.

title: The Cell
summary: The basic unit of life — the smallest thing that is itself alive, a membrane-bounded packet of chemistry that takes in energy, responds to its surroundings, grows, and reproduces. Cell theory holds that all living things are made of cells, that the cell is life's basic unit, and that every cell comes from a pre-existing cell. That last claim, with later experiments by Pasteur and Tyndall, helped end the old belief in spontaneous generation and made biology a single science: the same machinery runs a bacterium, an oak, and a human, all of them descended in an unbroken line from the first cells.
aliases: The Brick All Life Is Built From
The Cell as Property and Person
indexTerms: Alberts, Bruce; Johnson, Alexander; Lewis, Julian; Morgan, David; Raff, Martin; Roberts, Keith; Walter, Peter

title: The Census
summary: The systematic counting and categorization of populations by the state — which began in ancient Mesopotamia and Egypt as a tool of taxation and military conscription, became the instrument through which modern states know, administer, and govern their populations, and reveals in its categories (race, religion, language, occupation) how states define who belongs, who counts, and on what terms.
indexTerms: Constitutional Convention of the United States

title: The Central Asian Khanates
summary: From roughly 1500, Shaybanid Uzbeks seized Transoxiana and held its ancient oasis cities — Bukhara the Noble, Timur's Samarkand — founding the three Muslim khanates that would rule Central Asia for four centuries. As ocean trade routes shifted global commerce, regional caravan networks persisted but the khanates grew more isolated and fought one another. The Great Game drew British and Russian attention; Russia conquered them piecemeal between 1865 and 1876. The Soviets abolished them in the 1920s and redrawn boundaries created the Central Asian republics still on the map today.
aliases: The Backward Land That Needed Saving
The Forbidden Cities of the East

title: The Chagatai Khanate
summary: The Chagatai Khanate was one of the four Mongol khanates that emerged as the empire of Genghis Khan divided among his sons — the Central Asian state that took shape around his death in 1227 to rule Transoxiana and the Tarim Basin. The united realm fragmented in the mid-14th century. Its western half was absorbed by Timur around 1370, while its eastern successors, the khans of Moghulistan, persisted into the seventeenth century. It carried the name of Chagatai, Genghis Khan's second son, and lent that name to a Turkic literary language that would outlive the khanate by centuries.
aliases: The Khanate as Patron and Protector
The Khanate as Betrayal of the Steppe
The Khanate as a Legitimacy to Be Borrowed

title: The Chernobyl Disaster
summary: The explosion and fire at Reactor No. 4 of the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in Soviet Ukraine on April 26, 1986 — the worst nuclear-power accident in history — which killed dozens in the immediate aftermath and released radioactive contamination across Europe, forcing the permanent evacuation of Pripyat, exposing the Soviet system's institutional pathologies, and accelerating the political dynamics that would end the USSR by 1991.

title: The Chilean Coup of 1973
summary: The violent overthrow of Chile's democratically elected socialist president, Salvador Allende, on 11 September 1973, when the armed forces under General Augusto Pinochet bombed the presidential palace and seized power. It opened seventeen years of dictatorship marked by torture, execution, and forced disappearance, and it made Chile one of the early laboratories of the free-market revolution that reshaped the global economy.
aliases: The Wound That Will Not Close
A Hemisphere on the Front Line
indexTerms: Comisión Nacional de Verdad y Reconciliación

title: The Chinese Civil War
summary: One of the longest and largest civil wars of the 20th century — a struggle for control of China between the Nationalists (Kuomintang) and the Chinese Communist Party that ran, on and off, for more than two decades. It opened in 1927 with Chiang Kai-shek's bloody purge of the Communists, was partly suspended and overshadowed by the Japanese invasion, and resumed full-scale after 1945. It killed millions and ended in 1949 with Mao Zedong's Communist victory, the founding of the People's Republic on the mainland, and the flight of the Nationalist government to Taiwan — a division across the Taiwan Strait that remains unresolved.

title: The Chola Empire
summary: The Tamil dynasty of southern India that, at its height between the tenth and twelfth centuries, turned a river delta into the base of a rare thing in Indian history — a great sea power, whose navy crossed the Bay of Bengal to raid Southeast Asia and whose temples still stand.
aliases: The Golden Age of the Tamils
indexTerms: Hermann Kulke, K. Kesavapany, and Vijay Sakhuja (eds.)

title: The Chola Raid on Srivijaya
summary: The Chola Raid on Srivijaya was a seaborne military campaign, launched around 1025 CE by the South Indian emperor Rajendra I, that struck the ports of the Srivijaya maritime empire across the Strait of Malacca and Sumatra. It stands out as one of the most striking long-range naval raids ever mounted by a South Asian state, sending a fleet clear across the Bay of Bengal to strike a distant power.
aliases: Chola naval expedition to Southeast Asia
The blow from over the horizon
indexTerms: Kulke, Hermann; Kesavapany, K.; Sakhuja, Vijay (eds.)
Hermann Kulke, K. Kesavapany, and Vijay Sakhuja (eds.)

title: The City
summary: The concentrated human settlement that made possible — and required — writing, taxation, professional armies, law, specialization of labor, long-distance trade, public health infrastructure, and organized religion, and that has housed an increasing share of humanity since its first appearance in Mesopotamia, becoming the dominant form of human settlement in the 21st century for the first time in history.

title: The Civil Rights Movement
summary: The organized campaign by African Americans and their allies between 1954 and 1968 to dismantle the system of legal racial segregation and disenfranchisement in the United States — achieving landmark legislation in the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 through nonviolent direct action, legal challenges, mass organizing, and sustained political pressure, while revealing the limits of legal reform in addressing the structural economic dimensions of racial inequality.

title: The Code of Hammurabi
summary: The collection of 282 laws carved onto a black basalt stele by the Babylonian king Hammurabi around 1754 BCE — one of the oldest and most complete legal texts to survive from the ancient world — that established graduated punishments based on class, codified the principle of proportional retribution, and presented law as a gift from the gods entrusted to a just king.
indexTerms: G.R. Driver and John C. Miles

title: The Coffeehouse
summary: Born in the fifteenth- and sixteenth-century Islamic world — above all Istanbul — and reaching London and other European cities by the mid-1600s, the coffeehouse sold a stimulant rather than a depressant, keeping its customers awake, sober, and talking. Out of London's coffeehouses came Lloyd's insurance market, the trading floors that seeded the stock market, and early newspapers. Jürgen Habermas called the coffeehouse the classic site of the bourgeois public sphere; historians since have qualified how open and egalitarian it actually was, and how thoroughly it excluded women.

title: The Cold War
summary: The geopolitical condition of superpower standoff that structured international relations for four decades — and felt, to those inside it, like it would never end

title: The Columbian Exchange
summary: The biological and cultural transfer between the Old World and the New World that followed Columbus's 1492 voyage — connecting previously isolated ecologies through the movement of crops, animals, diseases, and peoples, producing one of the most catastrophic population collapses in human history and simultaneously reshaping the agricultural foundations of civilization on every inhabited continent
aliases: Smallpox and the Fall of Tenochtitlan
Maize, Sweet Potatoes, and Chinese Population Growth
The Exchange That Enabled the Slave Trade

title: The Commercial Revolution
summary: Between roughly 1100 and 1700, Italian city-states invented the instruments of modern commerce — double-entry bookkeeping, the bill of exchange, marine insurance, deposit banking, the joint-stock company — and European trade expanded from local markets to oceanic routes. The Hanseatic League and the Champagne fairs knit regional economies; the Age of Exploration shifted commercial gravity from Venice to Amsterdam and London. These instruments were morally indifferent. The same ledgers, insurance policies, and company charters that built modern finance also underwrote colonial conquest and the Atlantic slave trade. The wealth and the violence grew from the same root.
aliases: The View from the Counting House
The View from the Other Side of the Ledger

title: The Commons
summary: A resource held and governed collectively — neither privately owned nor state-controlled — covering medieval English pastures, ocean fisheries, the atmosphere, open-source software, and the digital knowledge pool. Long dismissed as inherently prone to ruin, the commons was rehabilitated by Elinor Ostrom's Nobel Prize-winning research showing that communities can and do govern shared resources sustainably, making the commons a serious third option alongside private property and the state.

title: The Communist Manifesto
summary: The argument that compressed a theory of history, a critique of capitalism, and a political programme into 23 pages — and then became more powerful as a symbol than as a text
indexTerms: Marx & Engels (Penguin ed., intro. Eric Hobsbawm)

title: The Congo Crisis
summary: The violent collapse of order (1960–1965) that followed the Belgian Congo's hasty independence — an army mutiny, the secession of mineral-rich Katanga, the dismissal and assassination of the first prime minister Patrice Lumumba, with Belgian and Katangan involvement and documented U.S. plotting against him, the largest United Nations peacekeeping operation of its era, and a Cold War proxy struggle that ended with the rise of the dictator Mobutu. It became the defining case of foreign intervention in newly independent Africa, and of the gap between the flag of sovereignty and the substance of it.
aliases: Sovereignty stolen at the moment of its birth
A dangerous opening to be closed
The mission that tested what peacekeeping could do

title: The Congo Free State
summary: The vast central African territory that one man, King Leopold II of Belgium, ruled from 1885 to 1908 not as a nation but as personal property, running it as a forced-labor rubber-extraction regime whose violence and population loss made it one of the great catastrophes of the colonial era, and whose exposure produced the first great international human-rights campaign before the territory passed to the Belgian state.

title: The Congo River
summary: The great river that loops through Central Africa, the world's deepest recorded river and one of the largest by discharge, usually ranked second after the Amazon when counted as a single river — artery of the Congo rainforest, and the channel through which one of history's most brutal colonial extraction systems was driven, making its name a European byword for a darkness that was largely European-made.

title: The Congress of Vienna
summary: The peace conference of 1814-1815, hosted by Austria and steered by its foreign minister Metternich, where the powers that had defeated Napoleon redrew the map of Europe on two old principles, legitimacy and the balance of power, restoring monarchies the Revolution had toppled and arranging territory so no single state could dominate again. Conservatives remember it for buying Europe roughly a century without a general war; liberals and nationalists remember it as a graveyard of liberty that handed peoples between thrones and crushed the hopes of freedom and nationhood.
aliases: Final Act of the Congress of Vienna
The Architecture of a Lasting Peace

title: The Contract
summary: The legal and social institution through which two or more parties create binding obligations by mutual agreement — the mechanism that organizes most of market society, that has been extended metaphorically to explain political obligation and social order, and that illuminates the conditions under which voluntary exchange is genuinely voluntary.
indexTerms: Supreme Court of the District of Columbia (per Wright, J., U.S. Court of Appeals D.C. Circuit)

title: The Cook Voyages
summary: The Cook Voyages were three British expeditions that James Cook led across the Pacific Ocean between 1768 and 1779. Backed by the Royal Society and the Royal Navy, they mapped New Zealand, the east coast of Australia, and many Pacific islands with new accuracy, observed the 1769 transit of Venus, and disproved the idea of a habitable southern continent. They also opened the Pacific to European settlers, missionaries, traders, and the diseases that followed, making the voyages both a landmark of science and the leading edge of European empire in the Pacific.
aliases: The Triumph of Science and Seamanship
The Strangers Who Came Before the Settlers

title: The Cooperative
summary: An enterprise owned and governed by its members rather than outside shareholders — the organizational form that asks whether democratic self-governance can survive the pressures of market competition, and that has generated an alternative tradition of economic thinking running alongside capitalism for nearly two centuries.
indexTerms: Whyte, William Foote and Whyte, Kathleen King

title: The Corporation
summary: The legal form that allows a group of investors to pool capital under a single artificial person — shielding them from personal liability, enabling the accumulation of wealth at a scale no individual could sustain, and producing an institution whose power to shape law, labor, and politics has made it the defining economic structure of the modern world.
indexTerms: Berle, Adolf A.; Means, Gardiner C.
Jensen, Michael C.; Meckling, William H.
Blair, Margaret M.; Stout, Lynn A.
Azar, José; Schmalz, Martin C.; Tecu, Isabel

title: The Counter-Reformation
summary: The Counter-Reformation was the Catholic Church's roughly century-long response to the Protestant Reformation, running from the opening of the Council of Trent in 1545 to the Peace of Westphalia in 1648. It combined an internal reform of the clergy and doctrine with a combative campaign to define Catholic belief against Protestantism and to win back lost ground.

title: The Court
summary: The institution where disputes are decided and the law is applied to particular cases — the place where abstract rules meet real people. Courts transform force into legitimacy: they are how a society claims its punishments and judgments are justice rather than mere power. Spanning adversarial and inquisitorial traditions, trial by jury and trial by judge, local magistrates and international tribunals, the court is civilization's most durable answer to the problem of who decides.
aliases: The Rule of Law as Achievement
indexTerms: Supreme Court of the United States (Chief Justice John Marshall)

title: The Crusades
summary: A series of religious wars launched from Western Europe between 1095 and 1291 to recover Jerusalem and the Holy Land from Muslim rule — establishing Crusader states in the Levant for nearly two centuries, devastating Jewish communities in Europe en route, transforming the papacy, enriching the Italian city-states, and producing a memory that continues to structure political rhetoric on multiple sides.
aliases: The Frankish Invasions and the Response
The Crusades as Turning Point in Persecution

title: The Cuban Missile Crisis
summary: The thirteen-day confrontation between the United States and Soviet Union over nuclear missiles in Cuba — the closest the world has come to nuclear war, and the crisis that forced both superpowers to develop the tools for managing their mutual destruction.
aliases: A Defensive Deployment Misread as Aggression
Excluded from Decisions That Would Have Killed Them

title: The Cuban Revolution
summary: The uprising that turned a Caribbean island into a Cold War battlefield — a guerrilla war led by Fidel Castro that toppled the US-backed dictator Fulgencio Batista in 1959, turned Cuba, within a few years, into the Americas’ first communist state ninety miles from Florida, and survived invasion, embargo, and a nuclear standoff while dividing opinion ever since between a beacon of defiance and a prison island.
aliases: The Revolution as Liberation and Defiance
The Revolution as Betrayal and Prison
The Revolution as Symbol and Cautionary Tale

title: The Cultural Revolution
summary: The decade-long political upheaval that Mao Zedong launched in China in 1966, formally the Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution, to purge his rivals and reignite revolution by mobilizing the young against the country's teachers, officials, and traditions. Mao called on students organized as Red Guards to destroy the so-called Four Olds and to denounce authority everywhere, plunging China into years of terror, factional violence, and ruin. It ended only after Mao's death and the arrest of the Gang of Four in 1976. In 1981 the Communist Party itself declared it a catastrophe and assigned chief responsibility to Mao.

title: The Culture Wars
summary: The culture wars are a framework for understanding the enduring conflict in American public life over values rather than economics: over abortion, religion, sexuality, guns, family, and what schools teach. The sociologist James Davison Hunter gave the idea its influential form in 1991, arguing that Americans were dividing into two moral camps, which he called orthodox and progressive, that cut across the older lines of denomination and class.
aliases: The Defense of a Sacred Order
The Expansion of Dignity and Freedom
indexTerms: Fiorina, Morris P., with Samuel J. Abrams and Jeremy C. Pope
DiMaggio, Paul, Evans, John, and Bryson, Bethany

title: The Dam
summary: A barrier built across flowing water to store, control, and redirect it — one of humanity's oldest and most ambitious works, and the technology through which states have seized authority over rivers, delivering water, power, and flood safety to some while displacing, drying, or drowning others.

title: The Dao De Jing
summary: The foundational text of Taoism, attributed to the legendary sage Laozi and probably compiled in the fourth or third century BCE. In eighty-one brief chapters of compressed verse, the Dao De Jing articulates a philosophy of non-action, naturalness, and the ineffable source of all things. It is the most translated Chinese text in history, the foundational scripture of a religious tradition spanning two millennia, and a resource for governance, military strategy, and contemplative practice that has operated entirely independently of any recoverable biography.

title: The Database
summary: The organized, queryable store of structured data — and the quiet infrastructure beneath almost everything digital. From paper files and punch-card registers to Edgar Codd's relational model of 1970 and the SQL query language that followed, the database transformed record-keeping into a recombinant power. Any institution that can store, find, and join records can know and act on everyone in them at once. The bank balance, the airline reservation, the state registry, the credit score: all are database queries. So is the targeted advertisement and the watchlist.
aliases: The Engine of the Information Economy

title: The Declaration of Independence
summary: Drafted chiefly by Thomas Jefferson and adopted in July 1776, the Declaration announced American independence from Britain and grounded it in Enlightenment natural-rights philosophy: all men are created equal, governments derive just power from the consent of the governed. Its author enslaved hundreds; Congress struck a passage condemning the slave trade; the text itself called Native peoples savages. The promise was larger than the men who made it. Abolitionists, the women of Seneca Falls, Lincoln, and anti-colonial movements worldwide all took Jefferson's words and turned them against the powers that withheld what he had proclaimed.
aliases: The Unanimous Declaration of the Thirteen United States of America
The Promise Turned Against Its Makers
indexTerms: Jefferson, Thomas, and the Second Continental Congress

title: The Delhi Sultanate
summary: The first major and enduring Muslim-ruled state in the Indian subcontinent (1206–1526), founded by a Turkic former slave-soldier and ruled by five successive dynasties from Delhi. It planted Persianate Islamic high culture across a Hindu-majority land, repelled repeated Mongol invasions that devastated the rest of Asia, and is read today in sharply opposed ways.
indexTerms: Ibn Battuta (H. A. R. Gibb, trans.)
Asher, Catherine B., and Talbot, Cynthia
Catherine B. Asher and Cynthia Talbot

title: The Discovery of DNA's Structure
summary: The 1953 discovery at Cambridge that the DNA molecule is a double helix — two strands twined together whose paired chemical letters spell out and copy the instructions for life. James Watson and Francis Crick built the model, but their leap rested crucially on the X-ray crystallography of Rosalind Franklin, whose key image was shown to Watson without her knowledge. The breakthrough revealed how heredity works at the molecular level and helped found the science of molecular biology.
indexTerms: Watson, J. D.; Crick, F. H. C.
James D. Watson (eds. Gann and Witkowski)

title: The Discovery of Middle Eastern Oil
summary: The early-twentieth-century discovery of vast petroleum reserves beneath Persia, Iraq, and the Arabian Peninsula — and how it remade parts of the region, from small Gulf sheikhdoms to older states such as Iran and Iraq, into the strategic center of the world economy.

title: The Divine Comedy
summary: Dante Alighieri's epic poem of a journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Heaven — written in Italian in the early fourteenth century — which synthesized medieval Christian theology with classical learning, established the Italian vernacular as a literary language, and remains the most ambitious and technically accomplished work in the Western literary canon.

title: The Dogon
summary: The Dogon people of the Bandiagara Escarpment in Mali, a living culture famous for cliff-side villages, elaborate masks and funeral ceremonies, and an Indigenous religion that survived the spread of Islam and Christianity — and famous too for a contested claim, advanced by French ethnographers, that they held secret ancient knowledge of the star Sirius.
aliases: A People of the Cliffs and the Ancestors
The Search for an African Philosophy
The Mystery That Was Never There
indexTerms: Rogier M. A. Bedaux and others

title: The Dome of the Rock
summary: The golden-domed Umayyad shrine on the walled platform that Muslims call the Haram al-Sharif and Jews call the Temple Mount — one of the oldest surviving works of Islamic architecture, raised over a rock claimed as sacred by three faiths and contested by two peoples.

title: The Dreyfus Affair
summary: In 1894 France convicted a Jewish army captain, Alfred Dreyfus, of selling secrets to Germany on the basis of forged documents. When the real traitor was identified, the army buried the evidence. Émile Zola's open letter J'Accuse...! (1898) broke the cover-up into the open, splitting the country into two furious camps. Dreyfus was exonerated in 1906. The affair exposed the antisemitism running beneath a modern republic, crystallized the figure of the public intellectual, and convinced the journalist Theodor Herzl that Jewish assimilation in Europe had no future — driving his turn to political Zionism.
aliases: The View from the Defenders of the Army

title: The Dutch East India Company
summary: Chartered in 1602 and given the powers of a state — to wage war, sign treaties, mint coins, and govern territory in Asia — the VOC was a private company whose shares traded on the Amsterdam exchange in what is often called the first modern stock market. For two centuries it pursued a spice monopoly from its base at Batavia, enforcing it with force. In 1621 its governor-general destroyed most of the population of the Banda Islands to control the nutmeg supply. Corruption and debt dissolved it around 1799, but its joint-stock form became the template of the modern corporation.

title: The Dutch East Indies
summary: The Netherlands' colony in what is now Indonesia, born from the Dutch East India Company (the VOC) chartered in 1602 and lasting until Indonesian independence in 1949. A small European nation extracted the wealth of a vast tropical archipelago for three and a half centuries — first through what is often called the first great multinational corporation, then as a colonial state running forced-cultivation systems — and in the process drew the borders that became the modern Indonesian republic.
aliases: The First Multinational and Its Ledger
indexTerms: Oostindie, Gert; Frakking, Roel; Luttikhuis, Bart (eds.)

title: The Dutch Republic
summary: The Republic of the Seven United Provinces — which emerged from revolt against Spanish Habsburg rule in the late 16th century, became the world's leading commercial power through the 17th century, pioneered financial and commercial institutions that subsequent capitalism built on, and declined relative to Britain and France while establishing a model of tolerant republican governance that influenced European liberalism.
indexTerms: De Vries, Jan; Van der Woude, Ad

title: The Dzungar Khanate
summary: The Dzungar Khanate was an Oirat (western Mongol) khanate whose ruling elite adopted and patronized Tibetan Buddhism, ruling the grasslands of Dzungaria, in what is now northern Xinjiang, from the 1630s to the 1750s. Often called the last great nomadic empire of the steppe, it fought the Qing dynasty of China for three generations. Qing armies conquered it in 1755 and, after a Dzungar revolt, completed the destruction of the people and the khanate by 1758. Most Dzungars died in war and massacre or from the smallpox and famine the campaigns unleashed, and others fled abroad; a number of scholars characterize the killing as genocide.

title: The Electoral College
summary: The Electoral College is the indirect method the United States uses to elect its president: voters in each state choose electors, apportioned by that state's seats in Congress, and those electors cast the official votes. It is less a single design than a founding compromise that later party competition and winner-take-all state laws reshaped into something its authors would not recognize.
indexTerms: Hamilton, Alexander, James Madison, and John Jay

title: The Electric Grid
summary: The continent-spanning network of generators, transmission lines, transformers, and substations that carries electricity from where it is made to where it is used — commonly called the largest machine ever built — and now the central chokepoint of the transition to clean energy.
aliases: The Grid as Distributor of Access and Exclusion
The Grid as Real-Time Balancing Act

title: The Encomienda System
summary: The central labor institution of early Spanish America: a royal grant entrusting the labor and tribute of a group of indigenous people to a Spanish colonist, the encomendero, in exchange for a duty to protect them and instruct them in Christianity. In law the grant was of people's labor, not of land, and the indigenous were free vassals of the crown rather than property. In practice the system became a brutally coercive forced-labor regime that helped devastate the peoples it claimed to shelter, built the wealth of colonial Spain, and provoked one of history's first great debates over the rights of the conquered.
aliases: The Reward a Conqueror Was Owed
Free in Law, Bound in Fact

title: The English Civil War
summary: In January 1649 a reigning English king walked to a scaffold outside his own palace and was publicly beheaded by his subjects. Charles I had waged war against Parliament since 1642 over whether the crown ruled by divine right or was bound by law, with bitter religious conflict between Puritans and the king's church running through everything. Parliament's New Model Army defeated him, tried him as a tyrant, and executed him. The republic that followed collapsed into Cromwellian dictatorship, and the monarchy returned in 1660 — but the precedent that a king must answer to the law did not.
aliases: The Wars of the Three Kingdoms
The Birth of Government by Law
indexTerms: Charles I and the High Court of Justice

title: The Enlightenment
summary: The 18th-century intellectual movement that made reason the supreme authority — and whose legacy is still being fought over
indexTerms: Denis Diderot and Jean le Rond d'Alembert (eds.)
Max Horkheimer and Theodor W. Adorno

title: The Epic of Gilgamesh
summary: The ancient Mesopotamian epic poem about the king of Uruk — compiled in its most complete form around 1200 BCE from older Sumerian traditions — that tells of friendship, loss, and the search for immortality, and is among the oldest surviving works of literature, containing a flood narrative that predates and closely parallels the biblical story of Noah.
indexTerms: Al-Rawi, Farouk N.H.; George, Andrew R.

title: The Epic of Manas
summary: The Epic of Manas is the monumental Kyrgyz oral epic, the first and central part of a trilogy that continues with Manas's son Semetey and grandson Seytek. It tells how the hero Manas gathers the scattered Kyrgyz tribes into a single people and wins them back a homeland. Among the longest epic poems in the world, with the fullest recorded cycles of the trilogy running to roughly half a million lines, it survives not as a fixed book but as a living tradition that specialist reciters called manaschi rebuild in performance, never reciting it the same way twice.
aliases: A Living Case of Oral Composition
indexTerms: Nora K. Chadwick and Viktor Zhirmunsky

title: The Eruption of Mount Tambora
summary: The largest volcanic eruption in recorded history, which killed over seventy thousand people directly and caused the 'Year Without a Summer' of 1816 — disrupting agriculture across the Northern Hemisphere, driving famine and migration from Europe to North America, and demonstrating for the first time that a single geological event could alter global climate.
indexTerms: Klingaman, William K. and Klingaman, Nicholas P.

title: The Factory
summary: The institution of concentrated, machine-powered, clock-disciplined production — many workers brought under one roof and synchronized to mechanical rhythms — that did not merely speed up the making of goods but reorganized human time, the laboring body, and the relationship between a worker and their work.
aliases: The Factory at the End of the Supply Chain
indexTerms: Jenny Chan, Mark Selden, and Pun Ngai

title: The Fall of the Berlin Wall
summary: The opening of the Berlin Wall on November 9, 1989 — a miscommunication that became revolution — and the symbol around which the end of the Cold War, the collapse of European communism, and the reunification of Germany crystallized.

title: The Fatimid Caliphate
summary: The Isma'ili Shi'a caliphate that ruled from 909 to 1171 CE — rising in what is now Tunisia, conquering Egypt and founding Cairo and al-Azhar, the mosque that became one of the Islamic world's great centers of learning, claiming suzerainty over the holy cities, dominating the Red Sea trade, and ending when Saladin restored nominal Abbasid-Sunni allegiance in Egypt. For more than two centuries it offered a rival claim to lead all Muslims.

title: The Federal Minimum Wage
summary: Whether and how the federal floor on wages should be changed — the most extensively studied question in labor economics, with genuine methodological disputes and genuine value disagreements about how to weigh competing outcomes

title: The Federal Reserve
summary: The Federal Reserve is the central bank of the United States, created by Congress in 1913 to supply an elastic currency and act as a lender of last resort after a century of recurring bank panics. Built as twelve regional banks coordinated by a board in Washington, it now issues the nation's money and sets the interest rate that prices credit across the economy. Its independence from elected officials, and the reach of its power, have been contested since its founding.
indexTerms: Friedman, Milton and Schwartz, Anna Jacobson

title: The Federalist Papers
summary: The eighty-five newspaper essays that Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay published under the shared pen name Publius in 1787–88 to persuade New York to ratify the Constitution. Partisan work written at deadline, it outlasted its occasion to become the classic explanation of the Constitution’s design: No. 10, where Madison argues that a large republic tames faction by multiplying competing interests, and No. 51, on why ambition must be made to counteract ambition across separated powers. The Supreme Court now mines the Papers for original meaning, an authority their authors never claimed.
aliases: A Brief to Win a Vote
indexTerms: Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay

title: The Fijian Chieftaincy
summary: The Fijian chieftaincy is the indigenous iTaukei system of hereditary titled chiefs bound to their people through the vanua, the web of land, kinship, and obligation that organizes iTaukei society. Once the framework of many independent island polities, it was formalized and frozen by British colonial rule after 1874 and remains a contested force in Fiji's politics today.
aliases: The Chief as the Living Land
The View from the Cane Fields
Tradition Written in a Colonial Office

title: The First World War
summary: A war fought between two alliance systems across Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and the oceans — the first industrial-scale conflict in history, which killed roughly 20 million people, destroyed four empires, redrew the political map of the world, and created the conditions for the Second World War.
aliases: The Stab in the Back and Its Consequences

title: The Forbidden City
summary: The vast walled palace at the heart of Beijing, built between 1406 and 1420 under the Ming dynasty's Yongle Emperor as the earthly mirror of cosmic order, from which twenty-four emperors ruled China for nearly five centuries — a forbidden inner world that ordinary people could not enter without permission, and now, emptied of its emperors, one of the most visited museums on earth.
indexTerms: Jeannette Shambaugh Elliott with David Shambaugh

title: The Forest
summary: The ecosystem that sheltered, fed, and equipped the human species for most of its existence — and whose systematic destruction over the past ten thousand years has been both the precondition for civilization and one of civilization's most consequential long-term costs.

title: The Form
summary: The printed document with blanks to fill in — the cell-level unit of modern bureaucracy through which states and large organizations see, sort, and process people. The form standardizes a person into data, determines who receives benefits and recognition, and encodes in its very categories the political question of who counts. No other technology of modernity is so common, so ignored, or so quietly decisive about identity and access.
indexTerms: Herd, Pamela and Moynihan, Donald P.

title: The Founding of Israel
summary: The establishment of the State of Israel in 1948, set in motion by the 1947 United Nations recommendation to partition British-ruled Palestine and sealed by the war that followed. David Ben-Gurion declared independence on 14 May 1948; neighbouring Arab states invaded; the war ended with an Israeli state larger than the partition lines. The same event is remembered by Israelis as the War of Independence and by Palestinians as the Nakba, the catastrophe in which around 700,000 people fled or were expelled, a double meaning that has shaped the region ever since.

title: The Four Noble Truths
summary: The Four Noble Truths are the framework at the center of Buddhism, a four-part analysis of why beings suffer and how they might not: that ordinary existence is pervaded by dukkha; that dukkha arises from craving; that craving, and so the suffering it feeds, can be brought to an end; and that a practical discipline, the Noble Eightfold Path, leads to that end. They are less a creed to be believed than a sequence to be worked through, and they remained the common inheritance of every school that later divided Buddhism.

title: The French Revolution
summary: The decade that relocated sovereignty from monarch to nation, invented the left-right political spectrum, produced the first systematic justification of state terror in the name of popular sovereignty, and established the template for every political revolution that followed
aliases: The Universal Claim and Its Problems

title: The Frigate Bird and Stick Charts
summary: The frigate bird and stick charts are two of the instruments behind traditional Pacific wayfinding — the system of memorized stars, wave patterns, and animal signs that let sailors cross open ocean and find islands with no compass, chart, or clock. Marshall Islands navigators built stick charts specifically to model ocean swells; voyagers across the wider Pacific read seabirds, clouds, and drift for the same purpose. Together they represent one of the most sophisticated navigation systems developed anywhere before mechanical instruments existed.

title: The Fur Trade
summary: The vast commercial system that linked Indigenous hunters and middlemen across North America to European markets through the exchange of furs — above all beaver — for manufactured goods. For two centuries it was a central engine of colonial expansion, ecological change, and Indigenous transformation, built on Indigenous knowledge and diplomacy as much as European capital.
aliases: The Trade as Diplomacy and Strategic Resource
The Value of the Middle Position
A People Built by the Trade, Abandoned by Its Successor

title: The Galápagos Islands
summary: A remote volcanic archipelago on the equator in the Pacific, about a thousand kilometers west of Ecuador, whose giant tortoises, marine iguanas, and island-by-island birds gave the young Charles Darwin evidence that, years later, helped him build the theory of evolution by natural selection. The islands' isolation bred a wealth of species found nowhere else, made them a living laboratory and a place of scientific pilgrimage, and turned them into a flagship of conservation now threatened by tourism, introduced species, and a growing human population.
aliases: The Myth of the Sudden Insight
indexTerms: Peter R. Grant and B. Rosemary Grant
B. Rosemary Grant and Peter R. Grant

title: The Ganges
summary: The river that runs about 2,500 kilometers from the Himalayas across the northern plain of India and on into Bangladesh, where it joins the Brahmaputra and Meghna to reach the Bay of Bengal — worshipped by Hindus as the living goddess Ganga, depended on by hundreds of millions for water and farming, and ranked among the most polluted rivers on earth.
