A single channel that is at once the holiest river in Hinduism and a grave environmental crisis.

title: The Garifuna
summary: An Afro-Indigenous people of the Caribbean coast of Central America — chiefly Honduras, Belize, Guatemala, and Nicaragua, with a large diaspora in the United States. The Garifuna (or Garínagu) formed on the island of St. Vincent from the mixing of Indigenous Island Caribs with Africans, were deported by the British in 1797 after years of resistance, and carried across the sea an Indigenous language, a distinctive music and spirituality, and a way of life that UNESCO named a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity.
aliases: A People Born at Yurumein and Carried Across the Sea
The Black Caribs to Be Removed
Ethnogenesis and a Culture of Movement

title: The Gear
summary: A toothed wheel that meshes with another to transmit rotation without slipping — and by trading the size of the wheels, trades speed for force or force for speed. Gears let a small effort move a great load, turned falling weights into regulated intervals inside mechanical clocks, and became the hidden grammar of nearly every machine from the water mill to the automobile gearbox.
indexTerms: Steven C. Frautschi, Richard P. Olenick, Tom M. Apostol, and David L. Goodstein

title: The Ghaznavid Empire
summary: A Turkic slave-soldier dynasty that ruled from Ghazni in Afghanistan from about 977 to 1186 CE, founded by Sebuktigin and dominated by his son Mahmud of Ghazni. It became famous in two opposite ways: as the raiders who first carried sustained Muslim campaigns deep into northern India, and as one of the most brilliant patrons of Persian high culture. Mahmud's campaigns into the Indian plains, above all the sack of the temple of Somnath, planted Muslim rule in the Punjab and are remembered today in sharply contested ways.

title: The Gift Economy
summary: A system of exchange organized through giving, receiving, and reciprocating rather than price and contract. Studied through the Kula ring, the potlatch, blood donation, and open-source software, the gift economy reveals that generosity is rarely free: a gift creates a social bond, an obligation, and sometimes a hierarchy — making it not the opposite of economics but a different economics, one that builds relationships rather than settling them.
indexTerms: Bruno S. Frey and Felix Oberholzer-Gee

title: The Gilded Age and the Robber Barons
summary: The Gilded Age is the name given to the decades of explosive American industrial growth, roughly the 1870s to 1900, when railroads, steel, and oil built the modern economy and a handful of businessmen amassed fortunes on a scale never seen before. It was an era of dazzling wealth alongside crowded slums, brutal labor, and open political corruption. The name comes from an 1873 satirical novel by Mark Twain and Charles Dudley Warner, mocking a society that glittered on the surface and was corrupt beneath, and the tycoons who dominated it are still remembered by two rival names: robber barons and captains of industry.
aliases: the age of the robber barons
The era as the building of a modern nation
The era from the people it ground down
The era as the birth of the corporate order
indexTerms: Mark Twain and Charles Dudley Warner

title: The Glorious Revolution
summary: The overthrow of England's Catholic king James II in 1688, accomplished by a Dutch invasion under William of Orange that a circle of Protestant English elites had invited, and remembered as the moment the English crown became a creature of Parliament and the law. The 1689 Bill of Rights fixed the terms: a monarch could no longer suspend laws, tax, or keep an army without Parliament's consent, and no Catholic could reign. It is called glorious and bloodless because it spared England a civil war, but that label holds only for England. The same takeover drowned Ireland in a bloody war and began centuries of Protestant ascendancy and Catholic grievance.
aliases: The Crown Brought Under the Law
The Glory That Was Our Defeat
A Move in the War Against France

title: The Göktürks
summary: The first empire to call itself Turk — a confederation of nomadic horse-warriors that erupted out of Mongolia around 552 CE and within a generation ruled the steppe from Manchuria to the borders of Persia and Byzantium. Founded by the Ashina clan under Bumin Qaghan, the Turkic Khaganate gave the name 'Turk' to history, opened long-distance diplomacy from China to Constantinople over the silk trade, and left the Orkhon inscriptions, the oldest surviving texts in any Turkic language. It helped define a model of steppe statecraft that later Turkic and Mongol powers would inherit and adapt.
indexTerms: Bilge Qaghan, Kul Tigin, and Tonyukuk (commissioners)

title: The Gold-Salt Trade
summary: The trans-Saharan exchange in which West African gold flowed north across the desert and Saharan and northern salt flowed south — the commercial engine that built the empires of Ghana, Mali, and Songhai and made cities like Timbuktu rich.
aliases: The Wealth That Came to the Throne
The Marvels of the Land of Gold

title: The Golden Age of Piracy
summary: The burst of intense maritime robbery, roughly the 1650s to 1730, that swept the Caribbean, the Atlantic, and the Indian Ocean. The entry treats piracy not as a world apart but as the violent shadow of empire — licensed in wartime, hunted in peace — and weighs the romantic pirate of fiction against the thinner, harder record.
aliases: Piracy as the tax on treasure
Piracy as escape from the lower deck

title: The Golden Horde
summary: The Golden Horde was the northwestern division of the Mongol Empire — the khanate that ruled the western steppe from its capital at Sarai on the Volga and held the Rus' principalities in tribute from the 1240s. Its overlordship profoundly conditioned the rise of Muscovy and politics across the Rus' lands. Its last core, the Great Horde, was destroyed in 1502, but the Crimean, Kazan, and Astrakhan khanates carried the Jochid line on into the sixteenth century.

title: The Goryeo Dynasty
summary: The Goryeo Dynasty (918–1392 CE) gave Korea its name and one of its great cultural flowerings — a Buddhist kingdom that reunified the peninsula, made celadon counted among the finest ceramics ever produced, carved the entire Buddhist canon onto more than eighty thousand woodblocks as a prayer against the Mongols, and printed with movable metal type generations before Gutenberg.
aliases: The kingdom that carved a canon
Goryeo as a Buddhist order to be replaced
indexTerms: Seckel, Dietrich, and Korean ceramics scholarship (survey)

title: The Gothic Cathedral
summary: Arising in northern France around 1140 and spreading across Europe by 1500, the Gothic cathedral solved a structural problem and turned the solution into theology. Pointed arches, ribbed vaults, and flying buttresses transferred the roof's weight onto an external skeleton of stone, freeing the walls to be dissolved into stained glass. The interior of Chartres or Amiens soars far overhead and glows from every side. These buildings were the work of generations — often a century or more — funded by church, towns, guilds, and pilgrims, and many were never finished as planned.
aliases: The Machine for Making Heaven Visible
The Transfer of Wealth and Power
Experiments at the Edge of Stone

title: The Graeco-Arabic Translation Movement
summary: The sustained, court- and elite-sponsored effort, centered in Abbasid Baghdad from roughly the eighth to the tenth century, to translate Greek scientific and philosophical works (along with some Persian and Indian material) into Arabic — drawing especially on Syriac-speaking Christian translators, alongside Muslim, Jewish, and other scholars, enabled by cheap paper and Abbasid wealth, and feeding the scholarship later called the Islamic Golden Age, much of which returned to Latin Europe centuries afterward.
indexTerms: Jon McGinnis and David C. Reisman

title: The Grand Canal
summary: The man-made river that held China together — a thousand-mile spine of canals dug and maintained by vast numbers of conscripted laborers to carry the grain of the rich southern rice lands to the hungry capitals of the north, widely called the world’s longest canal, and among the oldest large artificial waterways still in use.
aliases: The Cost Paid in the Digging
indexTerms: Sima Guang and the Tang official histories (as transmitted)

title: The Great Depression
summary: The most severe global economic crisis of the 20th century — triggered by the Wall Street Crash of 1929, propagated by bank failures, trade collapse, and deflationary spirals, and ending only with World War II mobilization — which permanently changed how governments understood their responsibility for economic stability and generated both the New Deal and the political conditions for fascism.

title: The Great Game
summary: The Great Game was the rivalry between the British and Russian empires over Central Asia and Afghanistan, conventionally dated from about 1830 to 1907, though historians dispute its dates and whether it was ever a single coherent contest. Britain feared Russia's absorption of the Central Asian khanates would open a path toward British India, and both competed through invasions, espionage, secret surveying, and buffer-state diplomacy. The 1907 Anglo-Russian Convention formalized spheres of influence and eased the acute rivalry rather than permanently settling it. The name is a Eurocentric frame that pushes the Afghan and Central Asian peoples, whose lands were the board, to the margins of their history.
indexTerms: Karl E. Meyer and Shareen Blair Brysac

title: The Great Irish Famine
summary: The catastrophic failure of the Irish potato crop from 1845 to 1852, which killed approximately one million people and drove another million to emigrate within a year — reducing Ireland's population by a quarter and setting in motion a century of emigration that permanently transformed both Ireland and the Irish diaspora.

title: The Great Leap Forward
summary: Mao Zedong’s campaign of 1958–1962 to vault China into communism almost overnight, through giant rural communes and mass-mobilized labor. It collapsed into the deadliest famine in recorded history — an estimated 15 to 45 million dead — a catastrophe the Chinese state concealed at the time and still suppresses.
aliases: The Anatomy of a Policy Famine

title: The Great Migration
summary: The decades-long movement, roughly from 1910 to 1970, in which an estimated six million Black Americans left the rural Jim Crow South for the industrial cities of the North, Midwest, and West — one of the largest internal migrations in American history. It was a mass act of self-liberation that turned a Southern, rural Black population into a largely urban one, built the great Black communities and political power of cities such as Chicago, Detroit, and New York, and carried Southern Black culture into the national mainstream, even as the migrants met new walls of segregation, hostile unions, and racial violence in the places they fled to.
aliases: Labor, Capital, and the Collapse of Cotton
The Promised Land That Was Not
indexTerms: Tolnay, Stewart E.; Beck, E. M.

title: The Great Mosque of Mecca and the Hajj
summary: The Sacred Mosque (al-Masjid al-Haram) in Mecca, commonly described as the largest mosque in the world, and the pilgrimage it gathers — the Hajj, one of the Five Pillars of Islam, a duty owed once in a lifetime by every able and able-to-afford Muslim. At its center stands the Kaaba, the cube toward which Muslims everywhere turn to pray, and around it each year flows one of the largest recurring gatherings of humanity on earth, a coordinated rite of consecration, circling, and standing that erases rank and binds a global community for a few sacred days.
aliases: The Pilgrimage as System and Arena

title: The Great Plains
summary: The vast grassland running down the center of North America — from the Canadian prairies to Texas — a system built by grass, fire, drought, and grazing that produced one of history's most dynamic mobile cultures after the horse arrived, was stripped of its defining animal and violently remade through the dispossession and confinement of Plains nations within a single human lifetime, and was then nearly destroyed as farmland by the same forces of soil and sky that had always ruled it.
aliases: The Grassland as Home and Sovereign Territory
The Promise and Failure of the Agricultural Frontier

title: The Great Wall of China
summary: Not a single wall but a succession of walls built, abandoned, and rebuilt across two thousand years by different Chinese states for different purposes — the rammed-earth barriers of the Qin, the Han extensions that guarded the Silk Road, and the famous stone-and-brick Ming fortifications — a military barrier that rarely stopped determined invaders but became, centuries later, one of the most powerful symbols of national identity in the modern world.

title: The Great Wave off Kanagawa
summary: A color woodblock print designed by Katsushika Hokusai around 1831, the most famous sheet in his series Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji. A great clawed breaker of blue and white rears over three slender fishing boats while, far off and tiny, Mount Fuji sits serene. Cheap popular art in its day, printed in many impressions and colored with the then-new imported pigment Prussian blue, it later poured into Europe after Japan reopened and helped ignite Japonisme. It has since traveled further than perhaps any picture ever made, becoming a global icon reproduced on everything from gallery walls to the Japanese banknote.

title: The Greco-Persian Wars
summary: A series of conflicts, fought roughly between 499 and 449 BCE, in which the Achaemenid Persian Empire tried twice to bring the Greek city-states of the Aegean under its control and failed — producing the Athenian victory at Marathon, the famous stand at Thermopylae, the naval triumph at Salamis, and the rise of Athens and its Delian League, while seeding a lasting Western myth of free Greeks against an Eastern despot that the historical record only partly supports.
aliases: A Frontier That Did Not Hold

title: The Green Revolution
summary: The mid-twentieth-century transformation of farming, from roughly the 1940s to the 1970s, that spread high-yield varieties of wheat and rice, synthetic fertilizer, and intensive irrigation across the developing world. Where it took hold, harvests doubled or tripled, and the new methods are widely credited with averting predicted famines and saving hundreds of millions, perhaps a billion, from starvation. The same methods also drained groundwater, degraded soil, narrowed crop diversity, and favored the larger farmers who could afford the inputs, leaving a dual legacy still debated today.
aliases: The View from the Research Institute
The View from the State Department

title: The Gulag
summary: The Gulag was the Soviet Union's system of forced-labor camps, run by the secret police, through which millions of prisoners passed between roughly 1930 and the mid-1950s. Its name is an acronym for the government office that managed the camps, and it came to stand for the whole world of Soviet forced labor: the camps, the sentences, and the literature of survival written by those who lived through them. The system reached its height under Joseph Stalin and was dismantled in the years after his death in 1953.

title: The Gupta Empire
summary: The north Indian empire whose two centuries of relative political stability produced one of the most concentrated periods of intellectual and artistic achievement in world history — the period retrospectively called the Golden Age of India.
aliases: India as Source of the Dharma

title: The Habsburg Empire
summary: The dynastic empire that the Habsburg family gathered across six centuries, as much by marriage and inheritance as by war, until it ruled the heart of Europe — a multiethnic patchwork of a dozen peoples held together by the dynasty, the Catholic Church, the army, and the bureaucracy, which finally broke apart in 1918 on the force of nationalism and left a fractured Central Europe behind.

title: The Hadith
summary: The vast body of reported words, deeds, and silent approvals of the Prophet Muhammad — transmitted through chains of named witnesses, sifted by an elaborate science of authentication, and gathered into canonical collections — that stands after the Quran as the second source of Islamic law and practice for mainstream Sunni and Shia Islam, though the accepted collections differ.

title: The Hagia Sophia
summary: The great domed church of Holy Wisdom in Constantinople, completed in 537 CE under the emperor Justinian I — for most of nearly a thousand years the cathedral of the Eastern Orthodox world, interrupted by Latin Catholic rule after the Fourth Crusade, then an Ottoman mosque after 1453, a secular museum under the Turkish republic from the 1930s, and a working mosque again since 2020, making the single building a record of every power that has claimed the city.
aliases: A Monument Belonging to the World
indexTerms: Mark, Robert, and Çakmak, Ahmet Ş. (eds.)

title: The Haitian Revolution
summary: The only successful slave revolution in history — a thirteen-year uprising in the French colony of Saint-Domingue that destroyed the most profitable plantation economy in the world, killed or expelled the colonial ruling class, defeated the armies of France, Britain, and Spain, and produced Haiti, the first Black republic and the first nation founded by formerly enslaved people.
aliases: The Founding Myth and Its Complications
The Terror That Shaped American Slavery
indexTerms: Catherine Porter, Constant Méheut, Matt Apuzzo, and Selam Gebrekidan

title: The Han Dynasty
summary: The second imperial dynasty of China — the political and cultural formation that established the institutional, philosophical, and ethnic foundations of Chinese civilization as it has persisted for over two thousand years.
aliases: Han and Rome as Parallel Formations

title: The Hanseatic League
summary: The loose league of German-speaking trading towns that, for some four centuries without a king or a standing army of its own, dominated the key trade routes and privileges of the Baltic and North Sea, set the terms of northern Europe's trade, and showed how merchants alone could wield the power of a state.

title: The Hawaiian Kingdom
summary: The Hawaiian Kingdom (1795–1898) was the sovereign island monarchy that united Hawai'i under one crown, won recognition as an independent nation, and modernized on its own terms across a century of missionaries, traders, and empires. Founded by Kamehameha I, it adopted Christianity, a written language, and a constitution, while introduced disease collapsed the Native Hawaiian population. In 1893 a small group of mostly American planters, backed by US Marines, overthrew Queen Lili'uokalani; the United States annexed Hawai'i in 1898. The entry holds together the kingdom's genuine statecraft, the catastrophe of disease, the planter coup, and the still-living sovereignty movement.
aliases: The kingdom as obstacle and opportunity
The nation that was taken, not lost

title: The Hebrew Bible
summary: The collection of ancient Israelite and Jewish texts — comprising Torah, Prophets, and Writings — composed and compiled over roughly five centuries, that became the founding scripture of Judaism, the Old Testament of Christianity, and a primary source for Islam, shaping the moral, legal, and literary imagination of Western civilization more than any other single work.
indexTerms: Israel Finkelstein and Neil Asher Silberman

title: The Heian Period
summary: The Heian period (794–1185 CE) was the era when Japan, having borrowed its capital, script, and statecraft from China, turned inward and produced a culture distinctively its own. A small court aristocracy, insulated from warfare and increasingly distant from provincial power, made refinement itself into the business of life — and its women, writing in a new native script, produced what many consider the world's first psychological novel.
aliases: The court seen from the countryside
The court as lost golden age
indexTerms: Shively, Donald H., and William H. McCullough, eds.
Adolphson, Mikael, Edward Kamens, and Stacie Matsumoto, eds.
Donald H. Shively and William H. McCullough (eds.)
Mikael Adolphson, Edward Kamens, and Stacie Matsumoto (eds.)

title: The Herero and Nama Genocide
summary: The destruction of the Ovaherero and Nama peoples by imperial Germany in its colony of German South-West Africa, present-day Namibia, between 1904 and 1908 — a colonial war of conquest that turned into a deliberate campaign to wipe out two peoples, complete with an explicit extermination order and a system of concentration camps. Widely described as the first genocide of the twentieth century, it killed a very large share of the Herero and roughly half the Nama, though exact figures are estimated and debated. Long neglected, it became the subject of an argument over whether it prefigured later European mass violence, and was officially recognized by Germany only in 2021.
indexTerms: Olusoga, David and Casper W. Erichsen
Administrator's Office, Union of South Africa
David Olusoga and Casper W. Erichsen

title: The Himalayas
summary: The highest mountain range on earth, raised by the collision of the Indian and Asian plates, that walls off northern India, traps the monsoon, and feeds the great rivers on which well over a billion people depend — a barrier, a water tower, and a holy landscape all at once.

title: The Hittite Empire
summary: The great Bronze Age power of Anatolia, ruling from Hattusa from roughly 1650 to 1180 BCE. One of the great powers of the Late Bronze Age Near East, alongside Egypt, Babylonia, Mitanni, and later Assyria, the Hittites were the speakers of the oldest written Indo-European language and the makers of the earliest surviving international peace treaty whose text is known in detail, sealed after the Battle of Kadesh. Their civilization was so completely forgotten that it had to be recovered by archaeology and decipherment in the modern age.
aliases: The King and the Land of a Thousand Gods
The Empire Recovered from the Ground

title: The Holocaust
summary: The systematic, state-organized persecution and murder of six million Jews and millions of others by the Nazi regime and its collaborators between 1933 and 1945 — the most extensively documented genocide in history.
indexTerms: Reich Security Main Office (Reinhard Heydrich, convener)
Deborah Dwork and Robert Jan van Pelt

title: The Holy Roman Empire
summary: The eight-century political contraption at the heart of Europe that claimed to inherit ancient Rome and the blessing of God, but was really a loose patchwork of hundreds of territories, mostly German but reaching at times into Italy, the Low Countries, and Bohemia, under an elected emperor with little central power — mocked by Voltaire as "neither holy, nor Roman, nor an empire," yet the durable frame of Central European law, dynasty, and identity for eight centuries.
aliases: Holy Roman Empire of the German Nation
The crown that did not command

title: The Homestead Act
summary: Two true things describe the same law. The Homestead Act of 1862 gave 160 acres of federal land, nearly free, to any settler willing to live on it for five years — including single women, immigrants, and, after the Civil War, the formerly enslaved. For millions shut out of ownership, this was real opportunity. The land it gave was the homeland of Indigenous nations, declared "public domain" only after the United States had taken it by treaty, war, and removal — processes the promise of free land then accelerated. The Act democratized landownership for settlers by distributing land that was not the government's to give until it had been seized.
aliases: The Land That Was Never Empty

title: The Hospital
summary: The institution through which organized societies have concentrated the sick, administered professional knowledge, and managed the boundary between life and death — an institution that transformed from a charitable shelter for the poor and dying into the central organizing structure of modern medicine, reshaping the relationship between patient and healer, individual and state, body and professional authority.

title: The House of Wisdom
summary: The Abbasid institution known as Bayt al-Hikma, tied in popular memory to a grand state academy of translators in ninth-century Baghdad — but which careful scholarship suggests was most likely a far more modest caliphal palace library and translation bureau, possibly tied especially to Persian administrative and astrological material. The entry is an anatomy of how a real but smaller institution becomes a civilizational myth, and why the smaller truth may be the more impressive one.

title: The Household Economy
summary: The idea that the home is a site of real economic production — cooking, cleaning, raising children, caring for the sick — and that the national accounts which measure a country's wealth systematically leave this work out, rendering invisible the labor that makes all other labor possible.
aliases: The Economy Has Always Had Two Halves
Where the Household Is the Economy
The Unwaged Work Beneath Waged Work
indexTerms: Mariarosa Dalla Costa and Selma James
European Commission, IMF, OECD, UN, World Bank

title: The Human Genome Project
summary: Thirteen years of international science produced a draft of all three billion chemical letters of human DNA, announced at the White House in June 2000. A publicly funded consortium spanning six countries raced a private competitor, Celera, over whether the sequence should be free or owned. The genome founded genomics and crashed the cost of reading DNA. It also humbled its authors: humans carry only around twenty thousand protein-coding genes, fewer than some plants, and the genome proved far harder to interpret than to spell. Lasting questions about genetic privacy and the patenting of human genes arrived with the sequence.
aliases: The View from the Public Project
The View from the Ethics Program

title: The I Ching
summary: The I Ching, or Book of Changes, is one of the oldest Chinese classics: at its core a Western Zhou divination manual of sixty-four hexagrams, each a figure of six broken or unbroken lines. Only centuries later did a set of commentaries, the Ten Wings, read those omens as a cosmology of yin, yang, and ceaseless change, turning the oracle into a treatise on change itself. Enrolled among the Five Classics under the Han, it has been consulted, in evolving forms, for roughly three millennia, at once a working oracle and a book of wisdom.
aliases: The Book as Answer to a Modern Lack
indexTerms: Richard Wilhelm and Cary F. Baynes
Kidder Smith, Peter K. Bol, Joseph A. Adler, and Don J. Wyatt
trans. Richard Wilhelm and Cary F. Baynes

title: The Ice Age
summary: The recurring deep freezes of the last 2.6 million years, when ice sheets kilometers thick advanced over the northern continents and the seas fell hundreds of feet — dozens of cold-warm cycles paced by slow wobbles in Earth’s orbit and amplified by feedbacks in ice and carbon. We live in a warm pause between them, the Holocene, whose mild stability made farming and civilization possible; the glacial record also shows how sharply the planet responds to a shift in its carbon balance.
aliases: The signal in the mud and the ice
The cold world that made us
indexTerms: Imbrie, John and Imbrie, Katherine Palmer
Hays, J. D., Imbrie, John, and Shackleton, N. J.
Martin, Paul S. and Klein, Richard G. (eds.)
John Imbrie and Katherine Palmer Imbrie
Paul S. Martin and Richard G. Klein (eds.)
Bjørn G. Andersen and Harold W. Borns Jr.

title: The Icelandic Sagas
summary: The body of prose narratives written in Iceland between roughly 1200 and 1400 CE — describing the settlement of Iceland, the feuds of its first generations, the Norse discovery of North America, and the lives of Scandinavian kings — that constitute the founding achievement of secular prose fiction and the most detailed literary record of any early medieval society.

title: The Iliad
summary: The oldest surviving Western epic — Homer's account of a few weeks in the last year of the Trojan War — that established war, heroism, honor, grief, and fate as permanent themes of Western literature and whose portrait of Achilles remains the most complex representation of the relationship between glory and mortality in the ancient world
aliases: Omeros — The Iliad in the New World

title: The Ilkhanate
summary: The Ilkhanate was the Mongol state that ruled Persia, Iraq, and the Caucasus directly, and held much of Anatolia as a tributary dependency through the vassal Seljuk sultans of Rum, from 1256 to 1335 — the southwestern quarter of the fractured Mongol Empire, founded by Hulagu Khan, whose armies destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate at Baghdad in 1258 and whose later kings converted to Islam and presided over a brilliant age of Persian art and learning.

title: The Immune System
summary: The immune system is the body's defense against infection: a layered network of barriers, fast general responses, and a slower, learning system that targets a specific invader and remembers it, which is why we catch some diseases only once. That memory is the basis of immunity and of vaccines, one of the greatest lifesavers in history. The same power can turn inward, attacking the body's own tissue in autoimmune disease or overreacting to harmless things in allergy. It is also what HIV destroys to such lethal effect. Understanding this defense is now one of medicine's great frontiers, underlying vaccines, transplantation, and a revolution in cancer treatment.
aliases: The product of an endless arms race
indexTerms: Flannery, Maura C. (ed.) and Cooper, Max D.; Alder, Matthew N.

title: The Inca Empire
summary: The Inca Empire (c. 1438–1533) was the largest state in the pre-Columbian Americas — and it functioned without money, markets, or writing. Its road network, labor-redistribution system, and quipu record-keeping show a different answer to the problem of organizing a large territorial state, and understanding that answer reframes what political and economic coordination actually requires.
aliases: The empire from local community life
What the Spanish found and kept

title: The Indian Independence Movement
summary: The decades-long campaign to end British rule over the Indian subcontinent, which drew on mass nonviolent resistance, constitutional agitation, armed revolt, and the organizational genius of the Indian National Congress to produce independence in August 1947 — accompanied by the Partition of British India into two states, India and Pakistan, and the largest forced migration in human history, in which approximately one to two million people were killed and fifteen million displaced.

title: The Indian Ocean Slave Trade
summary: The long-running system that carried enslaved Africans and others across the western Indian Ocean to Arabia, Persia, South Asia, and the islands — older and longer-lasting than the Atlantic trade, different in its uses, and far less remembered.
aliases: The African Face of the Trade

title: The Indus Valley Civilization
summary: The Bronze Age civilization that flourished in the Indus River basin from roughly 3300 to 1300 BCE, encompassing at its peak over a million square kilometers and cities including Mohenjo-daro and Harappa — the largest urban civilization of the ancient world, whose script remains undeciphered and whose collapse remains one of archaeology’s great unsolved questions.
aliases: The Vedic Civilization and Its Origins
The Harappan Heritage of South India
The Foundation of Pakistan’s Civilizational Past
indexTerms: Farmer, Steve; Sproat, Richard; Witzel, Michael

title: The Industrial Revolution
summary: The transition from an organic economy powered by plants, animals, and human muscle to a mineral economy powered by coal — which produced modern urbanism, the working class, industrial warfare, the end of Malthusian population limits, and the carbon emissions now reshaping the planet's climate
indexTerms: Peter H. Lindert and Jeffrey G. Williamson

title: The Influenza Pandemic of 1918
summary: The most lethal pandemic in recorded history, killing an estimated 50–100 million people worldwide — more than the First World War — in roughly two years between 1918 and 1920, disproportionately striking young adults rather than the elderly, spreading along the military transportation networks of the First World War, and being suppressed from public awareness by wartime censorship in most belligerent countries, producing a collective amnesia about its scale that lasted for most of the 20th century.
indexTerms: Taubenberger, Jeffery K. and Morens, David M.

title: The Informal Economy
summary: The vast world of market activity that happens outside the state's routine tax, licensing, and labor-protection systems — street vendors, cash work, unlicensed workshops, day laborers — which by ILO estimates accounts for the majority of employment globally, challenging the idea that formality is the norm and informality the exception.
indexTerms: Portes, Alejandro; Castells, Manuel; Benton, Lauren A.

title: The Inquisition
summary: Not one institution but several: the medieval Inquisition that began around 1184 against the Cathars; the Spanish Inquisition founded in 1478 and controlled by the crown; the Roman Inquisition of 1542. All used secret accusations, torture, and the threat of burning to police what people believed. The Spanish tribunal's reputation as a machine of mass slaughter became a byword in Protestant Europe. Its own records, when historians examined them, told a different story: somewhere between 3,000 and 5,000 executions across 350 years — a real engine of fear, but far smaller than the legend.
aliases: The View from the Converso Quarter
The View from the Black Legend

title: The Internet
summary: The global network of interconnected computers — originating from the US military’s ARPANET in 1969 and expanding into a worldwide civilian infrastructure from the 1990s onward — that has reorganized communication, commerce, knowledge, politics, and social life more profoundly than any technology since the printing press.

title: The Interstate Highway System
summary: The Interstate Highway System is the national network of limited-access expressways that the United States began building in 1956 under the Federal-Aid Highway Act. Now more than 46,000 miles long and paid for largely by federal fuel taxes, it standardized long-distance road travel, remade American cities and suburbs, and bound the country to a car-and-truck economy.

title: The Interwar Period
summary: The twenty years between the two World Wars — a period of democratic experimentation, fascist and communist revolution, economic catastrophe, and the failure of the international order created at Versailles — that ended with the most destructive war in human history.
indexTerms: Cato (Foot, Michael; Owen, Frank; Howard, Peter)

title: The Invention of the Printing Press
summary: The technology that made information cheap to reproduce — and institutional monopoly on knowledge permanently impossible

title: The Iranian Constitutional Revolution
summary: A movement between 1905 and 1911 that forced Iran's Qajar shah to grant a written constitution and an elected parliament, the Majles, drawing together merchants, reformist clergy, and Western-influenced intellectuals. It was among the first such constitutional movements in the Middle East and Asia. Royal counter-attack, foreign intervention, and a Russian ultimatum crushed the parliament by 1911, but it left a durable language of law and representation, and a still-contested debate about the role of clergy in Iranian politics that reached forward to 1979.
aliases: The Birth of the Nation Against Tyranny and Empire
A Contest Within Shia Political Thought
Iran as a Field of the Anglo-Russian Rivalry

title: The Iranian Revolution
summary: The 1979 revolution that overthrew Mohammad Reza Shah and replaced a secular monarchy with an Islamic Republic under Ayatollah Khomeini — the first modern revolution to install religious governance, the event that redefined political Islam globally, and the origin point of the conflict architecture that has shaped the Middle East ever since.

title: The Islamic Golden Age
summary: The period of extraordinary intellectual, scientific, and cultural achievement in the Islamic world from the Abbasid caliphate through the 13th century — during which Baghdad was the world's intellectual capital, scholars preserved and extended Greek learning, and Muslim scientists made foundational contributions to mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and optics that were essential to the European Renaissance.

title: The Israeli-Palestinian Conflict
summary: The Israeli-Palestinian conflict is the long, unresolved struggle between two national movements, Israeli Jews and Palestinian Arabs, over the same land between the Jordan River and the Mediterranean Sea. It turns on rival claims to territory, statehood, security, and the fate of Palestinian refugees, and it has run from the late nineteenth century into the present without a settled peace.
aliases: A Refuge and a Homeland Reborn

title: The Italian City-States
summary: The rich, fiercely independent cities of late-medieval and Renaissance Italy — above all Florence, Venice, Genoa, and Milan — whose banking, trade, and civic rivalry funded the explosion of art and ideas we call the Renaissance, taught a Florentine clerk named Machiavelli to write about power as it truly works, and whose fatal disunity left Italy easy prey for foreign invasion after 1494.
aliases: The view from outside the peninsula

title: The Janissaries
summary: The elite standing infantry of the Ottoman Empire, raised through the devshirme, a periodic levy of Christian boys taken from the Balkans, converted to Islam, and held as slaves of the sultan. They were among the first professional standing armies of the modern era, were early masters of gunpowder, and rose to make and unmake sultans before their violent abolition in 1826.

title: The Japanese Tea Ceremony
summary: One of the most demanding rituals ever built around a cup of tea — the Japanese art of chanoyu, in which the simple act of whisking and serving powdered green tea was raised into a complete spiritual and aesthetic discipline. Rooted in powdered-tea practices brought from China by Zen monks and later shaped in Japan by masters such as Sen no Rikyu, it became a meditation on simplicity, attention, and the beauty of imperfect, impermanent things, in which every gesture and silence is choreographed so that host and guest might share one unrepeatable moment.
indexTerms: Sasaki Sanmi (trans. Shaun McCabe and Iwasaki Satoko)

title: The Jesuits
summary: The Catholic religious order founded in 1540 as a disciplined force for the Counter-Reformation, which became the Church's most influential educators, missionaries, and intellectuals — feared and admired across the world, suppressed by their own Pope in 1773, and restored in 1814.
aliases: Soldiers of Christ and Servants of Learning
Protectors Within a System of Conquest

title: The Jewish Diaspora
summary: The dispersal of Jewish communities across the world following successive exiles and expulsions — and the remarkable development of institutions, texts, and practices that allowed a people to survive, maintain identity, and produce civilization-shaping intellectual and cultural achievements without a homeland for nearly two thousand years.
indexTerms: Rabbinic academies of Babylonia and the Land of Israel

title: The Joint-Stock Company
summary: The device that let strangers pool capital into a single enterprise by buying tradeable shares — spreading risk and raising sums no individual or partnership could. It built the great chartered trading companies, created the first secondary markets in shares, produced the first speculative bubbles, and bound the pooling of money to empire and colonial violence: ownership turned into a liquid, tradeable claim, and the form became the engine of both modern capitalism and armed corporate rule.

title: The Jury
summary: The institution of deciding legal questions by a body of ordinary citizens — one of the oldest mechanisms for involving non-specialists in governance, a site of persistent tension between democratic participation and expert judgment, and the institution that forces most directly the question of whether truth is best determined by the community it affects or by those trained to find it.
indexTerms: Hastie, Reid; Penrod, Steven D.; Pennington, Nancy
Supreme Court of the United States

title: The Justinianic Plague
summary: The first pandemic of bubonic plague, which struck the Byzantine Empire in 541 CE and recurred across the Mediterranean and Near East for two centuries — killing millions, disrupting Justinian's attempt to reunify the Roman Empire, and establishing plague as the defining catastrophe of the ancient world's final era.

title: The Kabbalah
summary: The major tradition of Jewish mysticism, devoted to the hidden inner life of God. From medieval Iberia and Provence it produced the Zohar and a map of divine emanation called the sefirot, then was transformed at Safed by Isaac Luria into a cosmic drama of contraction, shattering, and repair. It shaped later Judaism deeply, flowered into Hasidism, and survives today both as living tradition and as a stripped commercial product sold far from any synagogue.

title: The Kama Sutra
summary: A classical Sanskrit treatise on kama — the cultured pursuit of pleasure and the art of living well — attributed to Vatsyayana Mallanaga and composed around the third century CE. Organized in seven books, it covers desire, courtship, marriage, the refined life of the city gentleman, the world of courtesans, and the emotional lives of women and lovers; the famous sexual positions occupy only one part of one book. It reached the West largely through a loose Victorian translation by Richard Burton and F. F. Arbuthnot.
indexTerms: Vatsyayana, trans. Wendy Doniger and Sudhir Kakar

title: The Kamakura Shogunate
summary: The Kamakura Shogunate (1185–1333 CE) was the first warrior government of Japan — the moment a military regime of samurai, based in a coastal town in the east, rose beside the Kyoto court and took much practical power. It set the dual structure of sacred emperor and ruling general that would govern Japan for nearly seven centuries, and it twice threw back the Yuan-led invasion fleets of Kublai Khan.
aliases: The aristocracy that endured beside the warriors
Japan as the island that would not submit
Salvation made available to farmers and fighters

title: The Kazakh Khanate
summary: The Kazakh Khanate was a nomadic pastoral confederation on the Central Asian steppe, traditionally dated to a breakaway around 1465, when sultans descended from Genghis Khan led their followers east. Their people, organized into three tribal divisions called jüz, became the society from whom the modern Kazakh nation takes its name. It was never a tightly unified state, and Russia abolished the office of khan in stages — the Middle Jüz in 1822, the Junior Jüz in 1824. Kenesary Kasymov's revolt of 1837–1847 was a last bid to restore it. Russian absorption ended the khans' rule but not the Kazakh identity.

title: The Kebra Nagast
summary: The national epic of Ethiopia, written in Ge'ez and usually dated in its surviving form to the early fourteenth century. It tells how the Queen of Sheba visited King Solomon, bore him a son, Menelik I, who carried the Ark of the Covenant from Jerusalem to Aksum, moving the covenant's sacred center to Ethiopia. The text became the charter of the Solomonic dynasty, which ruled until 1974, anchored the Ethiopian Orthodox claim to hold the original Ark, and later shaped the Rastafari movement. Its world effect runs through dynasty, church, and crown across seven centuries, independently of any single author.
aliases: Sacred History of a Chosen Nation
A Charter Dressed as a Chronicle

title: The Khazars
summary: The Khazars were a Turkic people of the western Eurasian steppe who built a wealthy, multiethnic trading khaganate north of the Caucasus, between the Black and Caspian seas, from roughly the seventh to the tenth century. Sitting at a crossroads of the Byzantine, Islamic, and steppe worlds, they are best remembered because their ruling house and at least part of the elite adopted Judaism, probably in stages, and because they held back Arab expansion into eastern Europe, before the Rus' destroyed their capital around 965.
indexTerms: Golden, Peter B.; Ben-Shammai, Haggai; Róna-Tas, András (eds.)

title: The Khmer Empire
summary: The mainland Southeast Asian state that, for roughly six centuries, organized labor, land, and belief around a sacred kingship — building from its capital at Angkor an order that reached across much of what is now Cambodia, Thailand, and Laos before fading in the fifteenth century.

title: The Khmer Rouge
summary: The radical Communist movement that ruled Cambodia, renamed Democratic Kampuchea, from 1975 to 1979 under Pol Pot and the secretive party known as Angkar. On taking power it emptied the cities at gunpoint, declared a new beginning it called Year Zero, abolished money, schools, religion, and private property, and drove the whole population into rural forced labor. Roughly 1.5 to 2 million Cambodians — about a quarter of the country — died from execution, starvation, overwork, and disease before a Vietnamese invasion ended the regime in 1979.

title: The Khwarazmian Empire
summary: The Khwarazmian Empire was the Central Asian power built by the Turkic Anushtegin dynasty, which rose from provincial governors of Khwarazm — the oasis on the lower Amu Darya, south of the Aral Sea — to rule Persia and much of Central Asia in the 12th and early 13th centuries, before Genghis Khan's Mongol invasion of 1219–1221 destroyed it almost completely.

title: The Kīngitanga
summary: The Kīngitanga, or Māori King Movement, is a political and cultural institution founded in 1858 to unite the independent tribes of Aotearoa New Zealand under a single monarch and to halt the loss of Māori land to British settlers. Centred on the Tainui people of the Waikato, it crowned Pōtatau Te Wherowhero as its first king, survived a colonial invasion and mass land confiscation in the 1860s, and endures today as a living movement based at Tūrangawaewae marae, where Ngā wai hono i te pō was crowned as its eighth monarch in 2024.
aliases: A Rival Crown That Had to Fall
One of Many Unions Against the Settler

title: The Knights Templar
summary: The most famous of the medieval military-monastic orders, founded around 1119 to protect Christian pilgrims to Jerusalem. Its members were warrior-monks: knights who took monastic vows yet fought as shock troops in the Crusades. The order grew into an international financial network that held deposits and transferred funds, and became immensely wealthy. King Philip IV of France, deeply indebted to the order and eager to break an independent power within his realm, used heresy charges and confessions extracted under torture to force its destruction in 1307, and the Pope dissolved it in 1312.

title: The Korean War
summary: The war fought on the Korean peninsula from 1950 to 1953 — between North Korea (backed by China and the Soviet Union) and South Korea (backed by a US-led UN coalition) — which ended in armistice rather than peace, left the peninsula divided at roughly the same line where it began, and established the template for Cold War limited war that America would follow in Vietnam.
aliases: The War to Resist America and Aid Korea
indexTerms: Hanley, Charles J.; Choe, Sang-Hun; Mendoza, Martha

title: The Kushan Empire
summary: The crossroads empire of Central Asia and northern India — built by nomads turned kings, enriched by the Silk Road trade between Han China and Rome, and remembered above all as the patron that helped carry Buddhism north and east across Asia.
aliases: The Great Yuezhi in the West
indexTerms: Vesta Sarkhosh Curtis and Elizabeth Errington (eds.)

title: The Laboratory
summary: The room built to ask nature questions — a space set apart, equipped and controlled, where conditions are fixed, measured, and repeated so that the same procedure yields the same answer for anyone who performs it. Less a building than a method given a place, the laboratory turned curiosity into the most powerful knowledge-making engine ever devised, building medicine and materials while also building poison gas and the bomb.
aliases: The Laboratory as Engine of Reliable Knowledge
The Laboratory as Instrument of Power
indexTerms: Oreskes, Naomi and Conway, Erik M.

title: The Latin American Wars of Independence
summary: The roughly two decades of war, beginning around 1808, in which Spain's vast American empire shattered into more than a dozen republics, won through many regional campaigns, above all those associated with Simón Bolívar, José de San Martín, Antonio José de Sucre, and Mexico’s own insurgent and conservative leaders — a liberation that won independence from Spain yet largely preserved the old hierarchies beneath it, leaving the indigenous, the enslaved, and the poor still waiting for a freedom the American-born creole elite had claimed mainly for themselves.
aliases: A Liberation That Passed Them By

title: The League of Nations
summary: The first permanent international organization built to keep world peace, founded in 1920 after the First World War on the principle of collective security. Headquartered in Geneva and eventually some sixty members strong, it pioneered cooperation on health, labor, and refugees while proving too weak to stop the aggressors of the 1930s. The United States never joined, the League had no army, and it collapsed into a second world war — yet it became the blueprint for the United Nations.
aliases: The First Draft of World Order
The Mandate System and the Color of Trusteeship
indexTerms: Covenant of the League of Nations

title: The Library
summary: The institution for collecting, organizing, and preserving knowledge — from the ancient world's great repositories at Alexandria, Nineveh, and Pergamon through the Islamic House of Wisdom and the European monastery libraries to the modern public library and the digital archive. The library is the foundational technology of civilizational memory: it determines what survives, who can access it, and therefore what future generations can know about the past.
aliases: The Library as Contested Public Good

title: The Lighthouse
summary: A tower that burns a light to guide ships and warn them away from danger — one of civilization's oldest pieces of shared infrastructure, perfected through some of the boldest engineering ever attempted, and now fading into symbolism as satellite navigation takes over the work
aliases: A Light That Asks Nothing Back
A Structure That Became an Argument
Routine at the Edge of the World

title: The Little Ice Age
summary: The prolonged cooling that shaped European and global history for five centuries — and what a 0.5°C temperature drop can do to a civilization
indexTerms: Michael E. Mann, Raymond S. Bradley, Malcolm K. Hughes

title: The Loom
summary: The frame that turned thread into cloth and, in the form of the Jacquard's punched cards, turned a pattern into a program — making the loom not just the engine of humanity's first industry but a direct ancestor of the computer.

title: The Louisiana Purchase
summary: The 1803 deal in which the United States bought roughly 828,000 square miles of the continent's heart from Napoleon for about fifteen million dollars, doubling the nation at a stroke — a bargain made possible only because the formerly enslaved people of Saint-Domingue had destroyed Napoleon's army, and one that opened a century of westward expansion across lands home to dozens of Native nations who had no part in the sale.
aliases: The Land That Was Not France's to Sell
The Continent the Enslaved Won for Others
The Bargain and Its Constitutional Anxiety

title: The Maasai
summary: The semi-nomadic cattle herders of the savanna grasslands of southern Kenya and northern Tanzania — a Nilotic, Maa-speaking people whose age-set system and famous warrior grade, the moran, organize a society historically centered on cattle pastoralism, and who have spent a century fighting the loss of their grazing land to colonial treaties, ranches, and the wildlife parks that turned their pastures into tourist attractions.
aliases: A People of Cattle and Open Land
Empty Wilderness to Be Improved and Protected
The Timeless Warrior on the Plains
indexTerms: Tepilit Ole Saitoti and Carol Beckwith

title: The Mahabharata
summary: The Sanskrit epic attributed to the sage Vyasa, composed over roughly eight centuries and reaching its present form around 400 CE. At roughly 1.8 million words, it is the longest poem in any language. The Mahabharata tells the story of the Kurukshetra War between the Pandava and Kaurava cousins and contains within it the Bhagavad Gita, extensive philosophical and legal treatises, and a cosmological framework that has organized Hindu thought, law, and practice across South and Southeast Asia for two millennia.
indexTerms: Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute (V. S. Sukthankar et al., eds.)

title: The Malacca Sultanate
summary: The Malacca Sultanate (c. 1400–1511) was a Malay Muslim port-kingdom on the strait that bears its name, the chokepoint between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea. It grew into one of the greatest trading emporia of maritime Southeast Asia, became a springboard for the spread of Islam across the archipelago, and fell to Portuguese guns in 1511. The entry's purpose is to explain how a small harbor became the meeting-point of the monsoon trade systems, and what it left behind for the Malay world.
aliases: The seat of a golden age
A tributary of the dragon throne
indexTerms: Pires, Tome (Armando Cortesao, ed. and trans.)
Andaya, Barbara Watson, and Andaya, Leonard Y.
Barbara Watson Andaya and Leonard Y. Andaya

title: The Malay Annals
summary: The Malay Annals (Sejarah Melayu), also titled Sulalatus Salatin or 'the genealogy of kings,' is a court chronicle of the Malacca Sultanate and the central classical work of Malay literature. Compiled from older records and tales and repeatedly redacted, its best-known form is a 1612 Johor recension traditionally associated with the minister Tun Sri Lanang. Its genealogies, tracing the sultans back to Alexander the Great, and its founding tale of Singapura are court ideology as much as history, though it also preserves real memory of Malacca's rise and its 1511 fall to the Portuguese. It survives in around thirty differing manuscripts, today shared heritage across Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and Brunei.
indexTerms: Cheah Boon Kheng (ed.) and Abdul Rahman Haji Ismail
John Leyden (trans.) with Sir Stamford Raffles
Leonard Y. Andaya and Barbara Watson Andaya
Barbara Watson Andaya and Leonard Y. Andaya

title: The Malay World
summary: Maritime cultural sphere spanning tens of thousands of islands across present-day Indonesia, Malaysia, Brunei, Singapore, and beyond — held together not by any single empire but by a shared trade language, the reversing monsoon winds, and commerce. Port-polities rather than land empires shaped its political life, and Islam became the defining faith of many Malay courts over time. Its most durable legacy is the Malay language, modernized into the national tongues of both Indonesia and Malaysia and spoken by hundreds of millions today.
aliases: The World That Faced the Sea
indexTerms: Andaya, Barbara Watson; Andaya, Leonard Y.
Barbara Watson Andaya and Leonard Y. Andaya

title: The Mali Empire
summary: The Mali Empire (c. 1235–1600) was one of the great medieval West African empires to control the trans-Saharan gold trade at scale — founded by Sundiata Keita after the Battle of Kirina, reaching its height under Mansa Musa's extraordinary reign, and cementing Timbuktu and Djenné as centers of Islamic learning before giving way to the Songhai Empire that succeeded it.
aliases: The empire from the trade routes
The empire through an outsider's Islamic eyes
The founding story as living memory
indexTerms: Ibn Battuta (trans. N.J. Dawood / C. Defrémery and B.R. Sanguinetti)

title: The Mamluk Sultanate
summary: The sultanate that ruled Egypt and Syria from 1250 to 1517 CE — governed by mamluks, military slaves bought as boys, converted to Islam, and trained as elite cavalry, who seized power from the Ayyubid successors of Saladin and ruled as a self-perpetuating slave-soldier aristocracy in which authority passed among the trained military elite rather than by birth. The Mamluks halted the Mongol advance at Ain Jalut, destroyed the last Crusader states, hosted a shadow Abbasid caliph in Cairo, profited from and taxed the Red Sea and Egyptian route of the spice trade, and fell to the Ottomans under Selim I.

title: The Manhattan Project
summary: The secret American-led scientific and industrial program that built the first nuclear weapons during the Second World War — mobilizing over 130,000 people, spending two billion dollars, and producing the bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945, opening the nuclear age and permanently changing the relationship between science, the state, and mass destruction.
aliases: The Original Sin of the Nuclear Age
indexTerms: Franck, James et al. (Committee on Political and Social Problems)

title: The Māori
summary: The Māori are the indigenous Polynesian people of Aotearoa (New Zealand), descended from voyagers who reached the islands in ocean-going canoes around 1300 — the last major landmass humans settled. They built a society of iwi and hapū bound by mana, kinship, ritual, art, and warfare, signed with the British Crown the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840 whose Māori and English texts still do not agree, lost most of their land in war and law, and then drove one of the great cultural revivals of the modern world.
aliases: The treaty from the side that signed the Māori text
The treaty and the wars from the Crown's side
The long view, from near-disappearance to revival

title: The Maratha Confederacy
summary: The Marathi-speaking power of the western Deccan that, rising under Shivaji against the Deccan sultanates and the Mughals, broke Mughal dominance in the eighteenth century and spread across much of India — only to lose, divided among rival houses, to the British East India Company by 1818.
aliases: The mountain rats who would not be crushed

title: The Market
summary: The institution through which goods, services, labor, and capital are allocated by decentralized exchange between buyers and sellers, governed by price signals rather than central direction. Markets are among the oldest and most consequential human institutions: they emerged independently across civilizations, connected distant producers and consumers, generated the price information on which complex economies depend, and became the primary mechanism of capitalist organization — while remaining objects of profound disagreement about their scope, effects, and the conditions under which their outcomes are legitimate.
aliases: Markets as Built Things, Not Natural Ones
The Market as a Threat to the Natural World

title: The Marquesas Islands
summary: The Marquesas Islands are a remote archipelago of steep volcanic islands in the eastern Pacific, part of French Polynesia. Among the earliest-settled islands of East Polynesia and a likely springboard for the peopling of Hawaii and Rapa Nui, they developed one of the Pacific's richest cultures before European contact triggered a catastrophic collapse of their population. Annexed by France in 1842 and reduced by disease to a fraction of their former numbers, the islands are today the site of a determined Polynesian cultural revival.
aliases: The People Who Refused to Vanish
The Edge of the Great Migration

title: The Mataram Sultanate
summary: The Mataram Sultanate was the dominant Islamic kingdom of central Java, conventionally dated from 1587 to 1755. Chronicles place its emergence across the 1580s; the entry follows the usual marker of 1587, when the warlord Senapati broke from his overlord at Pajang and made Kotagede his capital. A rice-based inland power, it briefly unified almost the whole island under Sultan Agung, then slowly lost its independence to the Dutch East India Company. It ended not in conquest but in the 1755 Treaty of Giyanti, which partitioned the unified sultanate into the royal courts of Yogyakarta and Surakarta, whose dynastic lines continue today.

title: The Mau Mau Uprising
summary: An armed rebellion, centered on Kenya's Kikuyu people, against British colonial rule and the land dispossession at its heart — and the counterinsurgency that crushed it, with its mass detention camps, forced 'villagization,' and collective punishment, which has become one of the most documented and damning cases of violence in the late British Empire, and a story whose telling was for decades suppressed through destroyed and hidden colonial records.
aliases: The Emergency and its long silence
The land and freedom that did not arrive

title: The Māui Cycle
summary: The Māui Cycle is the shared body of traditional stories, told across the islands of Polynesia, about Māui, a clever demigod and trickster hero. In tellings from Hawai'i and Aotearoa (New Zealand) to Tahiti, Tonga, and Samoa, he fishes islands up from the sea, slows the sun to lengthen the day, and brings fire to humankind. The stories were carried for centuries by memory and performance rather than writing, so they differ from island to island, and most of what we can now read was recorded only in the nineteenth century.

title: The Maurya Empire
summary: The first state to bring most of the Indian subcontinent under a single rule — a centralized, bureaucratic empire whose greatest emperor, Ashoka, conquered a region called Kalinga, recorded his own remorse on rock and pillar, and helped turn Buddhism into a world religion.
aliases: The Empire of the Righteous King
The Empire Seen from the West

title: The Maya Calendar
summary: Not one calendar but a set of interlocking cycles — a 260-day ritual round, a 365-day solar year, and a linear day-count reaching back to a mythical creation — built on a base-20 place-value system and an independently developed sign for zero. It let Maya scribes fix any day in deep time, schedule the rituals of kingship, and write some of the most sophisticated astronomical tables of the pre-Columbian Americas.
aliases: Time as the Architecture of Kingship

title: The Maya Collapse
summary: The abandonment of the great southern-lowland Maya cities — Tikal, Copán, Palenque, Calakmul, and dozens more — across roughly the eighth and ninth centuries CE, when their divine kings stopped raising monuments, their courts fell silent, and their urban populations fell sharply. The causes are debated and almost certainly multiple: severe repeated drought, chronic warfare between rival dynasties, population pressure, intensive farming, deforestation, and soil stress, and the breakdown of the divine-kingship system. It was one of history's most dramatic societal collapses, but the popular story of a vanished people is wrong: northern cities like Chichén Itzá rose afterward, and millions of Maya live today.
aliases: A Cascade, Not a Single Cause
indexTerms: McAnany, Patricia A. and Yoffee, Norman (eds.)
Michael D. Coe and Stephen Houston
Arthur A. Demarest, Prudence M. Rice, and Don S. Rice (eds.)

title: The Meiji Restoration
summary: The transformation of Japan between 1868 and 1912 from a feudal society closed to the outside world into an industrialized nation-state with a constitution, a modern military, and imperial ambitions — the most rapid and comprehensive modernization in history, achieved on Japan's own terms.

title: The Mexican Revolution
summary: The decade-long upheaval that began as a call for honest elections and exploded into a many-sided civil war of peasant armies and warlords — killing perhaps a million people, toppling the dictator Porfirio Díaz, betraying or murdering nearly every leader it raised up, and producing a landmark constitution before settling into a new one-party order that claimed the revolution's name.
aliases: A Great Rebellion, Not a Revolution

title: The Mexican-American War
summary: The war of 1846 to 1848 in which the United States, provoked by its own government, defeated Mexico and seized roughly half of its territory — the present-day American Southwest and California. It carried the dream of continental expansion from sea to sea, made a conquered minority of the Mexicans who suddenly lived under American rule, and handed the United States a vast new land whose fate, slave or free, would help tear the nation apart in civil war fifteen years later.

title: The Mfecane
summary: Across southern Africa in roughly the 1810s to 1830s, wars, mass migrations, and political upheaval produced new, militarized states — the Zulu, the Ndebele under Mzilikazi, the Sotho under Moshoeshoe, the Swazi — and broke apart established communities. That much is agreed. The name, and the explanation, are fiercely contested: from the 1980s, Julian Cobbing and other revisionists argued that the older account, which blamed Zulu expansion under Shaka, served colonial purposes by obscuring the role of European and Griqua slave raiding and by portraying land as emptied by African violence. Historians now see a tangle of internal and external causes.
aliases: The upheaval as a chain reaction from Zulu power
The story as an alibi for colonial violence
The Mfecane as a claim about land and belonging

title: The Microscope
summary: The instrument that magnifies the very small — using shaped lenses, and later beams of electrons, to make visible what the unaided eye cannot reach. Robert Hooke's Micrographia (1665) named the 'cell'; Antonie van Leeuwenhoek saw living microorganisms in the 1670s; and centuries later the microscope helped make germ theory provable by letting scientists see the microbes that cause disease, founded cell biology, and — once light's wavelength was bypassed by the electron microscope — reached viruses, molecules, and atoms.
aliases: When the Image Becomes an Inference

title: The Millet System
summary: The arrangement by which the Ottoman Empire governed its non-Muslim subjects as self-administering religious communities — chiefly Greek Orthodox, Armenian, and Jewish — each headed by its own clergy and answerable to the sultan for taxes, law, and order within the group. Neither modern toleration nor simple oppression, it was a hierarchical accommodation that granted communal autonomy and protection of worship while keeping non-Muslims legally subordinate. The tidy three-millet scheme often described as ancient was largely a nineteenth-century construction, and the system broke apart as nationalism turned religious communities into rival nations.
aliases: The Order That Bred Its Own Undoing

title: The Mine
summary: The mine is the worked space where humans extract valuable minerals from the earth. It is one of civilization's oldest and most demanding systems, supplying the metals, fuels, and stones that made every material age possible — from bronze to iron to coal to the lithium of modern batteries. The mine has also long depended on some of the most dangerous and most coerced labor in economic history, and it has left lasting marks on the land, the water, and the communities built around it.
aliases: The Mine as Wound in the Land
indexTerms: Sachs, Jeffrey D. and Warner, Andrew M.

title: The Ming Dynasty
summary: The Ming Dynasty (1368–1644) sent the largest fleet in the world to the far edges of the Indian Ocean — and then cancelled the voyages, burned the ships, and turned inward. Understanding the costs and benefits of that choice, and what it reveals about how states decide to narrow their external commitments, is the entry’s central question.
aliases: The Zheng He visits from the receiving end
The officials who cancelled the voyages
indexTerms: Mote, Frederick W. and Twitchett, Denis, eds.
Frederick W. Mote and Denis Twitchett (eds.)

title: The Mint
summary: The state workshop that strikes coin — the institution through which rulers across three millennia have controlled the money supply, extracted revenue through seigniorage, waged economic warfare through debasement, and bound the authority to make money to the authority to govern.
indexTerms: Sargent, Thomas J. and Velde, François R.

title: The Mississippian Culture
summary: The network of maize-farming, mound-building societies that flourished across the river valleys of the southeastern and midwestern United States from roughly 800 to 1600 CE. Not a single empire but a shared world of ranked chiefdoms, monumental earthworks, and common religious imagery, the Mississippian culture produced the most populous and politically complex Native societies north of Mexico. Its largest center was the city of Cahokia, near modern St. Louis. Many Mississippian centers rose and declined over centuries before Europeans arrived, and the peoples did not vanish: their descendants include the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Chickasaw, and other Native nations of the American Southeast.
aliases: A Culture Read From the Ground
The Mounds That Did Not Fit the Story
indexTerms: Jerald T. Milanich and Charles Hudson
Richard F. Townsend and Robert V. Sharp

title: The Mita
summary: One word for two opposite things. Under the Inca, the mit'a was a rotating labor obligation — reciprocal, because the state fed and supplied the workers — that built roads, terraces, and storehouses across the Andes. The Spanish kept the name and rotational form but stripped the reciprocity, converting it into a forced-labor draft conscripting indigenous men by the thousands for the silver mines of Potosí and mercury mines of Huancavelica, where poisoning and exhaustion killed at rates no one recorded. Men fled villages to escape it: colonizers seizing an indigenous institution and inverting its meaning.
aliases: An Old Form for a New Hunger
The Turn That Became a Sentence

title: The Moche
summary: The Moche (Mochica) flourished on the desert north coast of Peru from roughly the first to the eighth century CE, a thousand years before the Inca. They left no writing, yet recorded their world in some of the most lifelike pottery produced in the ancient Americas. They irrigated a barren coast into abundance, raised mountainous pyramids of mud-brick, worked gold with rare mastery, and sacrificed captured warriors to their gods. Whether they were one state or many related polities, and why they declined, are still debated.
indexTerms: Walter Alva and Christopher B. Donnan

title: The Mona Lisa
summary: Small oil portrait by Leonardo da Vinci, begun around 1503 in Florence, showing a woman — most likely Lisa del Giocondo — before a hazy landscape with an ambiguous smile. Built in Leonardo's sfumato technique of gradated tone without hard edges, it was always admired. Its transformation into the world's most famous painting came largely from an accident: its theft from the Louvre in 1911 made it a global media sensation, and mass reproduction did the rest. It now hangs behind glass in the Louvre, the most visited and most reproduced artwork in history.
aliases: The Solution to Painting a Living Face

title: The Mongol Conquests
summary: The military campaigns launched from the Mongolian steppe by Genghis Khan and his successors between 1206 and the mid-fourteenth century, which created the largest contiguous land empire in history, killed tens of millions of people, connected Eurasia into a single trading system, and restructured the political order from China to Eastern Europe.

title: The Mongol Empire
summary: The largest contiguous land empire in history — a Eurasian empire founded by Genghis Khan in 1206 that within seventy years stretched from the Pacific to the Danube, destroyed some of the most sophisticated cities in the world, enabled unprecedented trade and exchange across the continent, and carried the plague that became the Black Death.
aliases: The Yuan Dynasty and Its Ambivalences

title: The Monroe Doctrine
summary: The principle, first stated by President James Monroe in an 1823 message to Congress, that the Americas were closed to further European colonization and that Europe must not extend its political system into the hemisphere. Militarily a bluff when issued — enforced in practice by British sea power — it hardened as the United States grew strong into a claim of hemispheric dominance, extended by the 1904 Roosevelt Corollary and read across Latin America less as a shield than as the charter of a new master.

title: The Monsoon
summary: The seasonal reversal of winds over the Indian Ocean and southern Asia that divides the year into a dry half and a wet one — the rains on which South Asian farming, Indian Ocean trade, and East African life have depended for millennia. Because its arrival and its failure decide harvests and livelihoods, the monsoon is a prime case of a natural rhythm that becomes the scaffolding of whole societies.
aliases: The monsoon as system to be captured

title: The Monument
summary: The deliberate raising of something huge and lasting to fix a memory, honor a power, or stake a claim — humanity's oldest way of forcing the future to remember. From the carved megaliths of Göbekli Tepe and Stonehenge to triumphal arches, war memorials, and colossal statues of kings, a monument is architecture built to endure and to mean. Because it fixes one version of the past in public space, it is also one of the most contested things a society can build, which is why revolutions topple statues and modern cities still fight over whose memory the stones are allowed to keep.

title: The Moon Landing
summary: The landing of Apollo 11 on the Moon on July 20, 1969, when Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the lunar surface while Michael Collins orbited above — watched or heard by perhaps a fifth of humanity, the climax of the Cold War space race, the farthest people have ever traveled, and a frontier the species reached and then stepped back from for half a century.
indexTerms: Murray, Charles, and Catherine Bly Cox

title: The Mughal Empire
summary: The Mughal Empire (1526–1857) built one of the most administratively sophisticated empires in early-modern history and created a political synthesis between Islamic governance and the Hindu civilization it ruled. How it managed that synthesis at its height, and why the synthesis broke down, is the entry’s central question.
aliases: The Mughals as commercial partner and prey
From devotional movement to military people

title: The Museum
summary: The public institution that gathers, preserves, studies, and displays the objects a society deems worth saving — opening the treasures of the world to everyone, even as its greatest collections hold much that was taken by force. The museum is the temple where a civilization keeps what it values, and argues over what it stole: a genuine public good built substantially on imperial plunder, and the place where the question of who owns the human record is fought out.

title: The Nanjing Massacre
summary: After Nanjing fell on 13 December 1937, Japanese Imperial Army troops spent six weeks killing, raping, and burning. Surrendered soldiers were executed along the Yangtze; tens of thousands of women were raped; the city was looted. That the atrocity occurred is established beyond doubt by foreign witnesses, survivor testimony, and Japanese soldiers' own diaries. What remains contested is scale: scholarly estimates range from tens of thousands to the 300,000 on China's national memorial. Japanese nationalist denial is not a dispute about evidence but a political stance. The unresolved reckoning keeps the massacre a live wound between two nations.
aliases: The Wound at the Heart of the Nation
A Society Divided Over Its Own Past
The Outsiders Who Saw and Wrote

title: The Nanyang Chinese Diaspora
summary: The Nanyang Chinese diaspora is the long process by which people from the coastal provinces of southern China migrated into Southeast Asia, the region Chinese called the Nanyang, or 'Southern Ocean.' Both halves of that label bear flagging: 'Nanyang' is a Sinocentric term, and 'diaspora' a contested one, kept here for familiarity even as the migrants became many distinct communities rather than a single people. A thin merchant traffic in earlier centuries swelled into a mass migration of millions in the 1800s and early 1900s, which slowly turned sojourners into settlers and, in time, into citizens of new Southeast Asian nations.
aliases: The sojourn that became a home
The visible face of an unfair economy

title: The Napoleonic Code
summary: The civil law code promulgated in France in 1804, drafted at Napoleon's direction to replace the country's tangle of feudal, regional, and Roman law with one clear written system. It gave ordinary men equality before the law, secure property, and freedom of contract, and it spread across much of the world as the model of modern civil law — even as it made married women legal dependents of their husbands and served a state that had restored slavery in the French colonies in 1802.
aliases: Equality at Home, Chains Across the Sea

title: The Nara Period
summary: The Nara period was the era of Japanese history that ran from 710 to 794 CE, named for the country's first lasting capital, at Nara. Modeling itself on Tang-dynasty China, it was the age when Japan set out to build a centralized state ruled by written law, compiled its earliest histories and poetry, and made Buddhism an instrument of the throne.

title: The Nazca Lines
summary: Scratched into a hyperarid Peruvian plateau between roughly 500 BCE and 500 CE, the Nazca Lines include hundreds of feet of straight geometric trenches and giant animal figures — a hummingbird, a monkey, a spider — visible whole only from the air. The Nazca made them by scraping dark surface stones to expose pale earth below, a simple technique that experiments have reproduced. The alien-runway theory is rejected outright. What scholars still debate is purpose: water and fertility ritual leads the field, with ceremonial pathways walked in procession a close second. Climate and development now threaten the lines' survival.
aliases: A Ritual Landscape of Water and Walking
indexTerms: Helaine Silverman and Donald A. Proulx
Markus Reindel and Günther A. Wagner (eds.)

title: The Neo-Babylonian Empire
summary: The empire ruled from Babylon between roughly 626 and 539 BCE, often called the Chaldean Empire after the dynasty that founded it. Nabopolassar broke free of Assyrian rule and, allied with the Medes, helped destroy Nineveh in 612 BCE; his son Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 605–562 BCE) made Babylon the monumental capital of the Near East, rebuilding it with the Ishtar Gate and a great ziggurat. The empire sacked Jerusalem in 587/586 BCE and deported much of its population, the event known as the Babylonian Captivity. It fell to Cyrus the Great's Persians in 539 BCE under its last king, Nabonidus.
aliases: The Restorer of the Ancient Order
The Empire That Deserved to Fall

title: The New Deal
